title: Texas landowners seek pause in $2B, 765-kV transmission line case over notice concerns
snippet: “A filing before the PUCT tied to Electric Reliability Council of Texas’ Permian Basin reliability planning alleges utilities including Oncor Electric Delivery Co. and the Lower Colorado River Authority failed to notify affected landowners after changes to the project’s route were made…Opposition has since intensified after state Rep. Brad Buckley, R-Salado, and 42 other Texas lawmakers filed an amicus brief supporting the American Stewards of Liberty, a property rights advocacy group. The legislative coalition is urging the PUCT to postpone routing decisions until a broader review of the 765-kV buildout is completed, arguing it could cost ratepayers billions and disproportionately affect rural property owners. Landowners are seeking to delay or pause the project until the January 2027 Texas legislative session so lawmakers can review the scope, costs and land impacts of the project. Evidentiary hearings took place June 8-12. A final PUCT decision is expected in September. ”

title: Controversial 2,000-acre solar farm in Stockton may avoid new rules
snippet: Here’s why “A controversial Stockton solar farm project would likely receive grandfathered status if residents vote next week to adopt zoning regulations, according to Baldwin County officials. That’s because developers submitted a completed application in advance of the zoning referendum. With only six days remaining before the special zoning referendum in Stockton, Jessica Waters, a spokesperson for Baldwin County, confirmed that a proposed $350 million, 2,000-acre solar farm will be exempt from zoning laws that could restrict its development, should the referendum pass.”

title: County continues to study ways to add teeth to solar, wind farm contracts
snippet: “The action came amid more discussion on what authority the county has to regulate wind and solar projects and to limit data centers in the county. State law now requires county ordinances to follow state building standards, preventing a county from being more strict that state regulations…Following a public hearing, the amended county wind ordinance was approved, aligning it with state regulations. The board postponed action on the special use permit for two solar projects…The owners had canceled and then resubmitted their applications to be sure they met county regulations after the county amended its solar ordinance last month.”

title: Zoning amendment for proposed Wexford solar farm enters formal review stage
snippet: “A proposed solar farm in Wexford Township is moving one step closer to consideration. The Wexford Joint Planning Commission has officially begun reviewing a zoning ordinance amendment tied to the project and is asking for more public input. The proposed shipyard solar project would span property in both Wexford and Grand Traverse counties. During Monday’s meeting, the commission voted to begin the formal review process and scheduled a public hearing after dozens of locals raised concerns.”

title: Illinois put community solar on a 150-year-old coal mine
snippet: “An old coal mine in Illinois is now generating community solar power for hundreds of households and businesses. Nexamp and TurningPoint Energy have completed two community solar projects on a reclaimed coal mine site in Woodford County, Illinois. Together, the solar farms can generate 9.8 megawatts of power and use nearly 17,000 solar panels spread across roughly 40 acres. The electricity flows directly to ComEd’s grid, and Nexamp says all the solar modules were manufactured in the US. ”

title: Persica urges judge to grant permit for solar farm near Porter – Oklahoma Energy Today

title: Is New England’s new hydropower transmission line paying off?
snippet: “When the New England Clean Energy Connect transmission line started carrying electricity from Canada into Maine in January, supporters hailed the project as a triumph for renewable power. Now, after nearly six months of operations, the early numbers raise questions about whether the project will be able to advance the region’s energy transition as much as advertised. Energy flow into New England is up just marginally, and there have been roughly 27 days when no power at all traveled along the new line, commonly called NECEC. If current trends hold, New England will receive less hydropower this year over two transmission lines than it did over just one line in 2023 and previous years.”

title: Dominion-NextEra merger raises questions for Virginia energy customers
snippet: “A proposed merger between Dominion Energy and NextEra would create one of the largest utilities in the country, and some state lawmakers and regulators are already raising questions about what the deal could mean for Virginia customers.The company expects to file its formal merger application in the third quarter. After that, the State Corporation Commission, or SCC, will have up to 180 days to decide whether to approve the deal. One of the biggest questions raised during the hearing was whether the SCC has enough time to fully review a merger of this size.”

title: Michigan utility Consumers Energy wins nod for 1.3 GW of solar PPAs
snippet: “Michigan energy provider Consumers Energy has secured regulatory approval to sign power off-take deals that will add 1,269 MW of new solar photovoltaic (PV) capacity to the Michigan electric grid. Specifically, Consumers Energy will buy the entire output of a 150-MW solar park in Illinois’ Clark County under a deal with Moonshine Solar Park LLC, while another contract with Hectate Energy will make it the power off-taker of the 499-MW Sunfish solar complex in Calhoun County, Michigan. The utility will also sign PPAs with Acceleration Solar LLC, Portside Solar LLC, Hawkeye Solar LLC and Hawkeye Solar II LLC that will enable it buy the entire output of the 90-MW Acceleration, 80-MW Portside, 200-MW Hawkeye and 250-MW Hawkeye Solar II projects in Iowa. ”

title: Big battery farms encounter resistance across western Washington
snippet: “Community opposition to big battery farms is spreading across western Washington just as the technology takes on growing importance for the state’s clean energy transition and to stabilize the electric grid. A multiplying number of cities and counties have enacted moratoriums on permitting for new utility-scale battery storage systems over the past year. Over a similar timeframe, project developers withdrew around a dozen early-stage battery storage proposals from the interconnection queue of Puget Sound Energy, Washington’s largest utility.”

title: Cypress Creek secures US$3.5 billion to fund 1.63GW/1.9GWh solar-plus-storage project
snippet: “US independent power producer (IPP) Cypress Creek Energy has secured US$3.5 billion in financing to support the development of a 1.63GW/1.9GWh solar-plus-storage project in Arkansas. The financing will support the first two phases of the three-phase Steel River Energy Center project, which comprises 2.45GW of solar PV and 2.9GWh of battery storage, making it one of the largest solar-plus-storage projects in the US. The entire project is expected to be completed by 2029.”

title: After nearly two decades, this massive New Mexico wind project is now powering California
snippet: “The largest wind energy project in U.S. history is now online, delivering power from a massive array in New Mexico to Arizona and California — and signaling a new era for sending clean electricity across the West. Nearly two decades in the making, the estimated $11-billion SunZia project from Pattern Energy is now fully operational, company officials said Thursday. It’s made up of 916 turbines that can produce up to 3.65 gigawatts of electricity, making it potentially more powerful than the Hoover Dam. ”

title: Solar generations surpasses natural gas in California after panel installs surge
snippet: “Utility-scale solar generation in California exceeded power from gas during 82% of the days this year through May, according to a Tuesday report from the US Energy Information Association. While solar has supplied more power to the state’s grid for short periods in the past, this marks the first year when average generation in the first five months has outpaced the fossil fuel that’s the biggest source of US electricity.”

title: Ellery nears moratorium extensions for wind, solar, BESS
snippet: The Ellery Town Board is poised to extend its moratorium on wind, solar, and battery energy storage system projects for up to one year. Public hearings were held June 9. The moratoriums will last six months after passage and can be extended twice for three months each. The town is drafting a local law for wind energy and considering one for BESS. For solar, the board will require that components be recycled as part of decommissioning. Solar projects in sensitive areas near Chautauqua Lake, schools, Long Point State Park, the village of Bemus Point, and wildlife management areas will face higher standards and a 2,500-foot setback from non-participating property lines. Once a special use permit is granted, developers will have six months to obtain a building permit and one year to complete the project. Town attorney Neil Robinson reminded the board that the local law “can’t be so restrictive that it prevents development of solar farms.”

title: NextEra to pay $150M to settle charges related to Florida political misconduct allegations
snippet: NextEra Energy agreed to pay $150 million to settle a shareholder lawsuit in federal court in Florida. The case involved allegations of political misconduct, including funding “ghost” candidates to defeat state lawmakers and spying on a reporter, in the context of NextEra’s proposed $67 billion merger with Dominion Energy. The settlement is covered by insurance and is the largest securities class action settlement in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida since 1995.

title: Community continues to voice concerns on expansive solar projects in Dubois County
snippet: Dubois County residents are raising ongoing concerns about large solar projects, including fire safety, road access, lack of independent county oversight, and inadequate financial protections. A moratorium remains in place on the second phase of the EDP Renewables Duff Solar Project while the county finalizes land-use rules.

title: Unfounded health concerns in Michigan are powering a solar backlash
snippet: St. Clair County adopted a novel public health regulation last year that set limits on solar development and battery storage, encouraged by the county’s medical director who warned of noise, visual pollution, and potential contamination. County Commissioner (now Chair) Steven Simasko accepted the medical opinion “as a good standard for the protection of the public health of our citizens.” The policy included a $25,000 nonrefundable fee and violations punishable by up to six months in prison. In February, a circuit court judge ruled the health regulation “invalid, null, and void,” but county officials unanimously voted to appeal.

title: Plymouth County hits pause on data centers with 1-year moratorium
snippet: Plymouth County Supervisors approved a one-year “revocable moratorium” on the construction of data centers and industrial battery storage facilities. The vote was 4-0. The moratorium aims to give the county time to prepare rules on construction, water and power usage, and noise issues. Supervisor Chairman Don Kass said, “It’s become more of a local issue. I myself had citizens come up to me and express their concern regarding these kind of facilities. So, we’ve decided we really need to be proactive and get ahead of this issue.”

title: Marathon County to hold listening session on proposed wind farm
snippet: Marathon County hosted a listening session on June 17 at the Lake View Conference Center at 6 p.m. to gather resident feedback on the proposed Hub City Wind Farm by Alliant Energy (28–38 turbines, ~150 MW). The project could power 45,000 homes and bring over $22 million in revenue. The county’s advisory committee is collecting input to inform negotiations with the developer. Committee chair John Robinson said, “Our (job) is not to pass judgment on the merits of the project, but rather collect the information” and “We’re trying to be inclusive and get as much input as we can.”

title: Big battery farms encounter resistance across western Washington
snippet: Community opposition to utility-scale battery energy storage systems has grown across western Washington, leading multiple counties and cities (including King, Skagit, Pierce, Kitsap, and Thurston counties, plus Snoqualmie, Carnation, North Bend, Puyallup, and Duvall) to enact or extend six-month to one-year moratoriums on new BESS permits. Concerns center on lithium-ion battery fire risks, toxic smoke, noise from cooling fans, and impacts on neighborhoods, schools, and farmland. Anacortes resident Elke Siller Macartney said, “There’s a dawning awareness of the awful effects of BESS installations such as thermal runaway fires and toxic smoke, and the horrible noise from cooling fans… It’s the height of arrogance for these companies to pretend to install these things for the supposed good of all.”

title: State goals, local impacts
snippet: Inside the fight over where large-scale solar should go in NY: New York’s push for large-scale solar is creating conflicts in rural upstate counties as projects like Flat Creek Solar (300 MW on 3,000 acres in Montgomery County) and Mill Point Solar move forward under the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES). Residents and local officials in Montgomery and Washington counties are raising concerns about cumulative impacts on farmland, wildlife, drainage, property values, and the loss of local control.

title: States claim victory as Trump admin ends wind court fight
snippet: The Trump administration dropped its appeal of a court ruling that struck down its “wind directive” pausing new onshore and offshore wind projects. The 1st U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals granted the Interior Department’s request to dismiss the appeal. New York Attorney General Letitia James said the ruling protects projects that “will create jobs, strengthen our economy, and bring down New Yorkers’ electric bills.” Massachusetts Attorney General Andrea Joy Campbell said the court “correctly protected those critical investments from the Trump administration’s unlawful order.”

title: Zeeland Twp neighbors fight to stay in solar farm approval process
snippet: Neighbors in Zeeland and Jamestown townships are fighting to remain as intervenors in the Michigan Public Service Commission case for RWE Americas’ proposed 200-megawatt Silver Maple Solar Farm on 1,900 acres. RWE’s attorneys moved to dismiss some non-adjacent neighbors. Administrative Law Judge James Varchetti will rule on intervenor status June 25. title: Solar-friendly bill would again cut Kern’s property tax receipts
snippet: Senate Bill 1329 would change how large-scale solar projects are assessed for property taxes by valuing only tangible assets and excluding power purchase agreements, tax credits, and subsidies. The change would reduce tax revenue for counties like Kern. Kern County Board Chairman Phillip Peters said, “The state continues to pass costs, unfunded mandates and other financial obligations down to the county.” State Sen. Shannon Grove (R-Bakersfield) strongly opposed the bill, saying she is “tired of providing the energy and using our energy and our resources, and now you won’t even let us have the tax revenue.”

title: ‘Different this time’
snippet: Wind farms and transmission lines poised to come to northern Maine: Maine’s Public Utilities Commission is moving forward with a new RFP (issued December 2025, bids closing June 12, 2026) to select wind and solar projects plus transmission infrastructure in Aroostook County to deliver at least 1,200 MW to the New England grid. The effort is a regional collaboration with Connecticut, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Vermont to share costs and benefits. Philip Bartlett, PUC chairman, stated, “This RFP builds on prior stakeholder input and regional transmission planning to move renewable energy projects in northern Maine from concept to reality.” Jack Shapiro of the Natural Resources Council of Maine noted, “This time they started working with all the other states in advance… It’s basically the biggest opportunity that we have for low-cost renewable energy in the medium term.”

title: Landlord Sues Vineyard Offshore for Missed Rent Payments
snippet: BP Hancock filed suit in Suffolk County Superior Court claiming Vineyard Offshore (an affiliate of Vineyard Wind backer Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners) owes more than $1.2 million in unpaid rent, fees, and security deposit replenishment for 28,370 square feet of office space on the 18th floor at 200 Clarendon Street in Boston. title: Rockingham supervisors reject solar farm proposal
snippet: The Rockingham County Board of Supervisors voted 4-0 to deny a special use permit for a solar farm near Timberville on agricultural land roughly a quarter-mile from Plains Elementary School. Concerns included stormwater runoff, rocky terrain making construction difficult, conflict with the county’s agriculture-support goals, high municipal water use for cooling, and potential negative impacts on property values. Resident David Mewellski said, “They’re not going to be able to just pound poles in the ground… The property they’re planning on putting it on is on nothing but rock.”

title: Sedgwick County weighing second temporary ban on solar energy projects in 3 years
snippet: The Sedgwick County Commission is considering a six-month moratorium on new solar projects (with possible extension). Commissioners cited the need for more time after reviewing two recent applications, concerns over protest petitions, proximity to Clearwater’s urban growth area, and infrastructure issues (including Ark 95 and potential south connector roads). Commissioner Jeff Blubaugh stated, “We don’t want to rush this… I want to make sure that we’ve turned over every stone.” A decision is expected at the June 17 meeting.

title: Apache County P&Z recommends renewable energy moratorium
snippet: The Apache County Planning and Zoning Commission voted 6-2 on June 3 to recommend that the Board of Supervisors adopt a temporary moratorium on new renewable energy conditional use permit applications while the county rewrites its renewable energy ordinance. Public concerns raised include impacts on public safety, emergency communications, water sources, roads, cultural/scenic resources, noise, and proximity to homes. A separate Black Ridge wind project application was submitted around May 26, adding urgency. The recommendation now goes to the Board of Supervisors for further process including public hearing.

title: [Opinion] Solar lawyer
snippet: Are bans on solar really banned?: “HB711, which amended VA Code 15.2-2288.8 (“Special exceptions for solar voltaic projects”), has been called the “solar standards” bill. It has also been called a pyrrhic victory because, for all that it does, it does not limit counties’ ability to turn down solar projects…The core point is not complicated. HB 711 gives covered solar projects a required path to local review. Virginia counties still control the ultimate vote. They can still consider local impacts. They can still impose lawful conditions. They can still deny projects that do not satisfy the applicable standards.”

title: Cypress Creek scores USD 3.5bn in funds for Arkansas solar-BESS giant
snippet: “US renewable energy developer and operator Cypress Creek Renewables has secured USD 3.5 billion (EUR 3.03bn) in financing for part of its gigawatt-scale Steel River Energy Center, a huge solar-and-storage project in Arkansas…When all phases are completed, the Steel River Energy Center will comprise 2.45 GW of photovoltaic (PV) farms and 2.9 GWh of battery energy storage systems (BESS)...The solar-storage complex will be located in Mississippi County in the northeastern part of Arkansas. Phases 1 and 2 will be equipped with domestically-made panels by First Solar and will be operated under a virtual power purchase agreement (PPA) with an investment-grade corporate counterparty.”

title: ​​Clark County Plan Commission issues favorable recommendation on solar amendment
snippet: “The Clark County Plan Commission voted on Wednesday to recommend a change to the Unified Development Ordinance that would prohibit agricultural land from being used for solar energy facilities…In a 4-3 vote, the plan commission voted to recommend that the Clark County Commissioners amend the UDO to only permit solar energy systems to be built on property that is zoned light or heavy industry.”

title: Grundy County intends to appeal appellate court’s ruling on solar panels
snippet: “The Grundy County Board approved a new solar guideline Tuesday that requires solar developers to have concrete plans in place before receiving permits from the county…The Third District Appellate Court ruled against Grundy County on Tuesday, March 10, in the case of Equity Solar Illinois vs. County of Grundy. According to the court document, the dispute between Equity Solar and Grundy County arose when Grundy County rejected special use permit requests, and the plaintiffs filed a mandamus action, which was granted by the Grundy County Circuit Court…The county will require more intensive financial and legal protections through explicit fee formulas, higher insurance limits and irrevocable financial assurances for both decommissioning and reclamation…The circuit court ordered Grundy County to issue the permits, and the county appealed. It lost its appeal, but it plans to appeal further.”

title: Massive clean energy project caught in legal crosswinds at WA Supreme Court
snippet: “Opponents of the Horse Heaven wind project contend that, in looking to act expeditiously, the state Energy Facility Site Evaluation Council and former Democratic Gov. Jay Inslee failed to properly account for the project’s negative impacts on the environment, surrounding communities and tribal cultural resources…Tri-Cities CARES, Benton County and the Yakama Nation filed separate petitions seeking to overturn the final decision issued in October 2024. Justices consolidated the petitions and will hear arguments at 10 a.m. Thursday. At stake is the future of a massive wind, solar and battery storage project envisioned on roughly 72,500 acres along Interstate 82 south of the Tri-Cities. More broadly, if the court sides with the project’s foes, it will seriously crimp state efforts to reduce Washington’s reliance on power generated from fossil fuels…Opponents contend the state’s Administrative Procedures Act was not followed and that the environment and wildlife are inadequately protected. Residents say nothing was done to address interference with their views of the Horse Heaven hills…The Yakama Nation contends the final decision resulted in a project that would have significantly greater harm to wildlife and their cultural resources compared to the council’s initial recommendation. They also said there was inadequate consultation between the tribe and the council.”

title: NY power grid squeezed by aging plants, extreme weather, and data centers
snippet: NYISO report: “The New York Independent System Operator warned on Tuesday that extreme weather and new corporate users with high demands are shrinking electricity reserves, leaving the state’s grid close to a shortage on the hottest and coldest days of the year…Despite the warnings about electric grid reliability, New York achieved a renewable energy milestone on June 3. According to NYISO, the record for solar power generation was broken during the noon hour that day, with solar panels generating 5,661 megawatts—about 29% of the entire statewide demand. Everyday residential New Yorkers generated most of that through small, private rooftop systems called “behind-the-meter” solar. Those systems created 5,131 megawatts, while large commercial solar farms generated just 530 megawatts.”

title: Cherokee County planners advance comprehensive zoning plan
snippet: “After hearing from residents and Confluence, the firm hired to help create the plan, the Cherokee County Planning Commission unanimously approved a resolution recommending commissioners adopt the comprehensive plan for the county. This comes as Clearway Energy has proposed a solar energy project. Currently, a moratorium is in place prohibiting large-scale projects like solar and nuclear, from moving forward. The process of creating a comprehensive zoning plan began as an attempt to stop new energy projects. The plan will now go before the Cherokee County Commission at its next scheduled meeting.”

title: Waller County Judge Posts, Comal Commissioner Shares, Explanation Why Counties Can’t Stop Developers, Data Centers
snippet: “Waller County Judge Trey Duhon explained on Facebook yesterday the reasons why counties can’t regulate the explosive growth reshaping Texas communities like Canyon Lake and New Braunfels… ‘I recently received a DM on Facebook from a resident inquiring about starting a petition against a new development because they are concerned that it’s going to affect electricity, roads, and flooding. This raises a very good topic for discussion, especially in light of current hot-button topics such as data centers, battery-energy storage facilities, solar farms, etc…Counties can only regulate what the Texas legislature has given us the ability to regulate, which honestly is not much. ‘For example, we do not have ‘zoning authority.’ We can not arbitrarily dictate where certain types of developments can be built. We do not have noise-ordinance authority…We can not impose arbitrary rules on any developer without running the risk of exposing the county to liability….As I frequently tell people, if you want our commissioners court to have more authority in terms of regulating these types of things, then your time and energy is probably better spent on communicating with state representatives, state senators, and state officials to give counties more authority to regulate these types of things (like zoning authority, for example).’”

This raises a very good topic for discussion, especially in light of current hot-button topics such as data centers, battery-energy storage facilities, solar farms, etc…Counties can only regulate what the Texas legislature has given us the ability to regulate, which honestly is not much. ‘For example, we do not have ‘zoning authority.’ We can not arbitrarily dictate where certain types of developments can be built. We do not have noise-ordinance authority…We can not impose arbitrary rules on any developer without running the risk of exposing the county to liability….As I frequently tell people, if you want our commissioners court to have more authority in terms of regulating these types of things, then your time and energy is probably better spent on communicating with state representatives, state senators, and state officials to give counties more authority to regulate these types of things (like zoning authority, for example).’” [ MyCanyonLake.com , 06/10/2026, link to article here ].

title: Neighbors rally against battery energy facility in Oshtemo Township
snippet: “A rally against a potential battery energy storage system, or BESS, facility that could be built on rural land near homes and agriculture drew a group of more than 100 neighbors to Little Pistol Farms in Oshtemo Township Monday night…Residents formed a group called ‘Say No to BESS,’ urging their local leaders to oppose BESS development, because of a variety of environmental and health concerns related to the technology…The land up against Little Pistol Farms and seven other residential property lines, on Van Kal Avenue, has been eyed by an out of state company, NewEdge Renewable Power. NewEdge Renewable Power has not submitted a formal application, but has previously stated plans to submit an application for the site during the second half of 2026, which is around the same time as the township's moratorium is expected to expire…Oshtemo Township’s one-year moratorium on commercial battery energy storage development applications was approved in November of last year. According to the township attorney, it could be extended. The next Oshtemo Township Planning Commission meeting is scheduled for Thursday, June 25th at 6 pm.”

title: Rep. Wortz
snippet: Residents 'spoke loud and clear' against solar expansion: “State Rep. Jennifer Wortz is praising Fayette Township's decision to deny an expansion for a utility-scale solar project, which could still be appealed for override by state regulators. The Fayette Township Planning Commission voted last week to deny a special land use permit sought by Ranger Power for its proposed Heartwood Solar II and III projects…Wortz added that federal incentives are likely contributing to the continued push for solar projects across Michigan. ‘The federal Solar Tax credit is expiring on December 31, 2027,’ she said. ‘For commercial solar projects to receive this 30% tax credit the project must be fully installed and functioning by this date. I believe that is why there is such a push to get these projects through.’ If Ranger Power appeals, Wortz said she hopes state regulators defer to the township's decision.”

title: Allium Solar
snippet: County could lose local control over battery energy storage project: “In August 2025, Boston-based Longroad Energy filed an application to build a 945-acre solar and battery facility north of the Hollister airport…The project, called Allium Solar, would sit north of Hollister on agricultural land bordering Highway 156, across from the Amazon warehouse, and stretching up to Hwy 25. Both the property owner and Longroad describe the site as low-productivity farmland. It would generate 110 megawatts of solar power and store an equivalent capacity in batteries…The county’s current moratorium remains in effect until the ordinance is presented to the board, which was supposed to occur on June 9. The draft ordinance, County Counsel Gregory Priamos said, was expected to be posted June 4 or June 5. However, on June 5, the county released a June 9 board meeting agenda without a draft energy storage ordinance that includes a scheduled public hearing with a staff recommendation to adopt an urgency ordinance to extend the moratorium for more than 10 additional months while staff continues work on an energy storage ordinance.”

title: Rural Showdown
snippet: Fayette Township Slams Brakes On Massive Heartwood Solar Farm: “On Monday night, Fayette Township’s planning commission slammed the brakes on Ranger Power’s Heartwood II solar proposal, voting to deny a special land‑use permit for a 140‑megawatt array that would have stretched across roughly 1,350 acres. The decision capped months of heated public meetings where neighbors, local officials and Hillsdale College warned the project would threaten water resources, wetlands and the township’s agricultural character…Zoning consultants from Carlisle/Wortman formally recommended denial, citing deficient wetland protection and the inappropriate conversion of productive farmland. The commission’s motion to reject the permit echoed those findings and, as reported by WTVB, also pointed to gaps in the applicant’s emergency‑response planning, the planned removal of dozens of acres of trees and other impacts to the township’s rural landscape.”

title: Huron County will continue review of Pine Sap Solar despite moratorium
snippet: “Despite Huron County's new moratorium on solar and battery energy storage projects, Ranger Power's application for the 150-megawatt Pine Sap Solar project in Rubicon Township will continue through the review process…The decision was based on a recommendation from the county’s corporation counsel. The Huron County Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a three-month moratorium May 12, nearly a week after Ranger Power submitted its application…During Wednesday evening's planning commission meeting, Building and Zoning Director Jeff Smith said the review process should continue as normal…Outlining the path forward, Smith said the application will remain under review, with a public hearing possible later in the process.”

title: Trump administration blocks wind farm development in Illinois and across the nation
snippet: “Billions of dollars in wind farm projects all over Illinois are on hold, including two projects in McLean County, two in Peoria County, and one in Tazewell County…Until last August, getting these [FAA (Federal Aviation Administration) compliance] certificates was routine. The FAA automatically kicks applications to the Department of Defense to see if there is a risk to military readiness, said a source knowledgeable about the process…Mitigation cases stopped getting approved starting last August, said a background source. In late April, even the easy cases stopped receiving certificates. Now, no wind farm project in the nation that didn’t already have permit approval is moving forward, said multiple sources…The American Clean Power Association estimated not allowing the U.S. to construct new wind projects would cost the national electricity grid an extra $361 billion over the next 25 years because of higher prices based on demand.”

title: Commission advances battery storage ordinance
snippet: “Ward County commissioners forged ahead with an ordinance governing siting of battery energy storage systems on Tuesday, June 2, even though the industry’s proposed language tweaks remain to be dealt with…The commission debated sending the proposed ordinance back to the planning commission and considered imposing a moratorium on applications until the ordinance is completed. title: A proposed Willamette Valley solar park is back. Neighbors still oppose it
snippet: “Developers of the Muddy Creek Energy Park have now submitted their preliminary application to the Oregon Energy Facility Siting Council – a volunteer advisory board appointed by the governor that’s charged with reviewing and approving large energy projects…But a group of local residents has long argued against the project. They worry Muddy Creek could harm wetland habitats for waterfowl that surround the site and replace productive farmland with expansive solar panels if the agricultural part of the project falls through…In a preliminary application hundreds of pages long, Hanwha developers say the site is ideal due to its proximity to a PacificCorp substation and power lines, which will allow solar energy to reach the electrical grid. Hanwha also said roughly 732 acres of the 873-acre solar farm will continue to be farmed…Once the application is complete, the state Department of Energy will hold a public informational meeting.”

title: Wind energy critics rally at Capitol, warn of a ‘Wyoming Wind Wall’
snippet: “Rather than take aim at any one particular wind energy project, rally organizers called on decisionmakers to consider the bigger picture: the cumulative impact of converting mostly undeveloped, agricultural landscapes into semi-industrialized zones with wind towers that are regarded by many as a blight on Wyoming’s open spaces…Cheyenne area resident Wendy Volk, who organized the event, has implored state officials to consider individual wind energy projects as part of a larger industrial infrastructure that’s morphing into a corridor of towers, power lines and electrical substations from Medicine Bow to Cheyenne to Douglas.”

title: US court dismisses GE Vineyard appeal
snippet: A Massachusetts judge has rejected GE Vernova’s request to throw out a previous order requiring the turbine supplier to continue working on CIP-Iberdrola’s 806MW Vineyard Wind 1 array off the coast of Massachusetts…Justice Peter Krupp of the Massachusetts Superior Court ruled in a court order late last week that the activation of Vineyard Wind’s power purchase agreement with the state of Massachusetts and declaration of COD does not mean that GE Vernova can now walk away from the project…[Krupp] added: “The fact that VW declared a particular milestone under the PPAs does not mean that the (turbines) are functioning at capacity, as designed, or without substantial need for repair and improvement; or that (GE Vernova) does not have specialised expertise needed to bring about timely repair and improvement to VW’s operational capacity.”

title: Accomack County BZA upholds stop-work order on battery storage facility
snippet: “The Accomack County Board of Zoning Appeals affirmed an earlier stop-work order from the Zoning Administrator on the construction of a battery storage facility on the 32400 block of Chincoteague Road after public comment in opposition during a hearing Wednesday morning…Accomack County officials approved a conditional use permit to operate a battery storage system there, with a three-year expiration in July 2025. The Accomack County Zoning Administrator later approved a site plan and land disruption permit in March 2026, after the expiration date. They issued a stop-work order on April 1, which Scout LLC appealed…While Scout Storage, LLC (“Scout”) respects the Board of Zoning Appeals’ decision today, it is disappointed with the outcome. Scout intends to file an appeal in the coming days.”

title: Trustees gather for informational meeting on wind, solar power
snippet: “Township trustees from around the county gathered Monday evening for an informational meeting regarding solar and wind energy hosted by the Ashtabula County Board of Commissioners…The commissioners had asked the trustees to decide whether they want to allow wind or solar projects in a given township with no restrictions, allow them only in certain areas, or prohibit them outright…The commissioners asked the trustees to pass one of the three resolutions by Sept. 1, so the county can then start its process which includes a variety of deadlines and public meetings.”

title: Supreme Court of Ohio reverses permit for Oak Run Solar site
snippet: “The Supreme Court of Ohio has cut down a previously approved permit for Madison County’s Oak Run Solar project, which would have been the largest solar installation in the history of the state. The court’s majority claim that the project’s permit application did not meet the Ohio Power Siting Board’s (OPSB) submission requirements in full…In the court’s decision, the justices order the siting board to “more thoroughly address” some of the purely visual impacts of the project. However, it is crucial to note that this decision does not outright kill the Oak Run Solar project, and that the opportunity is still open to solar developers around Ohio and the surrounding area.”

title: Why North Carolina’s electric co-ops are turning to grid batteries

title: Skagit County attempts to plan for clean energy future
snippet: “As proposed energy facilities cause conflict throughout the state, Skagit County is working on more specific development regulations for the projects that will inevitably come. Earlier this year, Skagit County received a $770,000 grant from the state Department of Commerce, funded through the Climate Commitment Act, to develop local clean energy development regulations and prepare an environmental impact statement, which requires significant public outreach. Similar grants were awarded to Spokane, King and Kittitas counties and the City of Richland…In Skagit County especially, where restrictive zoning to protect local agriculture means many parcels are relatively affordable, energy developers have been making overtures in the area for several years. The county has already seen two large battery facility proposals: one west of Mount Vernon that went through the county permitting process, and another outside of Sedro-Woolley that is still working its way through the state siting process, which supersedes county control...The project is expected to wrap up by May 2027, with opportunities for public comment throughout the process.”

title: Major battery storage project proposed in San Jose’s Coyote Valley
snippet: “A Virginia energy company is proposing to build one of the largest battery storage plants in California in Coyote Valley, a rural expanse of farmland and scenic open space on San Jose’s southern edges… the facility would include thousands of lithium-ion batteries in 312 shipping containers, company officials say, spread across 40 acres…Under state law, AES still must complete an environmental impact report and hold public hearings. [Dave Cortese, D-San Jose] said opponents should sue if the project moves forward…Families at the Charter School of Morgan Hill, located along Monterey Road about 100 yards from the site, have collected more than 2,900 signatures on a petition opposing the project.”

title: Wawarsing mulls moratorium on data centers and battery storage facilities
snippet: “WAWARSING, N.Y. — Spurred by a letter from a company expressing interest in putting an AI data center on the banks of the West Branch Beer Kill, town officials will consider this week placing a six-month moratorium on both data centers and battery energy storage systems. Supervisor Joseph Stoeckeler said he didn’t consider the inquiry serious given the terrain of the land the firm indicated interest in is in a former town park on Old Greenfield Park Road…Stoeckeler said that he expects board members to enact the moratorium at its meeting on Thursday, June 4…Although the Wawarsing inquiry is the first known inquiry by a company looking to locate a data center in Ulster County, there are currently several proposals for battery energy storage systems in the county.”

title: Poarch Creek Indians weigh in on large solar projects near tribal lands in Alabama
snippet: “Alabama’s only federally recognized tribe has weighed in publicly for the first time on the industrial-scale solar development happening near its tribal and historic lands in south Alabama…In a press release this week, the Poarch Creek Indians Tribal Council said it was gathering information on three industrial-scale solar projects in north Baldwin and Escambia counties: the Silicon Ranch solar facility in Stockton, a planned solar farm in the Tensaw region of Baldwin County, and a potential solar project in Escambia County, adjacent to the tribe’s current property…The tribe said its historic preservation office reached out to the developers of all three projects to proactively address any issues that may be of cultural and historical significance.”

title: Union County refuses to budge on solar project
snippet: “Alliant Energy’s proposed amendment to their battery energy storage system likely reached a final roadblock as Union County officials refuse to budge on their standing moratorium on solar fields…The original permit allowed a 25 MW BESS storage system to be installed. Over the past few months, representatives from Alliant have requested a change in the permit to increase the storage system from 25 MW to 40. During a meeting on April 29, the county pushed back against the amendment. County supervisors approved a moratorium in 2025 which limited the construction of new solar fields in the county. They wished to see more information on taxation before proceeding with additional projects.”

title: Winners of the 2026 Legislative Session
snippet: “At the beginning of session, Missouri’s solar industry faced what many viewed as a serious threat amid discussions of a potential moratorium that could have hindered the state’s competitiveness in the growing solar market. By the end of session, however, those proposals had largely stalled out. Instead, the debate appeared to strengthen support for solar development in parts of rural Missouri, where many landowners framed the issue through the lens of private property rights. As session concluded, Missouri’s solar industry appeared to be in a far stronger position than many observers initially anticipated.”

title: West Michigan township weighs one-year moratorium on data centers, solar farms
snippet: “Allendale Township leaders are considering putting a one-year moratorium in place preventing the development of any data centers, battery storage facilities, large-scale solar farms and wind farms. The township’s board of trustees will vote on the proposed measure at its June 8 meeting.”

title: Richland Township residents pack third solar project hearing as decision still months away
snippet: “At least 100 Richland Township residents were at Gull Lake Middle School Wednesday evening for a third continuation of a public hearing about a proposed large-scale solar farm project that could reshape more than 2,200 acres of Kalamazoo County farmland. The Liberty Farms Solar Energy Center, proposed by Consumers Energy, would generate 220 megawatts of electricity and power roughly 52,000 homes. The project has been working through Richland Township’s special exception use and site plan approval process since Consumers filed its application earlier this year.”

title: Mount Vernon council extends moratorium on BESS facilities
snippet: “The Mount Vernon City Council voted Wednesday to extend the city’s moratorium on battery energy storage systems, or BESS, for six months while the city continues to develop permanent regulations. The moratorium restricts the city from accepting applications for the siting, developing and constructing of BESS facilities. The moratorium has been in place since May 2025. Mount Vernon Principal Planner Rebecca Lowell told the council Wednesday that the city placed a moratorium on BESS facilities because the city code didn’t have an allowance for them.”

title: Berkeley County Council lifts moratorium for solar farm project in 3-2 vote amid outcry
snippet: “Tense moments returned to the Berkeley County Council chambers as council members voted to lift a large-scale development moratorium, clearing a key hurdle for a proposed solar farm in the Cross community despite loud opposition from residents. The motion passed 3-2, with three council members recusing themselves from voting… Lifting the moratorium is a step forward for energy company RWE, which is seeking to build the Sandy Run Solar project, a 198-megawatt solar facility that would cover about 1,500 acres in the rural community.”

title: Lincoln County approves permits for major power line project
snippet: “Lincoln County commissioners voted to approve conditional use permits for Nebraska Public Power District’s R-Project, a transmission line stretching from Sutherland to Holt County. The permits cover five temporary construction yards, helicopter landing pads and smaller staging areas known as “fly yards.” The sites will be set up at multiple leased locations across the county. NPPD says the R-Project will improve reliability and reduce electrical congestion across its power system.”

title: Defying Trump, California continues to bet big on offshore wind
snippet: “While the Trump administration takes extraordinary measures to halt the development of offshore wind power in the United States, Southern California is advancing a $4.7-billion plan to deploy hundreds of towering wind turbines in waters off the state’s coast. The proposed Pier Wind project at the Port of Long Beach is a 400-acre terminal for the positioning, storage and assembly of some of the world’s largest offshore wind turbines, which would be towed north to federal wind lease areas some 20 miles off Morro and Humboldt bays… California’s approach is to push forward with offshore wind preparations that fall within its jurisdiction, readying the ports and the power grid to eventually take on electricity from 1,000 turbines in federal waters. The aim is to wait out the current administration, which is notoriously hostile toward a form of renewable energy that is booming elsewhere in the world.”

title: Planning commission hears recommendations to mitigate noise, vibration and shadow flicker coming from wind turbines
snippet: “The nearly three-hour public hearing was the second of seven scheduled meetings used to gather information and hear public testimony before the planning commission writes an ordinance regarding wind energy conversion systems. That ordinance will then be sent to the Henderson Fiscal Court, which can approve it, decline it or send it back to the planning commission for further work. Holding the meetings, gathering the information and then writing an ordinance comes to the planning commission as a directive from the fiscal court. The next WECS public hearing will be 6 p.m. Wednesday, July 15, in the Fiscal Court courtroom. The topic of that hearing will be environmental and ecological impacts.”

title: Solar project looks to set up in Cross. Residents worry risks outweigh benefits .
snippet: “A German energy company is looking to turn 1,500 acres of forest and farmland in northwestern Berkeley County into a large solar farm. Residents in the surrounding area, however, believe the costs of the project outweigh the benefits it could provide. RWE Solar Development has asked Berkeley County Council to lift a large-scale development moratorium in order to proceed with the zoning process for a planned solar farm, Sandy Run Solar. In a request submitted to County Council, the company says it will build solar arrays, or panels, across 1,500 acres as part of a 7,500-acre planned development.”

title: Kenosha County votes to pause battery facility, project may still move forward
snippet: “On Tuesday night — the Kenosha County Board of Supervisors voted to adopt a one-year moratorium on the approval of new battery energy storage systems in the county. County Executive Samantha Kerkman specifically requested the moratorium — citing mounting public health and safety concerns from residents. The resolution specifically cites potential health and safety issues including toxic gases, fire hazards, and impacts on air and water quality. But Robin Energy Storage is not backing down. The company sent a letter to the county board the night of the vote — saying they were not notified of the resolution until that same day — and asking the board to decline action. The board voted anyway. Director of Project Development Jan Porvaznik told TMJ4 News the company is evaluating other options — including going directly to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission — a state level body whose approval could potentially override the county moratorium entirely..”

snippet: “The Tupper Lake Town Board tabled any action in regards to a potential moratorium on battery storage facilities last week until its members have had more time to review arguments in both directions. title: Guilderland adopts BESS moratorium
snippet: “The Guilderland Town Board this week voted unanimously to place a short-term halt on anything having to do with the installation of commercial battery energy storage systems as it looks to draft regulations for governing large-scale energy storage facilities in town. The four-month moratorium adopted by the board on Tuesday allows for the installation of residential systems, and also comes with the almost certainty of needing to be extended by the time September rolls around.”

title: [Hillclimber Solar Withdraws Application]
snippet: “Hillclimber Solar, a RWE-backed utility solar and storage project, this week withdrew its application to the Ohio Power Siting Board. Days earlier, project opponents packed a key OPSB hearing in the county, which has a solar moratorium. RWE was able to avoid the temporary ban because its agreements with PJM happened before it was enacted. But OPSB has tended to frown on tense situations like these. Ohio used to be a fine place for a solar farm but no more, thanks to changes in state law and regulators sympathetic to local opposition. Solar developer Open Road Renewables pledged in March to never develop another project in the state, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see more join them.”

title: Madison County Board Does Not Grant Special Use Permit to Proposed Glen Carbon Solar Farm
snippet: “The Madison County Board did not vote on a motion to grant a special use permit to Trentino Solar, LLC, to develop a commercial solar energy facility in Glen Carbon. During the Board’s regular meeting on Wednesday, May 20, 2026, several representatives with Trentino Solar and a volunteer with the Illinois Solar Education Association spoke in favor of the project during the public comments section of the meeting [, Mayor Bob Marcus submitted a letter against the project]... When the Building and Zoning Committee reviewed this project, there was no motion or second, so the committee ultimately did not vote on whether to recommend the project’s approval or denial… The Board then was asked to vote on whether to approve or deny the special use permit to Trentino Solar. Alison Lamothe made a motion to vote. There was no second, so the motion failed.”

title: Kenosha County votes to pause battery storage facility, but the project may still move forward
snippet: “ On Tuesday night — the Kenosha County Board of Supervisors voted to adopt a one-year moratorium on the approval of new battery energy storage systems in the county. County Executive Samantha Kerkman specifically requested the moratorium — citing mounting public health and safety concerns from residents. The resolution specifically cites potential health and safety issues including toxic gases, fire hazards, and impacts on air and water quality. But Robin Energy Storage is not backing down. The company sent a letter to the county board the night of the vote — saying they were not notified of the resolution until that same day — and asking the board to decline action. The board voted anyway. Director of Project Development Jan Porvaznik told TMJ4 News the company is evaluating other options — including going directly to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission — a state level body whose approval could potentially override the county moratorium entirely.”

title: Supervisors again fail to schedule ordinance hearing
snippet: “Wapello County supervisors Darren Batterson and Bryan Ziegler once again were unable to schedule a public hearing for a renewable energy ordinance during Tuesday's meeting at the courthouse. It's the second time the pair has been unable to agree to set a hearing for an ordinance that was crafted through a one-sided mediation session in February between Batterson and attorney Eric Updegraff, with little input from Ziegler.”

title: [Opinion] Defense Department rides with Trump’s mendacious assault on wind energy “The Defense Department, undoubtedly at Trump’s insistence, is working to achieve through stonewalling what the administration could not through other shenanigans
snippet: to stop or significantly slow the development of wind energy in the U.S. In Texas, 54 such projects are among 165 nationwide in limbo as the Defense Department claims to still be reviewing them to ensure they don’t impair national security. But those reviews, triggered by proposed structures 200 feet and taller and which federal law requires to be completed within 60 days, have ground to a halt with no reasonable explanation. Not only has the department not cleared a wind project since August, in April it canceled all meetings with wind developers waiting for action, the American Clean Power Association reported. Jason Grumet, CEO of the ACP, told the Associated Press that the backlog is unprecedented, calling it a “de facto moratorium” on new land-based wind energy.”

title: Company Seeks Permit for $174 Million Battery Energy Storage Facility in Codington County
snippet: “A Florida-based company is seeking approval from South Dakota regulators to build a $174 million battery energy storage system... The project would be located on 53 acres within the existing Crowned Ridge wind energy project, 13 miles northeast of Watertown. The permit application to the Public Utilities Commission was filed this month… The Public Utilities Commission will hold a public input meeting about the project at 6 p.m. Central time on July 1 at the Waverly South Shore School gymnasium, at 319 Mary Place in Waverly. The company hopes to have the project in operation by May 2028.”

title: Crook County weighs moratorium on new solar farms – Central Oregon Daily
snippet: “Crook County leaders are considering putting a pause on new solar farms. Members of the public have raised concerns about the pace and scale of solar development. Key concerns are on the impact on agriculture and wildlife. The Crook County Board of Commissioners has asked staff to conduct research on the topic. A moratorium would pause applications for all new large-scale solar development. No formal decision has been made, but staff are in the process of coming up with recommendations.”

title: Avangrid signs power purchase agreement with Puget Sound Energy for wind facility

title: West Rockhill weighs battery storage project amid data center concerns
snippet: “As West Rockhill Township officials work to address concerns from the community about data centers moving into the area, a landowner is now seeking permission to develop a battery storage system on Branch Road. Energy Mountain LLC, also known as Heritage Meadow, is seeking a special zoning exception to permit an F1— Utility known as a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at 25 Branch Road and on the parcel directly across the street… The West Rockhill Township Zoning Hearing Board will conduct a public hearing on this request at the Tylersport Fire House at 125 Ridge Road in Sellersville on June 3 at 7 p.m.”

snippet: "Quite a quick change for local governments" as MI looks to boost battery energy storage “Michigan has ambitious clean energy laws on the books, while also facing a surging demand for power. What’s less known is that it also has an ambitious law for utility-scale battery energy storage, which experts say is needed to make the grid state’s grid work as it undergoes a major power shift. That means there are lots of battery projects in the pipeline across the state, and that local governments have to make some quick decisions about how to handle them — often without much guidance.”

title: Solar amendments approved pending further ordinance review
snippet: “The Knox County Commissioners on Tuesday approved amendments to an existing ordinance they say will better protect county residents from future solar development. But more changes may be on the horizon. “This is something that will give us more protection for right now,” Commission President T.J. Brink said. “We’ll be coming back to the table at a later date with additional things to add to this.” “This is simply the first step in that.” Brink called the newly amended county solar ordinance a “stopgap” measure intended to buy the county time to take a deeper look at the legislation — originally passed in 2020 to establish guidelines such as setbacks and screening requirements for solar development projects — to determine whether additional changes are needed.”

title: Commissioners push back vote on solar project near Clearwater
snippet: “Hours of public comment hold back a solar project proposal in Sedgwick County. The Branch Line Project, submitted by the energy system developer TED Renewables, is one of several projects being considered in Sedgwick County… After hearing from the public, the commission voted to push back a decision, but tasked staff with researching several things concerning the project. title: Pennington County weighs revised alternative energy ordinance as residents raise concerns
snippet: “A newly revised alternative energy proposal in Pennington County would separate small-scale solar and wind systems meant to power a home or farm from utility-scale projects designed to feed electricity to the grid. Under the proposal, homeowners could still install smaller systems, but with added limits, such as prohibiting solar panels or wind systems in residential front yards. For larger projects, the rules would add new requirements. Developers would need a Conditional Use Permit and would be required to meet a noise limit near homes, along with other standards tied to siting and operations.”

title: Ticonderoga residents raise concerns over potential lithium battery plant
snippet: Residents in Ticonderoga voiced strong opposition to a proposed lithium battery energy storage facility, citing environmental impacts, water usage for cooling, potential runoff into local water systems, and long-term cleanup responsibilities. Town leaders are considering a six-month moratorium to review local laws and scientific data on battery storage. Town Supervisor James O’Bryan stated, “Moratorium obviously gets a chance to look at not only our local laws, but also any other facts, any other sciences that revolve around battery energy storage.” Resident Carol Ramundo said, “My stance is definitely no,” and added concerns about water use: “These plants also use a tremendous amount of water to cool their batteries. So where is that going to go?” A public hearing will be held to gather input before a vote.

title: Chateaugay officials talk Wind Repowering Project
snippet: Town officials in Chateaugay (Franklin and Clinton counties) held a public hearing on the proposed repowering of the existing Chateaugay Wind Park from 106.5 MW to 116.5 MW by Valcour Chateaugay Newco LLC (AES subsidiary). The project involves replacing older turbines with larger, more efficient models on ~803 acres and is under review by the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES). The project is expected to power 21,009 homes and continue economic contributions (over $14 million since 2009). Town Supervisor Donald Bilow noted the ORES process has been challenging. Fire Chief Gerry Blow praised AES as “great neighbors.” Landowner Bruce Bennett expressed strong support, saying AES helped fund his children’s college education. Written comments were due May 15, 2026.

title: Solar power hearing in Champaign County draws large crowd
snippet: A public hearing for RWE Clean Energy’s proposed Hillclimber Solar & Storage project (116.5 MW solar + 40 MW battery storage on 628 acres in Urbana Township) drew about 40 speakers. Opponents raised concerns about farmland loss, wildlife, and community character. James Cropper stated, “Taking thousands of acres of farmland to companies… they’re going to be making money off our backs.” Supporters emphasized energy needs and landowner rights. The Ohio Power Siting Board staff recommended denial; an evidentiary hearing is scheduled for May 26.

title: Berkeley citizens speak out against large solar farm
snippet: Residents in the Cross community of Berkeley County strongly opposed RWE’s proposed 198-megawatt Sandy Run Solar project on 1,500 acres. The Berkeley County Council voted to lift the large-scale development moratorium in the area, with a final vote scheduled for May 26. Resident Robert Barrett said, “This is not a good thing for the community of Cross… There will be big and little promises made, but the bottom line is it will destroy 7,500 acres for wildlife for no reason.” Supervisor Johnny Cribb stated he would vote no if it came to a tie.

title: Huntingburg water rate increases and Crossvine Solar settlement approved
snippet: The Huntingburg Common Council and Utility Board approved a settlement with Crossvine Solar (in partnership with AES) after the developer missed the original construction window. The agreement includes increased setbacks, screening, neighbor protections, incident monitoring, regulation of battery energy storage system locations, and investment in emergency services. The council also rescinded the prior moratorium on commercial solar and directed the Planning Commission to develop new standards.

title: Rome Common Council to investigate battery energy storage systems
snippet: “The Rome Common Council started the ball rolling on a moratorium on battery energy storage systems in the city to learn more about them before any official proposals come in. The council proposed a 6-month moratorium at its Wednesday night meeting at City Hall, then tabled that proposal so that the legislation could go before the Rome Planning Board for a public hearing. The council has already met with the mayor’s office and the code enforcement department to discuss the issue.”

title: Large crowd turns out for Hillclimber Solar hearing in Champaign County
snippet: “The Ohio Power Siting Board held a public hearing on Wednesday evening on the proposed Hillclimber Solar Project, allowing residents, elected officials, and other interested parties to provide sworn testimony for or against the project as the state determines whether to approve construction. Wednesday’s hearing drew a large crowd of residents, local officials, landowners, and industry representatives, highlighting the continued divide over utility-scale solar development in Champaign County. Hillclimber Solar… is seeking approval to develop a 156.5-megawatt solar and battery storage facility in Urbana Township in Champaign County… Despite the application advancing to the hearing stage, Ohio Power Siting Board staff has recommended the project be denied… The staff recommendation is considered a significant development in the case, as OPSB staff reviews applications for compliance with state standards before making recommendations to the board.”

title: COMMISSIONERS START PROCESS FOR SALDO AMENDMENT FOR SOLAR, WIND FARMS
snippet: “Earlier today, the Indiana County Commissioners approved starting the process to add a solar and wind development amendment to the county’s Subdivision and Land Development Ordinance. Josh Krug with the office of planning and development said that the amendments will deal with large-scale commercial solar and wind farms. The amendment will soon be made available online, at libraries throughout the county, municipal offices and at the Planning and Development and Commissioners offices, and there will be a 45-day period for the public to examine it. There will also be a public hearing on the matter, but that has not been scheduled yet.”

title: Rockdale County extends ban on data centers
snippet: “The Rockdale County Board of Commissioners on Tuesday night extended a moratorium on data centers and battery energy storage systems. The move follows an initial 180-day moratorium enacted in March 2026 to evaluate the cumulative impacts of high-water-use facilities. The county is reviewing regulations regarding noise, water, and power usage while updating its Unified Development Ordinance… Rockdale County’s new moratorium is good through Sept. 8 as the county continues its review process to determine future development rules.”

title: Morgan board, residents talk data center regulations during meeting
snippet: “[C]ommissioners began a review of the county's solar, data and battery storage ordinances. The discussion came two weeks after the board approved a six-month moratorium on data centers to give it time to review and establish guidelines for any projects that may seek to come to the area. Board Chair Michael Wankel read a report indicating a ‘commercial land energy conversion system ordinance update application’ and a ‘commercial solar energy facility ordinance update application’ would be ready for the county by Tuesday. Language surrounding battery storage systems for commercial wind and solar has been rewritten, he said, noting a separate ordinance and application for energy storage systems will be written by Oct. 1.”

title: Clark County planning commission delays solar ordinance decision
snippet: “The Clark County board of zoning appeals denied a special use exception to build a solar energy facility on private farmland. Now, the planning commission is considering a change to a land ordinance. The proposal comes after the Clark County Board of Zoning Appeals denied a special use exception to build a solar energy facility on private farmland. The commission heard about two and a half hours of public comment on the proposed amendment, but took no action during the meeting. The amendment was tabled until June 10.”

title: Huntingburg Council approves Crossvine Solar Project settlement
snippet: “The Huntingburg City Council voted unanimously at the regular meeting held on Tuesday to approve a settlement agreement with Crossvine AES, despite strong opposition from residents concerned about safety risks and property values. The decision came after the city’s special counsel, Joshua Claybourn, warned that Huntingburg’s moratorium on solar development sits ‘on thin ice’ legally and that litigation against the multi-billion dollar corporation would likely fail while costing the city hundreds of thousands of dollars. The settlement includes several concessions from AES, including a six-foot berm or cmomparable vegetation screening along neighboring properties, increased setbacks of 100 feet from property lines and 250 feet from residences, and incident monitoring equipment for the battery energy storage systems.”

title: Snoqualmie City Council Appoints New Member, Advances BESS Moratorium, Names Police Chief Finalist
snippet: “At the meeting, Mayor Mayhew introduced a moratorium ordinance on battery energy storage facilities within Snoqualmie city limits. He noted that a Snoqualmie moratorium would have no effect on the proposed Jupiter Power project, which is located in unincorporated King County, but said it was appropriate for the city to move forward with its own legislative process regarding whether such facilities should be permitted within city limits. He also said the continued focus on whether Snoqualmie should adopt a moratorium had become a distraction from the city’s work in the King County process…The council went into a 20-minute executive session with legal counsel to discuss the ordinance before voting to waive the standard 72-hour notification requirement, allowing the ordinance to be read in for the first time… The council voted 5-1, with Councilmember Washington the lone dissenting vote, preferring to act on the ordinance that night rather than refer it to committee.”

title: Attorney General James and Governor Hochul intervene to protect Sunrise Wind project
snippet: New York Attorney General Letitia James and Governor Kathy Hochul moved to intervene in a federal lawsuit seeking to vacate approvals for the 924-megawatt Sunrise Wind offshore wind project, which is expected to power 600,000 New York homes. The project is set to begin delivering power this year and become fully operational next year, providing more than $875 million in economic benefits and supporting over 3,500 jobs. Attorney General James stated, “Contrary to the president’s claims, wind energy is essential for our economy, our environment, and our electric grid… This administration has already tried to shut down Sunrise Wind once, and we will not leave the future of this critical project in its hands.” Governor Hochul added, “Offshore wind projects like Sunrise Wind will not only provide needed clean energy that will help our environment, but also create good-paying union jobs, benefit our local economies, and help secure our energy independence.”

title: Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban
snippet: Voters in Richland County, Ohio, upheld a ban on utility-scale solar and wind in 11 townships (53%–47%) on May 5, 2026, but exit polling suggests the result would have flipped to 54% favoring repeal if ballot wording had been clearer. The confusing language made a “yes” vote mean keeping the ban and a “no” vote mean repealing it. Campaign leader Morgan Carroll said, “The question of who gets to decide what happens on private land in Richland County did not end tonight.”

title: Berkeley County panel advances plan to lift Cross moratorium for 1,500-acre solar farm
snippet: The Berkeley County Land Use Committee advanced a plan to lift a large-scale development moratorium covering nearly 7,500 acres in the Cross community to allow rezoning for RWE Clean Energy’s 198-megawatt Sandy Run Solar project on 1,500 acres. The full council will consider the proposal in two weeks. Resident Robert Barrett said, “Cross is not an industrial community. It’s a retirement and recreational community… We don’t want that big industry in there.” Developer Peter Stalter noted the site is at least a mile from the nearest public right-of-way.

title: Shunning the sunshine? NC order for Duke Energy to hit brakes on solar raises policy, legal questions
snippet: The North Carolina Utilities Commission (via Chairman Bill Brawley) ordered Duke Energy to pause its 2026 solar procurement (~770 MW) until the 2026 Carbon Plan is approved by December 31. Clean energy groups called the unilateral order “arbitrary and capricious” and warned it threatens reliability and affordability amid rising demand.

title: Workers left in limbo after permit delays stalls wind energy project
snippet: A major wind energy project in Knox County, Illinois, has come to a halt due to federal permitting delays involving the FAA and Department of Defense. Construction crews have begun dismantling a mobile concrete batch plant and lay-down yard. Labor leaders report that about 25 workers from the laborers union and another dozen from other trades were laid off immediately. Dane Simpson, Renewable Energy Director for Great Plains LECET, stated, “They were two to three weeks into the project, and they really felt like the rug got pulled out from under them.” The project was expected to create around 300 construction jobs and generate roughly $48 million in tax revenue for Knox County over its 35-year life cycle. NextEra Energy Resources has not commented on when construction might resume.

title: No vote yet on large-scale Southern Michigan solar project after months of opposition
snippet: The Fayette Township Planning Commission postponed a decision on Ranger Power’s Heartwood Solar II project (~1,000 acres east of Jonesville). Resident Steve Oleszkowicz stated, “One out of every four acres of productive, agricultural land in this township gone, converted, industrialized.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss noted, “Load is growing in Michigan and solar is currently the most affordable way to meet that demand.”

title: Budget and batteries
snippet: Tupper town and school boards poised for big meetings Monday night “The Tupper Lake Town Board is slated to discuss a moratorium on lithium ion battery energy storage systems… Supervisor Rickey Dattola said the town doesn’t yet have a formal document and isn’t expected to vote on the matter on Monday. Rather, it’s still in the discussion stage and is a chance for the town board to hear any feelings from the public on the matter, as well as how each of its five members feel before possibly drafting a resolution for vote at a future meeting… Dattola added that the town board had previously been approached by a company who floated a battery storage facility at its old landfill, but that never advanced beyond informal discussions and is not an active pursuit.”

title: Clifton Park weighs short ban on solar-project applications
snippet: “The Clifton Park Town Board is considering a six-month moratorium on solar-energy-system applications as developers continue to seek to cover more acreage with the technology. The moratorium would specifically prohibit Tier 2 and Tier 3 solar energy system applications for 180 days. The town classifies Tier 2 and Tier 3 systems as medium- to large-scale ground-mounted installations, including solar facilities that involve substantial land area, electrical interconnection infrastructure and long-term land conversion.”

title: [Louisa County] ]Supes allocate $250k to fight VL transmission line
snippet: “The Louisa County Board of Supervisors on Monday night stepped up its efforts to oppose a controversial ultra-high voltage power line that could slice through the center of the county, appropriating $250,000 to cover potential legal expenses related to the project… Valley Link Transmission Company, a partnership between Dominion Energy, First Energy Transmission and Transource, has proposed the 115-mile, 765-kilovolt (kV) transmission line that, if approved by state regulators, would cross as many as nine mostly rural counties as it travels from the Joshua Falls substation near Lynchburg to the proposed Yeat substation in southeastern Culpeper…The company has said it plans to submit a single preferred route to the State Corporation Commission (SCC) for approval by September. It hopes to energize the $1 billion project by 2029.”

title: Berkeley County residents clash with county over proposed 1,500-acre solar farm
snippet: “Boos and pleas for county leaders to protect a rural community filled a public hearing in Berkeley County as residents in Cross pushed back against a proposed 1,500-acre solar farm and the decision to advance a request to lift a large-scale development moratorium. The Berkeley County Land Use Committee passed a motion to lift a large-scale development moratorium covering nearly 7,500 acres in the Cross community. The action moves forward a proposal tied to the Sandy Run Solar Project, which would require rezoning 1,500 acres to industrial planned development for a 198-megawatt solar farm that could power more than 37,000 homes… The matter will now go before the full Berkeley County Council at its next meeting in two weeks. If approved, RWE would still need county approval to rezone the 1,500 acres for the solar farm.”

snippet: “The Baldwin County Commission is seeking guidance from the Alabama attorney general on whether it can impose a temporary moratorium on solar developments in unincorporated areas, as concerns grow over a proposed large-scale project near Stockton. Commissioners approved a resolution authorizing the county attorney to request a formal opinion on the legality of temporary moratoriums on certain land use and construction approvals in areas without zoning regulations. The request comes amid increasing public concern over a proposed utility-scale solar farm on roughly 4,500 acres near the unincorporated community of Stockton. The project, proposed by Silicon Ranch Corp., has prompted objections from residents and environmental groups who warn of potential impacts on flood-prone land and natural resources connected to the Tensaw River and its delta… No timeline has been announced for when the attorney general’s office may respond.”

title: Quincy Township Adopts Strict Battery Storage Ordinance
snippet: “Quincy Township officials recently [unanimously] adopted a restrictive new ordinance for battery energy storage systems (BESS) to maintain local authority over utility-scale energy projects… The measure…restricts large-scale battery facilities exclusively to industrial zones as a special land use. This strategic move is designed to comply with Michigan’s Public Act 233, a 2023 law that allows the state to override local zoning for renewable energy projects unless a community has a ‘compatible’ local ordinance already in place. The new rules come in response to significant community opposition regarding the proposed 180-megawatt Santina Energy project on Boone Road, which residents feared could lead to fire hazards and groundwater contamination.”
