title: Oshtemo hosts discussions after battery storage prompts controversy
snippet: “Oshtemo Township is launching Community Conversations to give residents more time to chat with elected officials… The first Community Conversation last week focused on battery energy storage systems. The township passed a moratorium to block the development of battery storage facilities after NewEdge Power approached the township about building a 260-megawatt battery storage facility in 2024. Multiple community members have spoken out against the proposed battery storage facility. The battery storage facility was among reasons why some residents supported a campaign to recall several board members. New Edge hasn’t made any formal development proposals.”

title: Rules approved for data centers and battery storage – The Commercial Review
snippet: “Jay County Commissioners approved two new ordinances regulating battery storage facilities and data centers and amended existing ordinances regarding commercial solar and wind farms Monday. They also repealed a one-year moratorium on new wind farm developments and retroactively ended one-year moratoriums on new solar farm and battery storage facility developments. Jay/Portland Building and Planning has been working with the county plan commission on drafting zoning regulations for battery storage and data center facilities, as well as updating zoning restrictions for solar and wind farms, for about a year… Moratoriums on new solar farm and battery storage developments in Jay County ended April 28. The moratorium on new wind farm developments was set to remain in effect through July 21… With the ordinance approved Monday, [BESS] facilities in Jay County must have 500-foot setbacks from property lines. Equipment also needs to be enclosed within a 7-foot security fence set back 50 feet from the property line. Permit fees are $2,500 per megawatt.”

title: County enacts one-year moratorium on solar, wind projects
snippet: “Morrison County commissioners approved a one-year moratorium on certain solar and wind energy projects May 5, following a recommendation from the Morrison County Planning Commission. The interim ordinance applies to community solar energy systems, solar farms and wind energy conversion systems under county jurisdiction. It gives the county time to study how larger renewable energy projects fit into its comprehensive plan, zoning ordinance and land-use policies.”

title: Watching Snoqualmie, North Bend Pursues Moratorium on Battery Storage
snippet: “As the proposed Cascadia Ridge battery energy storage facility near Snoqualmie Ridge continues through King County’s permitting process, the City of North Bend is taking steps to ensure it doesn’t face a similar situation on its own doorstep. At an April 28 work study, North Bend city staff and council members discussed a proposed 12-month moratorium on commercial battery storage facilities, with a formal vote and public hearing scheduled for May 19. Community & Economic Development Director James Henderson presented the proposal, describing it as a response to growing interest in large-scale battery energy storage systems in the region… The May 19 North Bend City Council meeting is open to the public. A public hearing on the moratorium ordinance is expected the same night.”

title: Desert Rainbow Mega Solar Farm Targets 5,000 Acres In Goodyear
snippet: “A global energy company is looking to cover a big slice of southern Goodyear with panels and batteries, filing plans for a roughly 5,011-acre solar and storage complex that would put out about 550 megawatts of solar power and store another 550 megawatts. The project, called Desert Rainbow, is slated for Rainbow Valley and is pitched as producing enough carbon-free electricity to serve the equivalent of roughly 165,000 homes. At that scale, it would rank among the largest utility-scale solar footprints proposed in Arizona in recent years… City records show a special-use permit application, Case No. P25-00259, submitted by Desert Rainbow LLC for roughly 5,011 acres south of Queen Creek Road and east of Waterman Wash in Rainbow Valley… AES has penciled in development and permitting through 2029, with construction expected to run from 2029 to 2032 and operations following that window.”

title: Planning Commission Approves Acreage Cap for Solar Panels – Noozhawk
snippet: “The Santa Barbara County Planning Commission decided to cap the number of acres available for solar panel use to 16,000 acres and streamline the building process for new installations. The commission approved the countywide cap in a 3-1 vote on Wednesday during a discussion about where solar panels could be built, and which projects would be exempted from permitting… The 16,000-acre cap chosen by the commission will limit solar panels on agricultural land to 2% of the county’s available acreage.”

title: Move over, sheep. Cattle are grazing solar sites, too, and that’s good news for Virginia.
snippet: “Farmers are finding that cattle also thrive among solar panels — and they will get their chance to prove it in Virginia. The market for beef is vastly bigger than the market for lamb, so persuading farmers they should diversify into sheep as well as solar is a tall order. But if cattle prove as compatible with solar as sheep are, there will be vastly more opportunities for both farmers and the solar industry. Given the dire economic situation facing small farms in Virginia today, “cattle-voltaics” could offer a lifeline for rural communities.”

title: Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban
snippet: “Exit polling suggests Richland County would have elected to repeal its wind and solar ban, if not for residents who mistakenly voted in favor of the restriction. SB 52 states that for any referendum against a county’s ban, its commissioners’ resolution needs a majority vote in favor to go into effect So, a ​“yes” vote means someone opposes the referendum effort to overturn the ban, while a ​“no” vote means the person backs the campaign to get rid of the ban. “It’s confusing,” County Commissioner Cliff Mears told Canary Media.”

title: Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit
snippet: “The three-judge Michigan Court of Appeals panel ruled that state regulators followed proper legal processes when they set rules to carry out a controversial 2023 law that allows the Michigan Public Service Commission to approve large wind, solar and battery projects over local objections. But judges ruled the commission interpreted certain aspects of that law in ways that improperly limited local power.It wasn’t immediately clear how the split ruling could affect the multiple renewable energy development proposals currently awaiting state approval. However, state officials seemed to see it as a victory.”

title: Gas power leapfrogs wind for first time in 10 years in Texas’ grid connection queue
snippet: “For the last six months, the volume of gas generation in the Texas grid’s interconnection queue — the yearslong waiting list for electric generators wanting to connect to the grid — has surpassed wind.It’s the first time since January 2016 that gas has overtaken wind in the queue, a shift that reflects the policy and economic headwinds facing the wind industry and data centers favoring gas power as they seek to cash in on the artificial intelligence boom. ”

title: Amazon Invests in 700 MW of New Carbon-Free Energy Projects to Power Future Data Centers in Nevada
snippet: “Amazon announced that it is investing in 700 MW of new carbon-free energy projects in Nevada to power its future data center operations in the area. The company said that it has signed an agreement with Nevada-based NV Energy, a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway Energy, to add 100 MW of geothermal power from renewable energy company Zanskar, and 600 MW of solar plus 600 MW of battery storage from renewable energy developer Primergy to the Nevada power grid.”

snippet: It would be powered “100% off the Ruby Pipeline,” the natural gas line that crosses the area. The first phase of the project is expected to consume 3 gigawatts of electricity. O’Leary didn’t commit to actually using any of the renewables. ”

title: CT Senate passes major solar energy legislation
snippet: The Connecticut Senate passed House Bill 5340 on the last day of the 2026 legislative session. The bill extends solar incentive programs for residential, commercial, and community solar through 2035, expedites permitting for plug-in solar panels, creates a statewide agrivoltaics program, and establishes a pilot for low- or no-cost solar in 100 environmental justice homes. It also imposes a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield. Democratic Rep. Jonathan Steinberg stated, “This bill modernizes Connecticut’s renewable energy programs by introducing measures meant to make them more reliable, consistent, and flexible to economic fluctuations.” The bill now heads to Gov. Ned Lamont.

It also imposes a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield. Democratic Rep. Jonathan Steinberg stated, “This bill modernizes Connecticut’s renewable energy programs by introducing measures meant to make them more reliable, consistent, and flexible to economic fluctuations.” The bill now heads to Gov. title: Warren County commissioners uphold current solar farm ordinance
snippet: Warren County commissioners unanimously voted to reject proposed text amendments to the solar farm ordinance that would have allowed larger projects beyond the current 150-acre cap. The decision upholds the 2022 ordinance and blocks amendments sought by TED Renewables for the Horizon Solar project (160 MW combined on ~900 acres across two sites). Planning Director Mark Bloomer warned the changes would “open the door to solar farms of any size.” The meeting drew more than 40 speakers and lasted over four hours.

title: Clean energy groups push back on North Carolina regulators' solar energy pause
snippet: The Southern Environmental Law Center filed a motion asking the North Carolina Utilities Commission to reconsider its order pausing Duke Energy’s 2026 solar procurement (roughly 770 MW). The unusual order was issued solely by Commission Chair William Brawley without a public hearing. SELC called the pause “arbitrary and capricious” and warned that delaying the “missing megawatts” threatens reliability and affordability.

title: No vote yet on large-scale Southern Michigan solar project after months of opposition
snippet: The Fayette Township Planning Commission postponed any decision on a special use permit for Ranger Power’s Heartwood Solar II project (approximately 1,000 acres of panels on 1,400 acres east of Jonesville). The hearing on May 7 drew strong resident opposition. Resident Steve Oleszkowicz stated, “One out of every four acres of productive, agricultural land in this township gone, converted, industrialized. That is not incremental change. That is not balanced development. That is a transformation of this community.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss noted, “Load is growing in Michigan and solar is currently the most affordable way to meet that demand.” No new meeting date was set.

title: Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit
snippet: A Michigan Court of Appeals panel upheld most of the Michigan Public Service Commission’s rules implementing Public Act 233 but sided with local governments on two key points: the 30-day local approval clock starts after a meeting with developers (not when offered), and any local unit touching project land qualifies as an “affected local unit” eligible for developer payments. The ruling largely affirms the state’s ability to approve large solar, wind, and battery projects over local objections when towns lack compatible ordinances. PSC spokesperson Matt Helms said the decision “largely affirms the Commission’s approach.”

title: Farmers push back on proposed solar farm that could convert 1,500 acres of northern Michigan farmland
snippet: Farmers in Wexford and Grand Traverse counties are opposing Ranger Power’s (via subsidiary Shipyard Solar) early-stage proposal for a large solar array on nearly 1,500 acres of prime agricultural land. The project has not yet filed formal applications. Farmer James Cade stated, “You’re taking valuable farm ground, that is some of the best ground in the state of Michigan to raise vegetables that are feeding people.” Ranger Power’s Ryan Wardin said developments are built on “voluntary partnerships with local landowners who see solar as a benefit to their farming operations.” A public meeting is scheduled for May 18.

title: More turbulence for Washington State’s giant wind farm
snippet: Rep. Dan Newhouse continues to push the FAA to revoke “no hazard” determinations for Scout Clean Energy’s Horse Heaven wind farm in Benton County. Scout has filed new airspace applications while the project remains under litigation at the Washington Supreme Court (arguments in June). The developer has already reduced turbine numbers and increased heights. No public record exists on whether prior FAA approvals were revoked.

title: State Land Office awards lease to proposed wind farm near Corona
snippet: The New Mexico State Land Office awarded a lease to Southwestern Power Group for a 212 MW portion of the 1,100 MW Cedarvale Wind Project in Torrance County (8 miles northwest of Corona). The full project includes 243 turbines on 10,160 acres of state trust land and 51,149 acres of private land. It is projected to generate $98.9 million in school revenue over the lease lifetime. Construction is expected to begin in 2027 with operations in 2029. Land Commissioner Stephanie Garcia Richard stated, “Projects like this one are job creators in our rural communities and create long-lasting revenue streams for our public schools and other institutions.”

title: Navajo County citizens petition for immediate moratorium on utility-scale renewables
snippet: Citizens of Navajo County, Arizona, launched a Change.org petition calling for an immediate moratorium on all utility-scale wind, solar, and battery projects. The petition targets West Camp Wind Farm II (500 MW, 144+ turbines) and criticizes the dissolved REAL AZ entity’s continued influence. It demands 2-mile setbacks, signed power purchase agreements, independent water and soil studies, and full decommissioning bonding.

title: No decision yet on proposed battery storage sites in Huntington Town
snippet: The Huntington Town Board has not yet decided on two proposed battery energy storage facilities (one by Key Capture Energy) in East Northport and near the Greenlawn substation. A public hearing was held Tuesday with significant resident concerns over fire safety and emergency response for volunteer fire departments. Town Councilman Dave Bennardo stated, “The amount of false alarms that you have with a battery storage facility, the protocols of waiting outside the gates until you can get management to open the doors, that's impossible right now for a volunteer firefighting crew.” The board plans to meet with fire officials before voting and is considering a six-month moratorium to study risks.

title: Resident-led campaign fails to reverse Ohio county’s ban on renewables
snippet: Voters in Richland County, Ohio, rejected a referendum (53%–47%) to overturn a ban on utility-scale solar and wind in 11 townships. The ban, passed in 2025, remains in place. Campaign leader Morgan Carroll said, “We made a commitment to stand up to our elected officials… That’s not nothing. That is everything.”

title: Dominion upbeat on offshore wind as cost estimate eases, sales rise
snippet: Dominion Energy reported that its Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind (CVOW) project (2.6 GW) is now 75% complete, with nine of 176 turbines installed and power generation underway since March 2026. The company lowered its capital cost estimate to $11.4 billion while maintaining a levelized cost of energy at $84/MWh. CEO Robert Blue said, “With no change to either LCOE or customer bill impacts, CVOW remains one of the most affordable sources of energy for our customers.” The project is expected to generate approximately $5 billion in fuel savings over its first 10 years.

title: Oshtemo launches ‘Community Conversations’ after battery storage prompts controversy
snippet: Oshtemo Township (Kalamazoo County) launched monthly “Community Conversations” (first one Thursday, May 7) to give residents more informal input opportunities after controversy over a proposed 260 MW battery storage facility by NewEdge Power in 2024. The project was blocked by a township moratorium. Supervisor Cheri Bell said the sessions are “meant to be welcoming, unhurried and focused on listening… We want people to feel comfortable asking questions, sharing ideas and helping shape Oshtemo’s future.”

title: Douglas County residents ask commission to consider moratorium on battery systems, data centers
snippet: Douglas County commissioners held a work session on May 6, 2026, where residents requested a moratorium or ban on battery energy storage systems (BESS) and data centers, despite no local projects being proposed. title: Emery County Commission begins drafting ordinance to prohibit future utility-scale solar development
snippet: The Emery County Commission voted 2-1 on May 6, 2026, to begin drafting an ordinance prohibiting new utility-scale solar projects in the county. The move follows strong public opposition to a proposed solar project at Poison Springs Bench/Drunkard’s Wash. Commissioner Keven Jensen stated, “I think the strongest tool that we have to stop this project is the power of the ordinance.” Commissioner Jordan Leonard added, “I think at this point no more utility-scale solar… the public has spoken on that.”

title: Goshen adopts six-month solar energy moratorium
snippet: The Goshen Town Board passed a six-month moratorium on new solar farms at its April 23 meeting (4-0 vote). The moratorium does not affect projects already undergoing review. Councilman George Lyons stated, “We are not against solar farms. They are getting out of control. We want to take a closer look at it.” Councilman Richard Florio added, “We’re not looking to ban solar. We just want to make sure it’s done right.” Councilman Philip Canterino noted that solar farms were intended to be capped at five megawatts but projects were combining multiple parcels for higher output.

title: Baldwin County seeks legal clarity on solar moratorium amid Stockton project concerns
snippet: The Baldwin County Commission approved a resolution asking the Alabama attorney general for a formal opinion on whether it can impose a temporary moratorium on utility-scale solar developments in un-zoned, unincorporated areas (especially flood-prone zones). The request is driven by concerns over a proposed utility-scale solar farm on roughly 4,500 acres near the unincorporated community of Stockton by Silicon Ranch Corp. The nonprofit Friends of the Tensaw River has called for a one-year pause on permits to study environmental risks tied to the Tensaw River and its delta.

title: Marshall County approves ordinances restricting solar farms and prohibiting carbon capture
snippet: Marshall County commissioners gave final approval (2-0 vote) to the Farm Scale Solar Energy System ordinance, which strictly prohibits large-scale solar panel farms spanning multiple properties and acres across any zoning district. title: West Lafayette City Council approves solar power restrictions
snippet: The West Lafayette City Council approved (5-1 vote) an ordinance limiting new commercial solar developments to 400 acres per project, with at least one mile between projects and 500 feet from neighboring property lines. Total commercial solar in the county is capped at 6,000 acres. District 5 Rep. Kathy Parker stated, “It’s better than nothing, right? … If nobody comes for a whole year, then maybe we reexamine … It’s a starting place.”

title: Public hearing set for Longspur Wind Project in Morton County
snippet: The North Dakota Public Service Commission has scheduled a public hearing for 10 a.m. on May 18 at the City Auditorium in Glen Ullin on Minnesota Power’s proposed Longspur Wind Project (up to 45 turbines, 202.5 MW on ~26,100 acres in Morton County plus a 2.5-mile transmission line to Mercer County). Estimated cost: $791 million. ALLETE CEO Bethany Owen stated, “Longspur is part of the approximately $5 billion in investments we will be making over the next five years to serve our region.”

title: Developer of Watsonville battery storage project pivots to state permitting route
snippet: New Leaf Energy is withdrawing its county application for the Seahawk Energy Storage project (160-unit BESS on 16 acres at 90 Minto Road in unincorporated Watsonville) and will instead use the California Energy Commission’s “Opt-in” process under AB 205 to meet CAISO deadlines. Project lead Max Christian stated that the county’s draft ordinance amendments “would likely prevent the necessary upstream procurement commitments.” The company still supports the core draft ordinance, calling it “one of the — if not the — strongest battery storage ordinances in the country.”

title: Clay Town Board to hold public hearing on new rules for battery storage projects
snippet: The Town of Clay held a public hearing (May 4, 2026) at 7:47 p.m. at Town Hall on proposed Local Law No. 4 of 2026 to establish guidelines for battery energy storage systems (BESS). The hearing follows resident concerns over potential projects on Long Branch Road, Wetzel Road and Goguen Drive. Proposed rules include tighter site-location limits, updated fire-safety and emergency-response requirements, noise and screening standards, clearer decommissioning rules, and alignment with updated state fire codes. Deputy Supervisor Joe Bick stated, “The hearing will stay open until we get the law right.” No vote will be taken; the hearing will remain open until the law is finalized.

title: GE Vernova challenges Vineyard Wind’s claims of harm if it leaves project
snippet: GE Vernova filed an emergency motion to reconsider a preliminary injunction barring it from exiting its contract with Vineyard Wind 1, arguing that the project’s commercial operation date (achieved April 24, 2026) and activation of power purchase agreements undermine claims of irreparable harm. Vineyard Wind (806 MW, 62 turbines) is currently generating at less than half capacity. Attorney Michael Scheinkman for Vineyard Wind stated, “This is an 806-megawatt project that’s generating output at less than half of that, which is not sufficient to generate commercially viable levels of electricity.” The next court hearing is scheduled for May 12.

title: Tippecanoe County adopts revised solar ordinance, lifting 11-month moratorium
snippet: Tippecanoe County commissioners unanimously approved a new large-scale solar ordinance on May 4, 2026, ending an 11-month moratorium. The ordinance caps individual commercial solar developments at 400 acres, increases setbacks to 500 feet from adjacent property lines, and limits total commercial solar development in the county to 6,000 acres. Commissioner Dave Byers stated, “As the resident farmer up here, ... don’t think I don’t cringe every time we agree to a 40-acre development or an 80-acre development. I know that’s good ground disappearing. I was against this from the beginning. I see this as a compromise.” The changes were prompted by the proposed 1,700-acre Rainbow Trout Solar project.

title: Meridian Township Board considers creating data center and battery storage facility moratorium
snippet: The Meridian Township Board is considering a moratorium on data centers and battery storage facilities to allow time to develop protective guidelines. Supervisor Scott Hendrickson stated, “The reason why I was looking to chat about moratoriums, specifically, is because what I feel like we have seen across the state is a propensity to buy up property, eliminate the existing use and convert into a data center or a battery storage facility.” Community Planning Director Tim Schmitt noted no current proposals exist in the township. A decision could come at the May 7 meeting.

title: Two-year moratorium on commercial alternative energy projects in Grand County continues until June 2027
snippet: The Grand County Board of County Commissioners voted 3-0 to extend a two-year moratorium on commercial solar, wind, battery, and energy storage projects (those exceeding 25 kW) until June 24, 2027. The moratorium, which began June 24, 2025, gives the county time to update its master plan and draft regulations. Community Development Director Kristen Manguso stated, “We have less than 30 percent of developable area… The land is valuable here, and it is more valuable for development than it is for solar farms.” The measure does not affect residential solar.

title: California investigates Trump administration’s deal to end an offshore wind project
snippet: The California Energy Commission issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind seeking documents about its agreement with the Trump administration to terminate its offshore wind lease off California’s central coast in exchange for a payout (part of nearly $900 million in recent deals). CEC Chair David Hochschild stated, “The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation. Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout.” The administration has committed nearly $2 billion total to wind down offshore wind leases.

title: Clay Town Board to hold hearing on proposed battery storage law
snippet: The Town of Clay will hold a public hearing Monday at 7 p.m. at Town Hall on Local Law No. 4 of 2026, which would create guidelines for siting and installing battery energy storage systems. Two companies, Carson Power and Nexamp, are exploring potential sites in the town. Town officials noted the law aims to ensure responsible siting while supporting the transition to renewables. Local fire officials have begun discussions with developers on emergency response planning.

title: Southold Town Board extends BESS moratorium to 2027 amid safety and zoning debate
snippet: The Southold Town Board unanimously approved a 12-month extension of its moratorium on battery energy storage systems through April 2027. The moratorium, first enacted in 2023, gives the town time to update zoning, reconvene the BESS Task Force, and address fire safety concerns following a proposed 60 MW lithium-ion BESS by Key Capture Energy on Oregon Road in Cutchogue. Supervisor Al Krupski stated, “The recommended 12-month extension of the moratorium would be time to complete and accomplish the steps recommended by the state in their new BESS guidance document.” Kevin O’Mara of Friends of Oregon Road said, “The town has spent millions of dollars preserving the agricultural land around this site, which would become unusable if there were a fire because of the contamination of these heavy metals.”

title: AG rules against Southwick’s temporary moratorium on solar arrays and BESS systems
snippet: The Massachusetts Attorney General’s office disapproved Southwick’s temporary moratorium on large-scale solar arrays and battery energy storage systems, ruling it lacked sufficient public health, safety, or welfare justification and violated state law prohibiting unreasonable regulation of solar installations. The moratorium had been approved at a Special Town Meeting in mid-January 2026 and was set to last until May 31, 2027. Town Planner Alvin Allen stated, “If a company puts in an application, we’d have to move forward through our current process and hear it within 60 days.” Planning Board Chair Jessica Thornton said the ruling “was not unexpected” and that the town is awaiting a state template for zoning bylaws.

title: House OKs solar bill; GOP targets impact on public benefits charge
snippet: The Connecticut House passed House Bill 5340 on May 1, 2026, reauthorizing the state’s residential, commercial, and community solar programs through 2035 with an $85 million annual budget target. The bill includes expedited permitting for plug-in solar panels, a one-year moratorium on new grid-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield, and exemptions for solar paired with battery storage. Lori Brown of the Connecticut League of Conservation Voters said, “If they don’t pass a solar bill this session to extend the programs, it would be an epic failure on the part of a legislature.” State Sen. Ryan Fazio, R-Greenwich, called it “unconscionable policy… extraordinarily expensive.”

title: Virginia’s new law blocks counties from banning solar
snippet: Gov. Abigail Spanberger signed legislation that voids community-wide bans on solar fields and requires localities to submit written reasons when denying permits. The law takes effect July 1. Two-thirds of Virginia counties currently ban or heavily restrict solar. Evan Vaughan of the Mid-Atlantic Renewable Energy Coalition said, “Localities still are in the driver’s seat here. They can still deny every project from now until the end of time if they want.”

title: Bills to repeal Michigan’s clean energy mandate advanced by GOP-controlled House committee
snippet: The House Energy Committee advanced HB 5710 and HB 5711 along party lines. HB 5711 would eliminate the 100% clean energy standard; HB 5710 would remove environmental justice and greenhouse gas considerations from utility planning. Samarth Medakkar of Advanced Energy United said, “With energy costs skyrocketing this winter and the expensive summer cooling season looming, it is alarmingly clear why it’s so important that we diversify our energy resource mix.”

title: Judge temporarily denies Rancho Viejo Solar opponents’ request for records
snippet: A state district judge temporarily blocked the Clean Energy Coalition’s discovery requests in its appeal of Santa Fe County’s approval of the 96 MW Rancho Viejo Solar project (plus battery storage) on 680 acres south of Santa Fe. Coalition president Lee Zlotoff said the group is seeking information on whether “deals were made between the county and AES.”

title: Wilson looking to pass battery energy storage law
snippet: “Wilson is set to vote on a new local law limiting where certain battery energy storage systems can operate in the village. The law would limit Tier 2 battery storage systems in industrial-zoned areas, which for the village would be its southern parcels. Tier 1 storage systems, which can store up to 600 kWh, are allowed in all areas of the village, given their usage for rooftop solar systems. The language is based on the model law for battery storage that NYSERDA released, with Village Mayor Gregory Marin saying it is very similar to the Village of Lancaster’s law… The Niagara County Planning Board gave its support to this law at its April meeting, with the village board planning to vote on the law at its May 21 meeting. Wilson is among many Niagara County municipalities looking to establish such regulations, with others including Cambria, Newfane, Pendleton, and Porter.”

title: Southold Extends Moratorium on Controversial BESS Energy Storage Sites
snippet: “The Town of Southold extended a moratorium on the construction of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) on the North Fork to give officials time to finalize regulation of the facilities. The Southold Town Board unanimously voted on April 21 in favor of extending the BESS moratorium for another year following a public hearing. The decision was made to give the town’s Battery Energy Task Force and town board to follow New York State guidance on setting BESS policies to help mitigate the potential for harder-to-extinguish fires that can break out at such facilities.”

snippet: “The Pulaski County Advisory Plan Commission (APC) is recommending a 12 month moratorium on Commercial Solar Energy Systems (CSES) to the Pulaski County Commissioners. A special meeting with a public hearing was held on Monday, April 20… Before hearing public comment, Building Inspector and Plan Administrator Karla Redweik read the proposed ordinance with the 12 month moratorium aloud. The purpose of the ordinance and the moratorium is to give them time to understand the prevalence and presence of CSES within the zoning jurisdiction of Pulaski County, in consideration of the public life, safety, health and general welfare of Pulaski County, the effects on residents and natural resources and to conduct appropriate studies and to gauge whether CSES is appropriate within Pulaski County, and if so, the extent to which regulation should be developed and enforced beyond current ordinance regulations.”

title: Headline
snippet: “ANDOVER — The Ashtabula County Board of Commissioners spoke to local residents Wednesday at the Andover Public Library… Commissioner J.P. Ducro spoke about potential wind and solar power projects in the county. ‘The position of the commissioners has really been wanting to defer to local control,’ he said… He said the commissioners are aware some people have been approached about land leases. ‘Nothing has really even come to our desk at this point and time for us to weigh in on officially,’ Ducro said. Under Ohio Senate Bill 52, county commissioners have the power to give a thumbs up or down to economically significant wind and solar projects, which generate between five and 50 megawatts, he said. Ducro said the commissioners are working with the county building department to map out where they would permit or not permit wind and solar projects. He said the plan is to detail and update the map with each township wants them, do not want them or wants to restrict them.‘That process is probably going to take some time,’ Ducro said. ‘We imagine that that’s going to probably take us through the year 2026.’”

title: Kossuth County Holds Public Hearing on Wind Farm Restart
snippet: “The Kossuth County Board of Supervisors held a public hearing to potentially repower the Lakota Wind Farm. Iowa Lakes Electric Cooperative President and CEO Ryan Craig explained why the cooperative was applying for the permit. The project will work with the present wind generation units by upgrading them according to Craig. The hope is by using existing towers and systems, the cooperative can realize the full potential of the wind farm. The board approved the proposal.”

title: Solar debate spurs passionate comments from York County residents
snippet: “This week’s solar zoning debate spurred many passionate comments from York County residents as the county commissioners were tasked with creating solar zoning regulations. A public hearing was held Monday night and more public comment was heard Tuesday morning before the county board took a vote, ultimately deciding (with a split vote) to create regulations that include half-mile setbacks for non-participating residences, churches and schools.”

title: [Opinion] No time for timidity on clean energy
snippet: “Massachusetts has long been a leader in advancing smart, effective energy and environmental policies that reduce pollution, protect public health, create jobs, and lower costs for consumers over time. But lately, too many policymakers in Massachusetts are staying silent or even backtracking on proven strategies. Policymakers are failing to push back as fossil fuel interests try to distract from the harm they cause to our environment, public health, and runaway energy costs. As fuel prices surge from the war in the Middle East and residents demand relief from their monthly bills, now is the time to deliver clean energy and energy efficiency solutions for the people of Massachusetts.”

title: NY PSC Virtual Public Hearing on North Seneca Solar
snippet: “ The New York State Public Service Commission (Commission) will hold a virtual public statement hearing and procedural conference on Wednesday, May 6, 2026, regarding a petition filed on December 18, 2025, by North Seneca Solar Project, LLC (North Seneca Solar) seeking a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) pursuant to Public Service Law (PSL) Section 68, lightened regulation as a wholesale electric generator. North Seneca Solar seeks authorization to construct and operate a solar-powered electric generating facility with a maximum capacity of 90 megawatts in the towns of Waterloo and Junius, Seneca County. The facility will interconnect to the New York power grid via a switching station that will connect to the existing National Grid Farmington – Hamilton 115 kilovolt transmission line.”

The decision not only caught many locals by surprise; it also struck them as bad for economic development and as encroaching on individual property rights. Almost immediately after the county’s three commissioners made their decision, dozens of residents formed a group, called the Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development, to fight what they saw as an unjust restriction on renewable energy. Their initial goal was clear but daunting
snippet: Collect thousands of in-person signatures within 30 days in order to put the clean energy ban on the ballot during the 2026 primary election. They succeeded.”

title: Cerro Gordo County officials clash with state over solar development
snippet: “The Iowa Utilities Commission has issued a final order advancing review of a proposed large-scale solar project in Cerro Gordo County, setting up a clash with local officials who enacted a moratorium and subsequent ban on such developments. In a decision filed Monday, April 27, the commission required additional information from River City Energy LLC as part of its application for a generating certificate for a 500-megawatt solar facility planned on roughly 2,250 acres in the county. The state’s action comes after Cerro Gordo County supervisors already moved to halt such projects locally. Following months of public meetings and debate, the board approved an ordinance amendment banning new utility-scale wind, solar and battery storage developments in unincorporated areas, replacing a temporary moratorium first adopted to study land-use impacts.”

title: Decision delayed on proposed energy project in Sedgwick County
snippet: “A proposed solar and battery storage project in Sedgwick County will not go to county commissioners just yet. During a special meeting on Thursday, the Wichita-Sedgwick County Metropolitan Area Planning Commission chose to put off a decision on the Galena Solar Project until June 11. The company in charge of the project, Mission Clean Energy, submitted its application at the end of January. If it’s approved, 1,490 acres of land southwest of Wichita would be used for solar panels and battery energy storage systems. Energy from the systems would be transmitted to an Evergy substation near the project area.”

title: [Opinion] Proposed Evergy electric transmission line would only hurt Kansas
snippet: “As a Kansas rancher, I am writing to sound the alarm on Evergy’s proposed 133-mile, 345 kilovolt Buffalo Flats transmission line. This project, currently before the Kansas Corporation Commission, represents a profound threat to our state’s heritage, economy and environment. First, the Buffalo Flats project stinks of an insider deal. Unlike most major infrastructure, this $500 million project was not put out for public bid. On other Kansas projects, competitive bidding for transmission lines has realized savings of 40%. By bypassing this process, Kansas ratepayers are potentially left on the hook for hundreds of millions in unnecessary costs.”

title: York County Commissioners approve zoning for solar farm, creates ‘tough environment’ for project
snippet: “The York County Commissioners voted 3-2 to approve the zoning regulations surrounding a proposed 3,000-acre solar farm. Dozens of community members came out to make their voices heard one last time, filling every seat in the room and pleading with commissioners. ‘I moved out to York County to be surrounded by farm land, not an industrial solar park,’ said Kirkland Doht, a York County resident. The zoning regulations in place include a half-mile setback from any homeowner who is not a willing partner and from platted subdivisions. Other setbacks include 330 feet from cemeteries and 660 feet from state recreational areas. Commissioners decided to adjust the setback from churches and schools, which was previously set to 660 feet and is now a half-mile.”

title: San Benito County keeps battery storage at bay, DMV data sharing could hurt undocumented drivers
snippet: “The San Benito County Board of Supervisors has voted unanimously to adopt a new temporary moratorium on Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in unincorporated areas. The urgency ordinance passed on Tuesday cited public health and safety concerns. It extends a 45-day moratorium approved on March 10 for another 45 days. The county says they’ve used the moratorium to update parts of their countywide safety plans related to evacuations, emergency response, and hazardous materials.”

title: [Opinion] What does it mean to execute renewable energy in Minnesota? “Renewable Energy has been marketed as the oasis of clean energy, but what if this wasn’t the whole truth?... In truth, clean energy, specifically, renewable energy, is very sustainable and efficient when it’s working; however, the process that goes into constructing them is less sustainable. The latest example of this is the Boundary Waters… last Thursday, the Senate overturned this law in a 50-49 vote, jumpstarting the long-delayed mine proposed for the Superior National Forest near the Boundary Waters… The mining will contaminate the groundwater and just disturb the nature that makes the Boundary Waters what it is… These events all come as Minnesota strives toward its 2040 goal

title: NY PSC to Hold Public Statement Hearing to Discuss Two Rivers Solar Farm
snippet: “The New York Public Service Commission will hold a virtual public statement hearing to discuss Two Rivers Solar Farm LLC’s proposed 200-megawatt solar electric generating facility in St. Lawrence County. The facility would interconnect to the power grid via the New York Power Authority’s Haverstock – Willis E 345 kilovolt transmission line. The company plans to build the project on land leased and/or purchased from owners of private property within an area of approximately 3,168 acres. The project has the potential to power approximately 56,000 homes. The company is seeking a lightened regulatory regime as it intends to operate the project as a competitive wholesale generator. Comments are due by May 6. 25-E-0616 ”

title: Guilderland sets four hearings inspired by updated comprehensive plan
snippet: “On April 21, the board agreed to set public hearings for four proposed local laws… A moratorium on battery energy storage systems, known as BESS… During the April 21 public comment period, Robyn Gray, who chairs the Guilderland Coalition for Responsible Growth, told the board that residents had been receiving solicitation letters from energy developers offering ‘anywhere between ten- and thirty-thousand dollars a year rent’ to install shipping-container-sized batteries on their properties… Supervisor Peter Barber told Gray the town was already moving on the issue, proposing to enact a temporary ban on BESS installations. Later in the meeting, the board set a May 19 public hearing on Barber’s proposed four-month moratorium.”

title: Town of Oyster Bay approves sixth battery energy system storage moratorium
snippet: “The Town of Oyster Bay board voted unanimously to extend its moratorium on battery energy storage systems for another 12 months, citing ongoing safety concerns and continued public opposition during a recent meeting. The resolution passed with six votes in favor and none opposed as part of a broader action calendar approved by the town board during their regular April 14 meeting. It is another win for the anti-battery storage movement in the Glen Head community, which rallied around opposition to the Oyster Shore Energy Storage, a now-cancelled project by Jupiter Power in Glen Head which the moratorium was initially created to prevent.”

title: Dearborn County plan commission votes against creating a solar farm advisory committee
snippet: “The Dearborn County Plan Commission voted unanimously 8-0 against forming an advisory committee to amend commercial ordinances regarding solar, battery energy storage systems and data centers. The vote took place Monday evening, during the commission's first meeting since a 12-month moratorium on such developments was enacted in February. Elected officials said there will still be opportunities for public input throughout the ordinance process… It will now be up to the county commissioners to decide if a review committee is formed at that level, as recommended by the Plan Commission. The first in a series of informational workshops will be held May 20, where officials said there will be more opportunities for public input.”

title: CT public benefits charge cut; electricity rates to fall by nearly 15%
snippet: “Electricity rates in Connecticut will decline by nearly 15% in May, after state regulators on Wednesday approved changes that will all but remove the public benefits charge from customer bills. The Public Utilities Regulatory Authority voted unanimously in favor of the rate adjustments, which largely affect the costs borne by utilities for various state-mandated policy programs such as clean energy procurements and benefits for low-income customers.”

title: Southold Town Board extends BESS moratorium to 2027 amid safety, zoning debate
snippet: “Southold Town Board unanimously approved a 12-month extension of its moratorium on Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) facilities on Tuesday — buying time to draft and vote on local legislation governing their use. The latest extension keeps the moratorium in place through April 2027. The law was first enacted by the Town Board on Jan. 31, 2023, with an effective date of April 11, 2023, following public scrutiny of a proposed BESS facility by Key Capture Energy on Oregon Road in Cutchogue the previous year… Town officials said the additional year will allow them to reconvene the town’s BESS Task Force, gather public input and craft regulations aligned with new state guidance.”

title: Baldwin County residents push for solar moratorium as legal review continues
snippet: “A group of North Baldwin County residents is asking commissioners to temporarily halt large-scale solar development, citing environmental concerns and a lack of independent oversight. Megan Fowler spoke today on behalf of Friends of the Tensaw River, a newly formed grassroots organization, who made the request during Tuesday’s Baldwin County Commission meeting. The group is calling for a temporary moratorium on new utility-scale solar projects - potentially lasting up to 180 days - to give the county time to study environmental impacts, hire an independent engineering firm, and consider new regulations.”

title: Illinois Court Orders County to Issue Permits for Six Solar Energy Projects
snippet: “A Will County circuit court ordered the county board to issue permits for six solar energy projects in one of the first decisions applying last month’s landmark Equity Solar appellate ruling. Several lawsuits are pending throughout Illinois in which solar energy developers are challenging county boards that have denied siting permits for solar energy facilities… In Channahon McKinley Woods Solar, the Will County court ruled on six consolidated cases brought by solar energy developers against the county. The developers challenged the county’s refusal to issue special use permits for proposed solar facilities despite the proposed facilities meeting all statutory requirements. The court granted summary judgment in all cases, ordering the county to approve ordinances granting special use permits for all plaintiffs by April 17.”

title: Neighbors, Zeeland Township take steps to oppose proposed solar farm in Ottawa County
snippet: “Neighbors in Zeeland and Jamestown townships are intensifying their opposition to a proposed solar farm, taking steps to become formal participants in the state review process. Earlier this month, project developer RWE Americas, a subsidiary of RWE, filed an application with the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) to construct the 200-megawatt Silver Maple Solar Farm, bypassing local permitting efforts. The proposed facility would cover 1,900 acres in Zeeland and Jamestown townships and produce enough energy to power more than 34,000 homes. On Friday, nearly 100 residents gathered at the Ottawa Executive Airport in Zeeland Township for a workshop guiding them through how to petition to intervene in the state’s regulatory process — a step that allows certain parties to submit information and have a voice during hearings.”

title: Iowa Supreme Court says Worth County can limit wind turbine project
snippet: “A $300 million wind project in Worth County received a blow after the Iowa Supreme Court upheld a county zoning ordinance that would reduce the buildable sites for turbines by up to 98%. Worthwhile Wind and Invenergy, its parent company, spent several years and roughly $2.8 million, planning and preparing for the project. It was expected to generate 165 megawatts of electricity.”

title: Renewable ordinance headed to final stages
snippet: “One way or the other, the months-long saga over a proposed renewable energy ordinance for Wapello County may be nearing its conclusion. The board of supervisors set May 5 as the public hearing for the first reading of a revised ordinance that was reworked following a mediation session two months ago between Darren Batterson, Bryan Ziegler and Des Moines attorney Eric Updegraff. The third-floor McGiverin Courtroom was filled just for the date-setting hearing, with a handful of county residents speaking against the Dusty Fox Wind project. Because of strict setbacks contained in the revised ordinance, the Dusty Fox project — despite nearly 20,000 acres of land signed up for wind turbine leases — may be in jeopardy… It takes only one supervisor to vote no for the ordinance to fail, since supervisor Carrie Teninty has abstained due to a potential conflict of interest after her family signed a lease three years ago.”

title: OPPD halts $610M York County solar project amid zoning delays
snippet: “Omaha Public Power District (OPPD) has allowed its Generator Interconnection Agreement for the proposed K-Junction solar project near McCool Junction to expire, pausing the project for now. The utility said the decision was made because of ongoing uncertainty around York County’s proposed solar zoning regulations. The project, which could have delivered up to 310 megawatts of solar power and generated more than $610 million in economic development and $30 million in local tax revenue, cannot move forward until new interconnection milestones are approved. A public hearing on the county’s proposed utility-scale solar zoning regulations is scheduled for April 27 at the York County Courthouse. Residents can attend to learn more or share input on the future of solar development in the area.”

title: Snoqualmie neighbors rally against battery storage facility as permit application looms
snippet: “Hundreds of Snoqualmie residents marched through their neighborhood Sunday — one day before the developer behind a proposed lithium-ion battery storage facility is expected to file a permit application with King County… The proposed 45-acre facility would be located on parcels immediately south of the park. Residents carried signs reading ‘Don't BESS With Us’ — BESS standing for Battery Energy Storage System — and chanted as they marched. Many brought their children, and some of those children took to the podium to address the crowd, echoing their parents' concerns about the project. The protest marked a rapid escalation of community opposition. Residents say they only learned about the project roughly a month ago, despite planning being underway since at least 2023.”

title: Appeal of Bayview Ridge battery energy storage facility set for June
snippet: “An appeal of a Skagit County special-use permit for a battery energy storage system on Bayview Ridge will be heard June 23 in Whatcom County Superior Court. The Stewards of Skagit have filed the appeal over the permit, which was issued in January 2025. In June 2025, the Skagit County Board of Commissioners denied an appeal of the permit by the Stewards of Skagit, setting up the appeal in court under the state's Land Use Petition Act. The Stewards of Skagit cite concerns regarding safety, and an improper approval process by the county hearing examiner. The battery energy storage system, or BESS, proposed by NextEra Energy would be on 22 acres of land on McFarland Road west of Burlington and connect to Puget Sound Energy's nearby Fredonia substation. The system would use lithium-ion batteries capable of storing up to 200 megawatt hours of electricity.”

title: Public hearing regarding solar held By Emery County Commission
snippet: “The Emery County Commission had several requests for new ordinances, resolutions, or at least new requirements for permitting solar companies, so they held a public hearing at 6 p.m. on Tuesday evening. The commission room and adjoining other rooms were full of citizens, and over thirty of them came to the mic to address the commissioners… Various speakers spoke about the environmental impact the solar projects have on the county, i.e., wind storms, dust, and a decrease in rain… Only a couple of speakers spoke about the advantages of solar; most just remarked, “No more solar!””

title: Duke Energy’s proactive grid upgrades under fire from electric co-ops
snippet: “Duke Energy, the state’s predominant utility, is moving to proactively upgrade poles and wires to create room for prospective solar farms. Rather than making improvements pegged to specific projects and then charging solar developers for the full cost, as it did in the past, the company is now building in anticipation of future grid needs and spreading the costs among all customers…But one set of Duke customers isn’t happy. North Carolina’s electric member cooperatives, which buy most of their power wholesale from the utility, filed a complaint with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission in February over four grid projects. They argue that the cost of the upgrades — $57 million, in this case — should not be distributed evenly among all customers. Instead, they want solar developers to pay half the total cost. Many observers believe the protest is on shaky legal ground. Yet FERC is chaired by an appointee of President Donald Trump, who is known to attack renewable energy regardless of the law.”

title: Public hearing slated on requested amendments to solar farm ordinance
snippet: “The Warren County Board of Commissioners will conduct a public hearing at 6 p.m. on Monday, May 4, regarding a petition for several changes to the county’s solar farm ordinance. The hearing will be held at the Warren County Armory Civic Center, located at 501 US Highway 158 Business East, Warrenton. The requested amendments would allow larger-scale solar farm projects with certain conditions that include strict regulations for buffers and decommissioning plans. The local debate on whether the maximum size of permitted solar farms should remain at 150 acres began last year as TED Renewables announced its Horizon Solar proposed to be made up of an east site and a west site.”

title: Solar companies working with county to address concerns
snippet: “At Monday’s regular Dubois County Commissioner meeting, Commissioner Chad Blessigner discussed ongoing conversations with EDP Renewables regarding the second phase of their solar project in the Duff area. The company had come to the county to gauge their willingness to negotiate agreements to begin the process of expanding north of the railroad tracks crossing on County Road 600 West. With the moratorium in place, the county said any approvals would be on pause. The discussions come as the county prepares for the end of its current moratorium on solar developments. EDP is expected to seek approval for its second phase expansion once the moratorium expires in eight months.”

title: NextEra inks a transmission interconnection agreement for an unspecified 200 MW of wind power under development in western Wyoming
snippet: “Months after cancelling its massive 600-megawatt, $729 million Jackalope wind energy project in Sweetwater County, NextEra Energy Resources is advancing a handful of other renewable energy projects in the state…The company recently inked an agreement that secures access to Black Hills Energy’s electric transmission system for 200 megawatts of “wind power” generation still under development on the state’s east side, according to a federal filing…The agreement, which reserves space on Black Hill’s Pumpkin Buttes-Windstar 230 kilovolt line, doesn’t specify a particular NextEra project. However, the Florida-based company operates wind farms in Laramie and Converse counties — both with the potential for expansion. NextEra is also advancing a wind, solar and battery storage proposal in Platte County dubbed the Chugwater Energy Project…PacifiCorp — parent company to Rocky Mountain Power, Wyoming’s largest electric utility — recently wiped new wind and solar from its future plans in the state.”

title: 19 cities across Utah could be on clean energy sources, if their councils pass an ordinance
snippet: “Environmental groups are rallying people in 19 communities across Utah to contact their town and city councils, urging them to pass an ordinance saying they want to be on 100% net renewable energy…The Public Service Commission, the entity that regulates electricity rates in Utah, approved the Community Clean Energy Program last month. But cities across the state that signed up to participate years ago must pass an ordinance by June 2 indicating they're still in…The program has the communities partner with Rocky Mountain Power to ensure that the electricity they take from the grid comes from renewable energy sources like solar and wind by 2030. The 19 communities represent as much as 25% of Rocky Mountain Power's electricity in Utah…[C]ouncils across the state will need to make the deadline. Some will consider the ordinance in the next week or so. HEAL Utah and other groups are urging people to reach out to their elected officials and say they want it.”

title: Judge blocks GE Vernova exit from Vineyard
snippet: “A Massachusetts judge has blocked GE Vernova from abandoning work on the 806MW Vineyard Wind offshore project off the US north-east coast. Suffolk County Superior Court Judge Peter Krupp issued a preliminary injunction preventing a GE Vernova unit from terminating its contract and ceasing work. Krupp wrote that the project ‘is at a critical phase and the loss of VW's principal contractor would set the project back immeasurably and threaten VW's financing.”’

title: Tenaska, TVA sign agreement for 225 MW battery project in Tennessee

title: Kansas county weighs moratorium on solar development
snippet: “The Jackson County, Kansas Planning Commission met at the county courthouse to deliberate on a recommendation for a formal moratorium of large-scale solar development. If approved by the Board of County Commissioners, the pause would effectively freeze new solar project applications while officials finalize a permanent regulatory framework. The process that has remained in legislative flux since December 2024. The move follows years of escalating debate over the Jeffrey Solar project, a 5,000-acre, 500 MW installation proposed by NextEra Energy Resources.”

title: As the state tries to streamline battery and solar permitting, some residents wary of projects

title: Council revisiting postponed solar ordinance
snippet: “Davenport City Council is again considering how to regulate solar energy projects in the city. A moratorium on new utility-scale and free-standing solar projects in Davenport is expiring in June. In preparation, the city council is revisiting a more restrictive solar ordinance that council members decided to postpone in December to give more time for a new west Davenport land-use study to be done.”

title: Elbert County effort to block Xcel’s $1.7B Power Pathway upended by the PUC
snippet: “State utility regulators have overruled local land use decisions and cleared the way for Xcel Energy to build its $1.7 billion Power Pathway transmission line through Elbert County. The line will bring Eastern Plains wind and solar to the Front Range. The Elbert County Commission voted in June 2025 to deny Xcel Energy two key permits responding to protests by landowners and ranchers and an unwillingness by the utility to reroute the path, which cut through the heart of the county.”

title: Gainesville extends solar, battery moratorium
snippet: “The Gainesville Town Board voted Monday evening to extend its moratorium on new solar energy and battery storage facilities. The unanimous vote adds six months to a moratorium that had been in place for six months previously, while members of the town planning board work to draft a permanent law regulating the facilities. While details of the permanent law aren’t yet clear, Town Supervisor David Granger said Wednesday that the planning board is reviewing several other towns’ laws, as well as an example law provided by Wyoming County.”

title: Yes, Utica can extend moratorium on solar, wind farms
snippet: “Springfield adopted a law that prevents municipalities from halting green-energy projects. A lawyer for Utica said Monday it doesn’t apply to the village. As previously reported, the Utica Village Board voted 5-0 (Trustee Pete Pawlak was absent) on Thursday, April 9, to extend its moratorium on issuing permits for solar energy and wind farm systems inside village limits. Village officials extended the moratorium, which does not apply to residential solar panels, to see what new regulations come out of the current legislative session.”

title: Sparked by AES battery development, residents ask Huntingburg for development moratorium
snippet: “Huntingburg resident Amanda Souders Harris urged the common council to enact a temporary moratorium on new development within the city’s extraterritorial jurisdiction (ETJ) until the Unified Development Ordinance can be updated to address large-scale projects that current regulations weren’t designed to handle… The request comes as the city grapples with an ongoing solar project by AES that includes a battery energy storage system. Part of the project is located within the city’s extraterritorial jurisdiction, but not the batteries… City Attorney Phil Schneider explained that moratoriums require going through the UDO amendment process, including referral to the planning commission, public hearings and passing an ordinance. State law limits moratoriums to one year, and the city would need to hire consultants to draft development plans or restrictions during that time.”

title: Glen Cove City Council approves one-year extension for battery energy storage system moratorium
snippet: “The Glen Cove City Council approved an additional 12-month moratorium on battery energy storage systems and promoted two police officers at its Tuesday, April 14, meeting. The council unanimously approved a ban on any application for a battery energy storage system, which the city defines as a facility that holds electrochemical devices that charge or collect energy from a power grid and then discharge that energy to parcels, facilities, dwellings, or utilities serviced by that grid.”

title: Georgia Power will now let data centers bring their own clean energy
snippet: “After years of negotiations, data centers and other large customers of Georgia Power have finally won a pathway to pay for their own new clean energy projects to be built and connected to the utility’s grid. title: Solar regulation showdown
snippet: “In 2021, the county approved zoning requirements – including those for decommissioning, setbacks and others for agriculturally zoned land – for solar projects of 10 acres or more. Commissioners since have questioned whether those requirements contemplated projects that were more than 100 times bigger. In June 2025, county commissioners imposed a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar proposals to allow a review of zoning codes. That moratorium ends June 2… The Area Plan Commission will consider recommendations for rewritten zoning codes for solar energy systems at 6 p.m. Wednesday, April 15, at the County Office Building, 20 N. Third St. in Lafayette. (The discussion will follow an agenda of other subdivision and rezoning requests.) Recommendations from the APC would go to the county commissioners for a final vote as soon as May 4.”

title: Texas sharpens attacks on solar power
snippet: “Texas lawmakers and regulators have unleashed blistering new attacks against solar this month, setting up a showdown that could curb renewable energy growth. State senators grilled solar advocates over whether Chinese-made solar panels could give the Communist Party of China the ability to meddle with Texas’s isolated electric grid. The Public Utility Commission of Texas sent a solar and battery project back to a state administrative court, telling El Paso Electric it should consider natural gas or other traditional generation. And state Attorney General Ken Paxton (R) said his office filed civil investigative demands with four companies as part of a new initiative to investigate “widespread” fraud among rooftop solar companies.”

title: Commissioners Extend Solar Farm, Wind Turbine, and Nuclear Energy Moratorium
snippet: “[Kearney] County Attorney Melodie Bellamy asked the board to extend the solar farm, wind turbine, and nuclear energy moratorium. The County Planning and Zoning Commis-sion has been working on establishing regulations for these alternative energy sources, but it is a bigger project than originally anticipated. After an initial meeting to gather information and a public hearing to gather public input, Planning and Zoning met on April 1 to discuss proposed regulations. The meeting was lengthy as the board worked to create regulations in the best interest of all. They will meet again to consider the regulations with all proposed changes before the regulations are presented to the commis-sioners for final approval. The board adopted a resolution to extend the moratorium for six months.”

title: Kansas county weighs moratorium on solar development
snippet: “The Jackson County, Kansas Planning Commission met at the county courthouse to deliberate on a recommendation for a formal moratorium of large-scale solar development. If approved by the Board of County Commissioners, the pause would effectively freeze new solar project applications while officials finalize a permanent regulatory framework. The process that has remained in legislative flux since December 2024. The move follows years of escalating debate over the Jeffrey Solar project, a 5,000-acre, 500 MW installation proposed by NextEra Energy Resources.”

title: Wyoming electric utility dumps wind and solar in long-term planning
snippet: “Rocky Mountain Power’s parent company, PacifiCorp, updated its long-range planning document in March, shifting its previous upward trajectory for new wind and solar throughout its six-state service region to flatline status. The electric utility has no plans to add more wind or solar facilities in Wyoming, Utah, Idaho and California from 2027 through 2045. ‘Changes in this update are largely driven by the July 4, 2025, repeal of major portions of the Inflation Reduction Act.’”

title: Oshtemo Township hears from experts on BESS ordinance options under state law
snippet: Oshtemo Township (Kalamazoo County) held a meeting with experts from EGLE, MPSC and the University of Michigan to discuss drafting a local ordinance for battery energy storage systems (BESS) under Michigan's Public Act 233. A proposed BESS by NewEdge Renewable Power near Van Kal Avenue on the Oshtemo-Almena border triggered a one-year moratorium (approved end of 2025 and potentially extendable). All seven township officials and over 350 families oppose the project; more than 1,500 residents are in the "Say No to Oshtemo BESS" Facebook group with over 1,000 petition signatures. Resident Katie Schneider stated, "I'm a business owner, a farmer and a parent here in Oshtemo Township. I can tell you there are over 350 families with signs in their yard against this project." Experts noted fire/explosion risk but said lithium-ion batteries are "getting increasingly safe without injury" and that a "workable ordinance" must sit in the "sweet spot" above the state minimum to prevent developers from bypassing to the MPSC. No vote was taken; the planning commission will continue drafting with multiple future public-comment meetings.

title: Proposed wind turbines causing a storm in rural AR
snippet: A proposed 94-turbine wind farm in Crittenden County has sparked strong resident opposition ahead of a possible final vote this month on the first phase. The project would bring an estimated $26 million over 15 years, mostly to school districts like Earle, but residents cite lack of notice, property-value impacts, noise, fire-safety concerns (local departments lack equipment for turbine fires), and long-term effects over 30–40 years. Resident Carrie Fraley said, "These are rights that we have where we live, where we're raising our family, where we want to retire, and I'm concerned about the property value for everyone out here." Volunteer firefighter Scott Fraley added, "The big issue is… our little fire department does not have the means to barely put out a grass fire." Residents are circulating a petition for a moratorium and independent study; the Quorum Court is hiring a consultant on setbacks and impacts. The developer's attorney noted the study towers have operated for 10 years with "no incidents."

title: Solar project moratorium may be coming in Jackson County
snippet: Jackson County leaders are considering a moratorium on solar projects after years of discussion around NextEra Energy Resources' proposed 500 MW Jeffrey Solar project west of Holton that could use up to 2,000 acres. The project is projected to generate $136 million in new tax revenue over its lifespan, including $24.5 million in the first decade. NextEra representative Laura Gies stated, "Jeffrey Solar will contribute approximately $24.5 million to the area in the first decade of operations and is projected to generate a total of $136 million in new tax revenue over its lifespan. This funding can help support schools, roads and other vital public services." The Jackson County Planning Commission was scheduled to discuss and possibly recommend the moratorium at a public meeting on April 9, 2026 (7–8 p.m. at Memorial Hall in Holton).

title: Tiny Boulevard Town Goes Big, Demands $7M And Safety Overhaul From Starlight Solar
snippet: The tiny town of Boulevard (San Diego County, pop. ~400) is demanding a $7 million community benefits agreement and major safety changes from Starlight Solar's proposed project on 588 acres along Jewel Valley Road. The development would pair up to 100 MW of solar with a 217.4 MW battery energy storage system. Residents and neighborhood groups want the battery component moved farther back, a second evacuation route, detailed fire plans, water-use assurances, larger setbacks, and funding to refurbish the Backcountry Resource Center and build a playground. They cite precedent from the Jacumba Valley Ranch project ($4 million community fund). The project faces county Planning Commission review on May 8; a town hall is scheduled for April 16.

title: TVA CEO Don Moul to retire July 1
snippet: Tennessee Valley Authority CEO Don Moul, who has served as TVA's fourth CEO since April 9, 2025, plans to retire July 1, informing the board of his decision on April 3. TVA is the nation's largest public utility and supplies power to the Glasgow Electric Plant Board. "The Board appreciates Don's service to TVA, its employees and the people of the Tennessee Valley region," Director Mitch Graves said. "Under his leadership, TVA has had strong operational and financial performance delivering reliable, affordable, American energy that helps communities across our seven states prosper." Moul's decision comes after President Donald Trump publicly criticized him and vowed to make his life "miserable." Trump has appointed four of the six board members who will pick the next CEO. In 2025, Moul drew roughly $6 million in direct compensation, but Trump is pressing the board to cap CEO pay at $500,000.

title: Solar power company appeals to south Alabama residents
snippet: 'We double crop': About 200 people attended a town hall Wednesday night at the civic center in Bay Minette where Silicon Ranch representatives addressed concerns about the Nashville-based company's proposed 4,500-acre solar farm in Stockton, with tensions boiling over late in the meeting into a verbal altercation that resulted in one woman being escorted out. The solar panels are expected to generate 260 megawatts and be operational by the end of 2028, and Silicon Ranch has a power purchase agreement with Alabama Power to offset energy demand from Meta's forthcoming $1.5 billion data center in Montgomery. "Your bill is not going up because of solar," said Reagan Farr, CEO of Silicon Ranch. "We're not sneaking around." Silicon Ranch officials said 2,500 acres of the site — largely wetlands and bordering areas — will be conserved, while solar panels on the remaining 2,000 acres will host a flock of sheep. "We double crop," Farr said. "We harvest sunlight to produce energy, and we sell grass-fed meat…It basically runs as an agricultural operation after it's built." About 75 residents protested outside the civic center before the event, joined by former U.S. Rep. Jerry Carl, who is running for Alabama's first congressional district. The article also notes developers are looking to build a nearly 3,000-acre solar farm further north in Baldwin County by Tensaw Solar, LLC.

title: Heelstone Renewable starts construction on 206MW US solar projects
snippet: Heelstone Renewable Energy, a Qualitas Energy company, has started construction on two solar photovoltaic projects in the US with a combined capacity of 206MW, supported by long-term corporate power purchase agreements with a US hyperscale data centre developer. The 104MW Alligator Creek Solar facility is located in Wheeler County, Georgia, and the 102MW Murch Solar project is in Van Buren County, Michigan. Heelstone expects both plants to reach commercial operation by the end of 2026. Alligator Creek reached financial close in December 2025 and Murch closed in March 2026. Heelstone appointed Pure Power Contractors for Alligator Creek and Greensol Renewables for Murch. "Backed by long-term PPAs and fully structured project financing, these projects reflect our ability to originate, finance and build high-quality utility-scale renewable assets," said Heelstone Renewable Energy CEO Mike Weich. "The start of construction on Alligator Creek and Murch marks another important step in scaling our platform across key US markets." The milestones follow Heelstone's recent completion of a $200m senior secured corporate credit facility.

Heelstone expects both plants to reach commercial operation by the end of 2026. Alligator Creek reached financial close in December 2025 and Murch closed in March 2026. Heelstone appointed Pure Power Contractors for Alligator Creek and Greensol Renewables for Murch. "Backed by long-term PPAs and fully structured project financing, these projects reflect our ability to originate, finance and build high-quality utility-scale renewable assets," said Heelstone Renewable Energy CEO Mike Weich. "The start of construction on Alligator Creek and Murch marks another important step in scaling our platform across key US markets." The milestones follow Heelstone's recent completion of a $200m senior secured corporate credit facility. title: Xcel Minnesota is building a first-of-its-kind virtual power plant
snippet: The Minnesota Public Utilities Commission last week approved Xcel Energy's plan to become the first utility in the nation to build and operate its own virtual power plant. Under the new program, known as Capacity*Connect, Xcel will spend up to $430 million to deploy up to 200 megawatts of batteries, in 1-megawatt to 3-megawatt increments, over the next two years. Almost every other virtual power plant program in the U.S. is organized around third-party companies — like solar and battery vendors or specialized "aggregators" — that tap into energy resources installed and owned by customers. Clean energy advocates, solar industry groups, and state agencies had raised concerns that Xcel's utility-owned model would crowd out competition. Shannon Anderson of Solar United Neighbors said: "The Minnesota story is part of a national trend… There are a number of filings coming, a lot of paperwork to evaluate and justify the program. This is just the beginning of the conversation from our perspective."

title: AES pulls out of San Diego area battery project after local opposition
snippet: AES Corp. withdrew its application to develop the Seguro battery system in Escondido, 30 miles from San Diego, after a barrage of local opposition. The company had intended to fill a former horse ranch with 320 megawatts of battery containers, which would have been one of the most powerful stand-alone energy storage facilities in the country and would have strengthened the Southern California grid late in the day when solar generation fades and home consumption surges. Residents decried adding large-scale power infrastructure so close to homes and 1,600 feet from Palomar Medical Center Escondido, especially given the multiday fires at other large batteries in the state. AES needed the hospital to sign off on running high-voltage wires through its property, which the hospital board voted down in 2024. AES said in a statement it would prioritize other development efforts but remained "committed to advancing projects that can provide the safe, reliable, and affordable power needed to strengthen the region's electric grid and generate meaningful economic benefits locally." JP Theberge, a board member of the Elfin Forest Harmony Grove Town Council who rallied resistance to the battery, framed his neighbors as a victorious David smiting a corporate Goliath. "The forces of greed are very powerful in this world. The only way to stop them is to be united and determined and forceful," he wrote in a post on the Stop Seguro Facebook group. "This was a great win and we should all be proud of our efforts."

title: New York State clears Cordelio's plan for 300-MW solar project
snippet: New York's Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission has issued a final siting permit to Cordelio Power for the Flat Creek Solar Project, an up to 300 MW facility spanning roughly 1,000 acres in the Towns of Canajoharie and Root in Montgomery County. Cordelio plans to build the project in two phases — an initial 200 MW followed by another 100 MW — with startup targeted for late 2028 or early 2029. Flat Creek is expected to generate approximately $10 million in direct payments to the host towns over the first 20 years, $16.6 million to the Canajoharie Central School District, and $9.8 million to Montgomery County. ORES issued the Cordelio approval alongside a separate siting permit for Liberty Renewables' 99 MW Agricola wind project in Cayuga County.
