title: Montanans want more solar, natural gas development, are less interested in new coal plants
snippet: “The results of the poll, conducted in late December and early January, come as the Trump and Gianforte administrations work to expand domestic energy production amid a surge in demand wrought by growth in artificial intelligence and other electricity-intensive industries…Gov. Greg Gianforte and Montana’s federal delegation, all Republicans, are aligned with President Donald Trump in backing coal, which has fueled Montana’s largest power plant for more than 40 years. Jon Tester, the last Democrat to hold a statewide office, supported both renewable and fossil fuel energy sources during his tenure at the U.S. Senate, occasionally countering the Biden administration’s efforts to tighten emission regulations and restrict new federal coal leasing.”

title: California orders utilities to add 6 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2032

title: Community forum focuses on future of nuclear power in Minnesota – Brainerd Dispatch
snippet: “Currently, there’s a moratorium on building new nuclear power plants in Minnesota. But to meet future power demands, experts say that rule should be reconsidered. Nuclear power was the subject of a Monday, Feb. 23, Rosenmeier Forum at Central Lakes College in Brainerd…Currently, Minnesota has three nuclear power plants, all owned by Xcel Energy — one in Monticello and two at Prairie Island near Red Wing…Blaine asked whether the mandate to be 100% clean energy by 2040, signed into law by Gov. Tim Walz in February of 2023, would be possible without nuclear energy. Hult said it was theoretically possible but in reality it would be a stretch to reach that goal.”

title: Is the future of Puente Hills Mall in batteries and data?

title: Protect McKean County from RWE’s 480 MW Stargazer Solar & Transmission Project
snippet: Residents and landowners launched a Change.org petition on February 25 demanding an immediate halt to RWE Solar Development LLC’s proposed 480 MW utility-scale solar project and associated transmission lines across Keating, Hamlin, Norwich and Sergeant Townships in McKean County. The petition states, “We, the residents, landowners, camp owners, sportsmen, business owners, and families who cherish McKean County, Pennsylvania, demand that the RWE Stargazer Solar Project be stopped immediately.” It warns of permanent conversion of hardwood forests, trout streams, dark skies and rural character near the Allegheny National Forest, noting RWE has already secured land options, leases and easements since 2024. Petitioners add, “We are not asking for adjustments. We are not asking for mitigation. We are demanding that this project be stopped.”

title: Dearborn County approves one-year moratorium on solar farms, battery storage and data centers
snippet: After a packed public meeting that lasted several hours at South Dearborn High School, Dearborn County Commissioners approved a 12-month moratorium on all new applications for solar farms, battery storage facilities and data centers. The decision follows Linea Energy’s (San Francisco) proposal for a large solar + battery storage project on 1,000 acres in the Manchester area, which triggered anti-solar signs across the county and neighboring areas. No applications can be submitted during the ban.

title: Gigantic Form Energy iron-air battery to power Google data center in Minnesota
snippet: Form Energy will build a 300 MW / 30 GWh iron-air battery system in Pine Island, Minnesota, under a deal with Xcel Energy to deliver round-the-clock clean power to a Google data center, with the ability to dispatch for up to 100 hours straight. Google is funding 1.4 GW of new wind and 200 MW of solar to charge the batteries and covering all new grid infrastructure costs. Form Energy CEO Mateo Jaramillo said, “They don’t treat us like mom and give us cookies when we feel bad — they hold us to a very high standard.” Batteries are expected to start delivery in 2028, with full clean-energy installations phasing in through 2031.

title: Utility-scale battery energy storage facility planned in Hayden
snippet: Jupiter Power is developing a 400 MW standalone battery energy storage system on a 72-acre site (30–40 acre battery footprint) on Routt County Road 51 southwest of the Hayden Power Station. The project, approved by the Colorado PUC last week as part of 10 bid projects, will sell power to Xcel Energy under a power purchase agreement and connect via a new transmission line to the Mount Harris substation. Hayden Town Manager Mathew Mendisco said, “Development like this is always good… We are excited to have land near the airport developed, and we think this seems to be a good fit.” Construction is expected to start in 2027 with commercial operations targeted for 2028.

title: Solar power taking only tiny fraction of Colorado farmland despite fears, industry study shows
snippet: A new COSSA industry study shows all ground-mounted solar projects covered just 14,326 acres across 39 counties at the end of 2024 (utility-scale ≥1 MW used 12,601 acres), less than 1% of the state’s 30.2 million farmland acres; another 6,700 acres were added in 2025. COSSA CEO KC Becker stated, “Farmland isn’t being lost to renewable energy, it’s being lost to suburban sprawl,” and noted that even the 60,000–80,000 acres needed for 12,000 MW of large-scale solar by 2050 would equal only about one-tenth of one percent of all land in Colorado. Examples include an 80 MW array on 475 acres in Delta County (later reworked for sheep grazing) and a 100 MW project on 640 acres in San Miguel County that was abandoned due to opposition.

title: Massive solar project proposed where crops once grew
snippet: NextEra Energy has proposed the Spud Valley Energy Center — a 600 MW solar + 600 MW battery storage project on 2,578 acres of former cropland near Mosca and Hooper in Alamosa County. Project manager Evan Reimondo said a number of participating landowners “have already either retired their wells or they’re participating in CREP to rest their lands,” and added, “Colorado’s demand for electricity is going to keep growing… By permitting for 600, it gives us that future flexibility.” The initial 200 MW / 200 MW phase targets operation by the end of 2029, with full buildout later as transmission improves; the county is now reviewing the 1041 permit application.

title: Huron County residents rally against solar farms and data centers
snippet: “As Huron County officials weigh zoning changes and potential proposals for renewable energy developments on farmland, a small but passionate group of agriculture-minded residents has stepped forward…When asked to rank which projects the group is most concerned about, they said solar farms and data centers worry them most because of land footprint and the potential for significant water demand to cool the data centers…One of the group’s main concerns is what happens if farmland continues to disappear…During the Feb. 4, 2026…the Planning Commission voted to recommend two amendments to the Huron County Zoning Ordinance following a lengthy public hearing.The amendments were scheduled to go to the county board of commissioners for review and a vote Feb. 24.”

title: New moratorium on BESS development advances in Newfane
snippet: “The Newfane town board is moving to call another six-month moratorium on local siting of battery energy storage systems (BESS)...A public hearing on the proposed local law declaring the moratorium will be held at 6:45 p.m. Feb. 25, next Wednesday, ahead of the board’s 7 p.m. business meeting at the town hall. The board imposed a six-month hold on local BESS development in March 2025 and did not extend it, nor was a local law drafted to regulate BESS facilities. The new moratorium comes after feedback from the town’s fire board, and as three town-approved, 5mW solar energy generation projects move to the construction stage and a fourth project is pending.”

title: Board delays vote on Pontoon Beach solar farm
snippet: The Madison County Board voted Wednesday night to postpone action on a permit for Arena Renewables’ proposed 3-megawatt solar farm along Route 162 in Pontoon Beach. The zoning board of appeals had recommended denial; concerns included possible disturbance of Native-American artifacts. Developer Sam Walch highlighted the project’s financial benefits while resident Jim Greenwald voiced neighborhood worries.

title: Stutsman County Commission approves solar farm ordinance
snippet: On Feb. 17 the Stutsman County Commission approved zoning ordinance amendments for solar energy facilities by a 4-1 vote, setting a 1,320-foot setback from occupied residences (down from the proposed half-mile). The changes pave the way for New Leaf Energy’s 247-megawatt Buffalo solar project on 1,600 acres north of Jamestown (construction possible summer 2028). Commissioner Levi Taylor said, “I’m not so much concerned about the noise… as much as what it looks like.” Developer William Dean called a half-mile setback “overly prescriptive” and “a denial” of the project.

title: Grant Co. solar projects moving forward
snippet: Multiple solar projects are advancing in Grant County, including the 400-MW Dry Falls Solar near Coulee City (conditional use permit issued, negotiating BPA contract) and the 300-MW Appledale Solar northwest of Quincy (conditional use permit received). Planning Director Jim Anderson-Cook noted “overwhelming support at the public hearing” for one project, adding support from local labor unions. Royal Slope Solar (260 MW) is targeting an April hearing.

title: Denial of special permit for battery storage facility reversed
snippet: Superior Court Judge Jeffrey A. Lanphear vacated the Smithfield zoning board’s denial of a special use permit for a battery energy storage facility off Mountaindale Road. GDM ESS, LLC filed its master plan application Nov. 22, 2023 (certified complete Dec. 17, 2023) before the town’s Feb. 9, 2024 ordinance amendment prohibiting energy storage. The judge stated, “The Master Plan Application is unmistakably an application for development... This entitled the project to the protections of §45-24-44.” The board must now consider the permit under the ordinances effective Dec. 17, 2023.

title: 870-acre solar farm proposed in Ingham County
snippet: A 90-megawatt, $100 million Acceleration Solar Project on 873 acres across Leslie, Vevay and Onondaga townships in Ingham County is under review by the Michigan Public Service Commission. Leslie Township Supervisor Dallas Henney stated, “We can’t do much to stop it. To be honest with you, there’s not a lot you can do, you just try to work with them.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss said, “Once operational, the project will produce enough energy to power an estimated 20,000 homes.” Resident Amy Clark wrote to the MPSC, “I know the majority of the community absolutely does not want these industrial solar sites taking over the farmland in our community,” citing property values, drainage, fire hazards and wildlife. Construction is set for 2027 with operations in 2028; the project is expected to generate $25 million in tax revenue.

title: Starke County approves new moratoriums on solar projects, battery energy storage systems
snippet: Starke County Commissioners unanimously approved new moratoriums on solar projects and battery energy storage systems through July 25. A plan commission representative stated they have been “actively hashing out language for future solar projects but still need more time and expect to have everything finalized in May,” at which point the moratorium would lift for updated zoning ordinances; the prior moratoriums were set to expire this week.

title: Iowa bill seeks to end constantly blinking red lights on wind turbines
snippet: A House subcommittee advanced House File 2081 requiring wind turbines to use aircraft detection lighting systems instead of constantly blinking red lights. Rep. Dean Fisher, R-Montour, stated, “Constituents who used to have just a nice sunset view out their window now are looking at a row of flashing red lights all night long. Very annoying, very disturbing.” The bill would mandate new wind farms install sensor-based lights and existing turbines retrofit by 2028. About 27% of Iowa’s turbines already have the systems; the Sierra Club and Iowa Environmental Council support the measure with no opposition registered.

title: Neighbors pack hearing over proposed battery storage site in north Greene County
snippet: Neighbors packed a hearing over a proposed battery energy storage facility on 40 acres in north Greene County near Farm Road 84 and Rocky Lane, featuring 242 battery containers covering 16.5 acres. Opponent Gayle Emery stated, “A fire event of this type could put this company completely bankrupt immediately... We will be left holding the bag for maintaining and getting rid of this thing and cleaning up.” Tracy Ojakangas added, “We would be responsible for most of the things that will happen in the fire department... So, our taxes will go up probably … And also our insurance would go up because we would have to insure our property and our buildings.” Developer Sofos Power pledged collaboration with local fire departments on prevention plans and highlighted millions in tax revenue and jobs. The Greene County Planning Board will make the final decision.

title: Clearway energises USD-685m solar-storage complex in Texas
snippet: “Clearway Energy Group brought online the Pine Forest Energy Center in Hopkins County, a combined 300 MW solar project paired with 200 MW of battery storage. The company said the project totaled a $685 million investment and is expected to generate ‘over USD 58 million in property tax revenue’ over the next 20 years. The article notes the solar output is contracted via virtual power purchase agreements, and the standalone BESS is intended to ‘enhance grid reliability’ by shifting energy to higher-demand periods.”

title: South Dakota just approved its largest wind farm ever
snippet: The South Dakota Public Utilities Commission approved its largest wind farm ever — the 333-MW Philip Wind Project in Haakon County — on Feb. 12 via settlement agreement. The project includes up to 87 turbines at a $750 million cost, a 5.5-mile 230-kV transmission line and other infrastructure; construction could start as soon as June 2026. PUC vice chairperson Kristie Fiegen said, “That meeting gave us an opportunity to get additional information, make changes, and develop conditions that have made this project better.” Commission chair Chris Nelson stated, “The developer met the legal requirements for approval.”

title: Clymer extends battery storage moratorium
snippet: “The Clymer Town Board voted unanimously to push its battery‑storage moratorium out another year, to Jan. 31 2027, saying they’re waiting for state guidance and don’t want to permit large battery projects without clear rules. Supervisor Brian Willink admitted they’re ‘basically kicking it another 12 months down the road,’ but board members noted there’s nothing stopping them from renewing the moratorium annually . The extension means any battery‑storage proposals will remain on hold until at least early 2027 while officials study regulations and monitor state actions .”

title: Ulster Town hearing on Terra‑Gen battery storage plan to include adjacent Central Hudson land
snippet: “Ulster officials have scheduled a scoping hearing for Terra‑Gen’s proposed 250‑MW lithium‑ion battery energy storage facility at the former Coleman High School. The hearing will also consider adding a strip of land owned by utility Central Hudson to the site, meaning the project could cover roughly 15 acres with ‘a 250‑megawatt lithium‑ion battery energy storage system’ on 12 of those acres. The scoping session is the first step in the state environmental review; residents and agencies can suggest issues for the forthcoming draft environmental impact statement, and the project will need further town approvals.”

title: Fayette Township begins review process for proposed solar ordinance
snippet: “After months of protests over the 1,300‑acre Heartwood II solar project, the Fayette Township Board voted to send its Compatible Renewable Energy Ordinance to the planning commission. Resident Stephen Oleszkowicz called the unanimous vote ‘a change in tide’ because the ordinance would let the township set siting and safety rules instead of deferring to the state . The commission will hold a public hearing, incorporate community feedback and then return the ordinance for adoption; without a local ordinance, developer Ranger Power could bypass the township and seek direct state approval .”

title: Amid resolute opposition to new solar farm, township makes move to retain local control
snippet: “About 100 residents packed a Feb. 16 meeting in Fayette Township to oppose the 140‑MW Heartwood Solar II project, which would put panels on roughly 1,000 acres . The board voted to ask the planning commission to review a compatible renewable‑energy ordinance and hold a public hearing ‘as soon as practical’ ; Supervisor Nate Baker recused himself because his father leased land to the developer . Without local rules, Ranger Power can petition the Michigan Public Service Commission for approval under a 2023 state law, so residents say adopting the ordinance quickly is the only way to keep the project under township control .”

title: Will farmers be able to ‘farm the sun’ on agricultural land in Yakima?
snippet: “Yakima County commissioners are considering rules to allow agrivoltaics — solar panels raised high enough to graze livestock or grow crops underneath — on previously unfarmable land . More than a dozen people spoke in favor at a Feb. 15 meeting; Yakima resident Jean Mendoza said agrivoltaics could help the state meet energy needs, noting that ‘whenever we start looking at something new, there’s a trial‑and‑error period’ . Property owner Jacob Jones proposed a 2‑MW project combining solar and sheep grazing, arguing it would improve soil health and give low‑income residents access to community‑solar credits ; commissioners sent the proposal to county attorneys and said the goal is to protect prime farmland while possibly exempting unfarmable plots .”

title: Wyoming moves to pull the plug on Pronghorn and Sidewinder wind projects
snippet: “On Feb. 5 the Wyoming State Land Board voted 3–2 to start a legal process that could cancel leases for the Pronghorn H2 and Sidewinder wind projects on state lands; Secretary of State Chuck Gray, Superintendent Megan Degenfelder and Auditor Kristi Racines supported the motion, while Gov. Mark Gordon and Treasurer Curt Meier dissented . Gray cited a judge’s ruling that the board broke its own rules when approving the Pronghorn lease, and Racines said both leases may be illegal . Developer Paul Martin of Focus Clean Energy said they invested millions and would contest the move but won’t sue yet because the board opted for due process rather than outright cancellation; critics warn a reversal could trigger a multibillion‑dollar breach‑of‑contract claim .”

title: A new Ohio bill could be a de facto statewide ban on solar and wind
snippet: “A bill now working its way through the Republican-controlled Ohio legislature threatens to raise even more barriers for wind power and solar farms. On Tuesday, the Ohio Senate’s Energy Committee held its third hearing on Senate Bill 294… The bill would declare it to be state policy ​“in all cases” for new electricity-generation facilities to ​“employ affordable, reliable, and clean energy sources.” But the bill’s definitions not only veer from common usage in ways that would exclude renewables but also threaten to block wind and solar development altogether.”

snippet: “A House subcommittee advanced a bill Wednesday that would create standard language for counties to adopt pertaining to setbacks, moratoriums, property tax and other siting considerations for renewable electric power generation facilities. title: Northampton Town Board holds tense public hearing for lithium battery facility
snippet: “Tonight at a meeting of the Northampton Town Board they held public comment with over 100 concerned residents and advocates in attendance on a proposed lithium battery storage system. The board agreed unanimously to vote on whether to hold a moratorium in March according to advocate Sara Will-White. The Adirondack Park Agency and Northampton Planning Board had already approved plans to install a lithium battery storage system at 1030 Ny-30 for the developer CarsonPower before the February 10 meeting. In response a public petition by Sara Will-White to oppose the facility gathered over 1,000 signatures as of February 10.”

title: Oregon bill seeks to temporarily fast-track siting of renewable energy projects
snippet: “Oregon lawmakers are considering a bill that would fast-track siting and permitting of renewable energy projects. House Bill 4031 would temporarily give counties power to grant renewable energy permits that are currently issued by the state. title: Sodium Is Cheap, Abundant, and Now Powering Batteries That Could Rival Lithium
snippet: “As demand for electric vehicles and grid storage surges, battery makers are searching for alternatives to lithium that are cheaper and easier to source. New research suggests sodium-ion batteries, which have long been heralded as a promising alternative, may be maturing faster than expected. Lithium-ion batteries dominate the market thanks to their excellent energy density and well-developed supply chains. But lithium prices have been swinging wildly in recent years, and there are concerns about lithium market concentration…This has driven interest in novel chemistries. Sodium is a leading contender due its low price and abundant deposits all over the globe, but performance concerns have held back adoption.”

title: Grid operators making ‘significant progress’ on generator interconnection reform
snippet: AEU “In the last two years, grid operators have made “significant progress” on their generator interconnection queues, partly by implementing reforms mandated by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, according to a report released Tuesday. However, there isn’t yet evidence that interconnection requests are being processed more quickly, according to the Advanced Energy United report. Also, fast-track interconnection policies are causing limited system headroom to be used by resources that are overly utility-affiliated and fossil-heavy compared to average queue resources, AEU said in the report, which was drafted by Grid Strategies and the Brattle Group.”

title: Scientists strapped GPS trackers to seals in the North Sea expecting them to flee the wind farms, but the animals were hunting along the turbines instead, swimming the rows so precisely you could map every foundation from a single seal’s trail
snippet: “I was shocked when I first saw the stunning grid pattern,” Russell said when the University of St Andrews announced the findings, which were published in the journal Current Biology . The seals were not just passing through. They appeared to be working the structures one at a time, swimming the rows like a delivery driver hitting addresses, stopping to forage at certain turbines and moving on…A new foundation being hammered in is a disturbance. A foundation that has been quietly growing mussels for fifteen years is something closer to a reef. “The potential value of these structures as artificial reefs should be considered,” Squire said, especially as governments start writing the rules for tearing them down.”

title: USA’s Largest Renewable Project Comes Online with More Power Than the Hoover Dam
snippet: “The largest renewable energy infrastructure project in US history is fully operational. The SunZia project is expected to generate and deliver more power than the Hoover Dam and be a reliable energy to the western United States for the next 30 years. The approximately 3,650-megawatt (MW) wind project and 550-mile high-voltage direct current (HVDC) transmission line that comprise SunZia generate power in New Mexico to Arizona and deliver it to customers across the western grid. At full capacity, the project can deliver enough energy to power approximately one million American homes annually.”

title: Solar and wind try to navigate Trump’s obstacle course for tax credits
snippet: Solar and wind developers must secure federal tax credits by achieving “safe harbor” status before the July 4, 2026 deadline under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), with projects covering 216-240 GW of solar and 16 GW of onshore wind already qualified. However, these projects must be placed in service within four years or lose eligibility. Developers face additional challenges from a hostile Trump administration, including new “foreign entity of concern” rules, lengthy permitting, grid interconnection backlogs, and a Department of the Interior memo freezing permits for projects on public lands, delaying or canceling over 22 GW of solar and wind. U.S. Rep. Mike Levin told Canary Media, “I think that the OBBBA — the one not-so-beautiful bill — was a direct hit to the cheapest electricity that we have.”

title: AI firms are getting creative as they scramble for energy
snippet: AI companies facing insufficient utility-provided energy are pursuing unconventional power sources for data centers, including jet-engine turbines, floating data centers powered by wave energy, underwater data centers, and space-based solar power beamed to Earth. Crusoe signed a $1.25 billion contract with Boom Supersonic for 29 jet-engine turbines, while Meta and Overview Energy are exploring capturing solar energy in orbit and beaming it via infrared lasers. Elon Musk has also discussed placing data centers in space using SpaceX. These efforts highlight the scale of the energy crunch facing the AI sector.

title: Solar Energy Is Essential (Trump Just Won’t Admit It)
snippet: The US Air Force is actively pursuing space-based solar power to reduce reliance on diesel generators, fuel convoys, and vulnerable supply chains for expeditionary operations. In May, the Air Force awarded a contract to Overview Energy to demonstrate space-based solar systems. The PERSEUS project at Air University is developing mobile receivers for wireless energy transmission from orbit to support distributed operations in the Indo-Pacific. The Air Force stated that space-based solar power “could become a future capability that reduces logistics burdens, supports distributed operations and increases operational tempo for the Air Force and the Joint Force.” Overview Energy anticipates delivering megawatt-level power by 2030 and more than a gigawatt of 24/7 clean energy in the early 2030s.

title: Solar surpasses gas in California after panel installs surge
snippet: Solar power generation in California has surpassed natural gas for the first time on a sustained basis. Utility-scale solar exceeded gas generation during 82% of days through May 2026, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. This marks the first year where average solar generation in the first five months outpaced gas, driven by a surge in panel installations.

title: Judge restores 5% safe harbor rule for wind and solar
snippet: A federal judge vacated Treasury guidance that had eliminated the 5% cost threshold for proving construction start on wind and solar projects. The ruling restores an important pathway for developers to qualify for tax credits ahead of the July 4, 2026 deadline under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

title: Clean energy trends and big picture news Trump administration blocks wind farm development in Illinois and across the nation
snippet: “Billions of dollars in wind farm projects all over Illinois are on hold, including two projects in McLean County, two in Peoria County, and one in Tazewell County…Until last August, getting these [FAA (Federal Aviation Administration) compliance] certificates was routine. The FAA automatically kicks applications to the Department of Defense to see if there is a risk to military readiness, said a source knowledgeable about the process…Mitigation cases stopped getting approved starting last August, said a background source. In late April, even the easy cases stopped receiving certificates. Now, no wind farm project in the nation that didn’t already have permit approval is moving forward, said multiple sources…The American Clean Power Association estimated not allowing the U.S. to construct new wind projects would cost the national electricity grid an extra $361 billion over the next 25 years because of higher prices based on demand.”

But a West Virginia native who made his career as an energy reporter believes the Mountain State can learn from the UAE’s clean energy revolution…According to an informational page on the website of the UAE Embassy in Washington D.C., oil and gas imports make up 30% of the nation’s total economic activity. But over the last 21 years, the UAE has been working toward pairing traditional fossil fuels with alternative energy sources…According to the UAE Energy Strategy 2050, the nation has the goal of raising the percentage of alternative energy in its total energy mix to 30% by 2030, tripling its renewable energy capacity by 14 gigawatts and creating 50,000 new jobs. The end goal is to be carbon neutral by 2050….Despite [West Virginia’s] energy largely being supplied by several coal-fired power plants, coal mining in the state has declined over decades due to a national decrease in coal-fired electric generation. Half of West Virginia’s coal is exported to other countries…The Legislature did pass House Bill 5381, the Comprehensive Energy Policy and Development Plan Act of 2026, which tasks the Office of Energy with creating a long-term strategy to bolster electrical grid stability and energy security through 2050. The legislation is the next step in codifying Gov. Patrick Morrisey’s 50 by 50 plan to increase the state’s electric-generating capacity from 16 gigawatts to at least 50 gigawatts by 2050...Silverstein said if West Virginia wants to reverse the trend in downward population growth and attract news residents and workers to the state, it needs to follow the example of UAE in being able to support its fossil fuel industry and being willing to embrace renewable energy projects and the innovation that will come.” [Wheeling News-Register, 06/08/2026, link to article here ]. Michigan Senate panel mulls financial ‘Catch-22’ for farms pledged federal clean energy funding: “Members of the Michigan Senate continued to interrogate the impact that the administration of President Donald Trump has had on business and energy. Giving testimony on Thursday to the Senate Energy and Environment Committee were individuals harmed by the administration’s move to cancel rural energy grants. Among them were Allen Bonthuis, the vice president of sales and marketing at Jackson-based Harvest Solar, and Kyle de Beausset, a member of the Sierra Club and manager of Westcroft Gardens and Farm. The pair testified on how the Trump administration’s move to cancel funding for renewable energy projects through the Rural Energy for America Program had affected their businesses…’We relied on the government’s commitment. We made plans in good faith, and for us, it’s not easy to absorb that loss, I’ll say that straight up. It’s a $120,000 project, we would have gotten $60,000 back,’ de Beausset said, noting that’s more money than the farm made last year.” [Michigan Advance, 06/05/2026, link to article here ]. In Massachusetts, parked EVs will start feeding the grid this summer: “After the school year ends in the Massachusetts towns of Acton and Boxborough, the district’s electric buses will mostly stay put in a parking lot. But they won’t sit idle all summer. The three vehicles will charge up their nearly 200-kilowatt-hour batteries overnight, when the power supply is at its cleanest and cheapest, then send energy back to the grid from 4 p.m. to 7 p.m. on days when the grid is strained.” [Canary Media, 06/03/2026, link to article here ]. What the NextEra-Dominion merger means for nuclear power in New England: “NextEra Energy, the owner of New Hampshire’s Seabrook Nuclear Power Station, announced plans last month to acquire Dominion Energy, the owner of Connecticut’s Millstone Nuclear Power Station, in a deal worth nearly $67 billion. If approved, the sale would create one of the world’s largest utility companies with control over all of the nuclear power produced in New England. That power accounts for roughly one-quarter of the region’s energy supply, and half of all of its carbon-free power, according to data from ISO New England, the regional grid operator. NextEra also owns solar, battery and natural-gas fired power plants throughout the region.” [AP News, 06/05,2026, link to article here ]. Renewable Energy Industry Groups Challenge DoD Wind Moratorium | Harris Beach Murtha: “A coalition of nine renewable energy trade associations, including the Alliance for Clean Energy New York, recently filed a lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon over recent federal government actions blocking the development of onshore wind energy projects across the United States. The May 31, 2026, lawsuit – Renewable Northwest v. Hegseth – alleges the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) has violated its statutory mandate to assess the national security impacts of wind energy projects, preventing the issuance of necessary permits that must be obtained before construction is allowed to proceed. According to the complaint, these actions have resulted in a complete halt to onshore commercial wind development since approximately August 2025, stalling the permitting of at least 125 utility-scale projects across 25 states…The outcome of Renewable Northwest v. Hegseth will carry major implications for wind energy projects across the United States and their ability to obtain federal permits required to begin construction. More broadly, it will affect the interests of the businesses and investors whose capital, contracts, and financing arrangements depend on a predictable and timely federal approval process.” [BYTSEU, 06/09/2026, link to article here ]. What rural Americans actually want from large-scale solar: “The report, titled “Rural Perspectives on Large Scale Solar,” contains data from nearly 5,200 surveys of rural Americans in 47 states conducted by researchers at the University of California, Santa Barbara…In addition to examining the direct benefits a large-scale solar installation could offer a local community, the researchers examined levels of support for projects based on how they managed the impacts of construction and operation to the local environment…The researchers also discovered that agrivoltaics practices may cause people who are strongly opposed to large-scale solar facilities to change their minds, with data showing that agrivoltaics projects receive a neutral attitude from respondents who said they were strongly opposed to projects that replace farmland.” [PV Magazine, 06/09/2026, link to article here ]. Good news corner Safe Harbor Win for Solar and Wind Projects: Court Vacates IRS Notice That Had Eliminated Beginning-of-Construction 5% Safe Harbor for Wind and Large Solar Facilities: “In Oregon Environmental Council v. IRS, issued on June 6, 2026,1 the US District Court for the District of Columbia held that IRS Notice 2025-42 (the “Notice”)—which eliminated the longstanding 5% safe harbor method for establishing “beginning of construction” for wind and large-scale solar facilities—is arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act, and accordingly the Court vacated the Notice in full and remanded the matter to the IRS for further administrative action…The Court’s decision vacating the Notice removes a major restriction on how wind and large-scale solar facilities establish “beginning of construction” under the relevant provisions of the OBBBA. However, near-term uncertainty remains given the remand to the IRS and potential further administrative or judicial developments. Wind and solar projects must begin construction by July 4, 2026, to qualify for either the Section 45Y production tax credit or the Section 48E investment tax credit without being subject to an additional requirement that the project be placed in service by December 31, 2027.” [Chapman, 06/10/2026, link to article here ]. Solar power hits new milestones in the US even as Trump boosts coal over clean energy: “Data released Wednesday by global energy think tank Ember, along with a report by the Solar Energy Industries Association and analytics firm Wood Mackenzie, show the continued growth of solar and decline of coal in the United States despite federal policy. In May, for the first time, solar supplied more of the nation’s electricity than coal, or 12.8%, Ember said. Coal supplied 12.2%, its fourth-lowest monthly share ever…States won by Trump in the 2024 election accounted for 74% of all solar capacity installed in the first quarter of 2026, with Texas, Florida, Ohio, Indiana, Michigan, Arizona and Mississippi ranking among the top 10 states for new solar additions, SEIA said…’I think there is a misconception in the United States that clean energy is something for the coasts and liberal cities,’ [Johanna Neumann, at the Environment America Research and Policy Center] said. ‘The true story of renewable energy is a 50-state story.’” [AP News, 06/10/2026, link to article here ].

According to the complaint, these actions have resulted in a complete halt to onshore commercial wind development since approximately August 2025, stalling the permitting of at least 125 utility-scale projects across 25 states…The outcome of Renewable Northwest v. Hegseth will carry major implications for wind energy projects across the United States and their ability to obtain federal permits required to begin construction. More broadly, it will affect the interests of the businesses and investors whose capital, contracts, and financing arrangements depend on a predictable and timely federal approval process.” [BYTSEU, 06/09/2026, link to article here ]. What rural Americans actually want from large-scale solar: “The report, titled “Rural Perspectives on Large Scale Solar,” contains data from nearly 5,200 surveys of rural Americans in 47 states conducted by researchers at the University of California, Santa Barbara…In addition to examining the direct benefits a large-scale solar installation could offer a local community, the researchers examined levels of support for projects based on how they managed the impacts of construction and operation to the local environment…The researchers also discovered that agrivoltaics practices may cause people who are strongly opposed to large-scale solar facilities to change their minds, with data showing that agrivoltaics projects receive a neutral attitude from respondents who said they were strongly opposed to projects that replace farmland.” [PV Magazine, 06/09/2026, link to article here ]. Good news corner Safe Harbor Win for Solar and Wind Projects: Court Vacates IRS Notice That Had Eliminated Beginning-of-Construction 5% Safe Harbor for Wind and Large Solar Facilities: “In Oregon Environmental Council v. IRS, issued on June 6, 2026,1 the US District Court for the District of Columbia held that IRS Notice 2025-42 (the “Notice”)—which eliminated the longstanding 5% safe harbor method for establishing “beginning of construction” for wind and large-scale solar facilities—is arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act, and accordingly the Court vacated the Notice in full and remanded the matter to the IRS for further administrative action…The Court’s decision vacating the Notice removes a major restriction on how wind and large-scale solar facilities establish “beginning of construction” under the relevant provisions of the OBBBA. However, near-term uncertainty remains given the remand to the IRS and potential further administrative or judicial developments. Wind and solar projects must begin construction by July 4, 2026, to qualify for either the Section 45Y production tax credit or the Section 48E investment tax credit without being subject to an additional requirement that the project be placed in service by December 31, 2027.” [Chapman, 06/10/2026, link to article here ]. Solar power hits new milestones in the US even as Trump boosts coal over clean energy: “Data released Wednesday by global energy think tank Ember, along with a report by the Solar Energy Industries Association and analytics firm Wood Mackenzie, show the continued growth of solar and decline of coal in the United States despite federal policy. In May, for the first time, solar supplied more of the nation’s electricity than coal, or 12.8%, Ember said. Coal supplied 12.2%, its fourth-lowest monthly share ever…States won by Trump in the 2024 election accounted for 74% of all solar capacity installed in the first quarter of 2026, with Texas, Florida, Ohio, Indiana, Michigan, Arizona and Mississippi ranking among the top 10 states for new solar additions, SEIA said…’I think there is a misconception in the United States that clean energy is something for the coasts and liberal cities,’ [Johanna Neumann, at the Environment America Research and Policy Center] said. ‘The true story of renewable energy is a 50-state story.’” [AP News, 06/10/2026, link to article here ]. title: Trump administration blocks wind farm development in Illinois and across the nation
snippet: “Billions of dollars in wind farm projects all over Illinois are on hold, including two projects in McLean County, two in Peoria County, and one in Tazewell County…Until last August, getting these [FAA (Federal Aviation Administration) compliance] certificates was routine. The FAA automatically kicks applications to the Department of Defense to see if there is a risk to military readiness, said a source knowledgeable about the process…Mitigation cases stopped getting approved starting last August, said a background source. In late April, even the easy cases stopped receiving certificates. Now, no wind farm project in the nation that didn’t already have permit approval is moving forward, said multiple sources…The American Clean Power Association estimated not allowing the U.S. to construct new wind projects would cost the national electricity grid an extra $361 billion over the next 25 years because of higher prices based on demand.”

title: Beyond King Coal
snippet: Can West Virginia Follow the UAE’s Lead into a Green Future?: “West Virginia and the United Arab Emirates have something in common – both are known for their fossil fuels, coal and oil respectively. But a West Virginia native who made his career as an energy reporter believes the Mountain State can learn from the UAE’s clean energy revolution…According to an informational page on the website of the UAE Embassy in Washington D.C., oil and gas imports make up 30% of the nation’s total economic activity. But over the last 21 years, the UAE has been working toward pairing traditional fossil fuels with alternative energy sources…According to the UAE Energy Strategy 2050, the nation has the goal of raising the percentage of alternative energy in its total energy mix to 30% by 2030, tripling its renewable energy capacity by 14 gigawatts and creating 50,000 new jobs. The end goal is to be carbon neutral by 2050….Despite [West Virginia’s] energy largely being supplied by several coal-fired power plants, coal mining in the state has declined over decades due to a national decrease in coal-fired electric generation. Half of West Virginia’s coal is exported to other countries…The Legislature did pass House Bill 5381, the Comprehensive Energy Policy and Development Plan Act of 2026, which tasks the Office of Energy with creating a long-term strategy to bolster electrical grid stability and energy security through 2050. The legislation is the next step in codifying Gov. Patrick Morrisey’s 50 by 50 plan to increase the state’s electric-generating capacity from 16 gigawatts to at least 50 gigawatts by 2050...Silverstein said if West Virginia wants to reverse the trend in downward population growth and attract news residents and workers to the state, it needs to follow the example of UAE in being able to support its fossil fuel industry and being willing to embrace renewable energy projects and the innovation that will come.”

title: Michigan Senate panel mulls financial ‘Catch-22’ for farms pledged federal clean energy funding
snippet: “Members of the Michigan Senate continued to interrogate the impact that the administration of President Donald Trump has had on business and energy. Giving testimony on Thursday to the Senate Energy and Environment Committee were individuals harmed by the administration’s move to cancel rural energy grants. Among them were Allen Bonthuis, the vice president of sales and marketing at Jackson-based Harvest Solar, and Kyle de Beausset, a member of the Sierra Club and manager of Westcroft Gardens and Farm. The pair testified on how the Trump administration’s move to cancel funding for renewable energy projects through the Rural Energy for America Program had affected their businesses…’We relied on the government’s commitment. We made plans in good faith, and for us, it’s not easy to absorb that loss, I’ll say that straight up. It’s a $120,000 project, we would have gotten $60,000 back,’ de Beausset said, noting that’s more money than the farm made last year.”

title: In Massachusetts, parked EVs will start feeding the grid this summer
snippet: “After the school year ends in the Massachusetts towns of Acton and Boxborough, the district’s electric buses will mostly stay put in a parking lot. But they won’t sit idle all summer. The three vehicles will charge up their nearly 200-kilowatt-hour batteries overnight, when the power supply is at its cleanest and cheapest, then send energy back to the grid from 4 p.m. to 7 p.m. on days when the grid is strained.”

title: What the NextEra-Dominion merger means for nuclear power in New England
snippet: “NextEra Energy, the owner of New Hampshire’s Seabrook Nuclear Power Station, announced plans last month to acquire Dominion Energy, the owner of Connecticut’s Millstone Nuclear Power Station, in a deal worth nearly $67 billion. If approved, the sale would create one of the world’s largest utility companies with control over all of the nuclear power produced in New England. That power accounts for roughly one-quarter of the region’s energy supply, and half of all of its carbon-free power, according to data from ISO New England, the regional grid operator. NextEra also owns solar, battery and natural-gas fired power plants throughout the region.”

title: Renewable Energy Industry Groups Challenge DoD Wind Moratorium | Harris Beach Murtha
snippet: “A coalition of nine renewable energy trade associations, including the Alliance for Clean Energy New York, recently filed a lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon over recent federal government actions blocking the development of onshore wind energy projects across the United States. The May 31, 2026, lawsuit – Renewable Northwest v. Hegseth – alleges the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) has violated its statutory mandate to assess the national security impacts of wind energy projects, preventing the issuance of necessary permits that must be obtained before construction is allowed to proceed. According to the complaint, these actions have resulted in a complete halt to onshore commercial wind development since approximately August 2025, stalling the permitting of at least 125 utility-scale projects across 25 states…The outcome of Renewable Northwest v. Hegseth will carry major implications for wind energy projects across the United States and their ability to obtain federal permits required to begin construction. More broadly, it will affect the interests of the businesses and investors whose capital, contracts, and financing arrangements depend on a predictable and timely federal approval process.”

title: What rural Americans actually want from large-scale solar
snippet: “The report, titled “Rural Perspectives on Large Scale Solar,” contains data from nearly 5,200 surveys of rural Americans in 47 states conducted by researchers at the University of California, Santa Barbara…In addition to examining the direct benefits a large-scale solar installation could offer a local community, the researchers examined levels of support for projects based on how they managed the impacts of construction and operation to the local environment…The researchers also discovered that agrivoltaics practices may cause people who are strongly opposed to large-scale solar facilities to change their minds, with data showing that agrivoltaics projects receive a neutral attitude from respondents who said they were strongly opposed to projects that replace farmland.”

title: Safe Harbor Win for Solar and Wind Projects
snippet: Court Vacates IRS Notice That Had Eliminated Beginning-of-Construction 5% Safe Harbor for Wind and Large Solar Facilities: “In Oregon Environmental Council v. IRS, issued on June 6, 2026,1 the US District Court for the District of Columbia held that IRS Notice 2025-42 (the “Notice”)—which eliminated the longstanding 5% safe harbor method for establishing “beginning of construction” for wind and large-scale solar facilities—is arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act, and accordingly the Court vacated the Notice in full and remanded the matter to the IRS for further administrative action…The Court’s decision vacating the Notice removes a major restriction on how wind and large-scale solar facilities establish “beginning of construction” under the relevant provisions of the OBBBA. However, near-term uncertainty remains given the remand to the IRS and potential further administrative or judicial developments. Wind and solar projects must begin construction by July 4, 2026, to qualify for either the Section 45Y production tax credit or the Section 48E investment tax credit without being subject to an additional requirement that the project be placed in service by December 31, 2027.”

title: Solar power hits new milestones in the US even as Trump boosts coal over clean energy
snippet: “Data released Wednesday by global energy think tank Ember, along with a report by the Solar Energy Industries Association and analytics firm Wood Mackenzie, show the continued growth of solar and decline of coal in the United States despite federal policy. In May, for the first time, solar supplied more of the nation’s electricity than coal, or 12.8%, Ember said. Coal supplied 12.2%, its fourth-lowest monthly share ever…States won by Trump in the 2024 election accounted for 74% of all solar capacity installed in the first quarter of 2026, with Texas, Florida, Ohio, Indiana, Michigan, Arizona and Mississippi ranking among the top 10 states for new solar additions, SEIA said…’I think there is a misconception in the United States that clean energy is something for the coasts and liberal cities,’ [Johanna Neumann, at the Environment America Research and Policy Center] said. ‘The true story of renewable energy is a 50-state story.’”

title: Clean energy trends and big picture news Grid Modernization Is Rebuilding the American Power System
snippet: “Decades ago, electricity demand followed predictable patterns…That is no longer the case…Today’s grid has to support electric vehicle charging, battery storage facilities, cloud computing centres, manufacturing expansion, and renewable energy generation all at once. Power demand spikes faster and moves in less predictable ways. The American Society of Civil Engineers recently gave U.S. energy infrastructure a C- grade. One of the biggest changes happening right now is the shift toward underground transmission infrastructure…Underground transmission is expensive. In some cases, it costs several times more than overhead systems. Utilities are still moving forward because long-term reliability is increasingly valued over short-term savings…Utilities are no longer planning only for efficiency. They are planning for resilience…That means stronger substations, upgraded transmission corridors, improved backup systems, and more redundancy throughout the grid.”

The first of these “renewable portfolio standard,” or RPS, laws was likely the one adopted in Iowa in 1983. title: Grid Modernization Is Rebuilding the American Power System
snippet: “Decades ago, electricity demand followed predictable patterns…That is no longer the case…Today’s grid has to support electric vehicle charging, battery storage facilities, cloud computing centres, manufacturing expansion, and renewable energy generation all at once. Power demand spikes faster and moves in less predictable ways. The American Society of Civil Engineers recently gave U.S. energy infrastructure a C- grade. One of the biggest changes happening right now is the shift toward underground transmission infrastructure…Underground transmission is expensive. In some cases, it costs several times more than overhead systems. Utilities are still moving forward because long-term reliability is increasingly valued over short-term savings…Utilities are no longer planning only for efficiency. They are planning for resilience…That means stronger substations, upgraded transmission corridors, improved backup systems, and more redundancy throughout the grid.”

title: 7 states sue to stop Trump’s offshore wind deal with TotalEnergies
snippet: “...TotalEnergies forfeited its lease for a large offshore wind area near New York and New Jersey. In exchange, Interior said it would ​“reimburse” the company for the $795 million it paid in lease fees, money that TotalEnergies promised to put toward fossil fuel projects….New York is joined in the lawsuit by Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, New Jersey, Rhode Island, and Vermont, which claim that the lease cancellation harms the states’ economies, power grids, and climate targets. title: In new attack on solar, lawmakers spread myths about potato farms
snippet: “Is Frito-Lay categorically refusing to buy potatoes grown on farmland that has hosted solar installations? No, the company says. That hasn’t stopped lawmakers in Michigan and Pennsylvania from spreading the false claim about one of the biggest purchasers of potatoes in the country…PepsiCo, which owns Frito-Lay, told Canary Media that the company ​’has not issued blanket guidance to growers that fields with solar installations will not be accepted.’ Nor is there any published evidence that solar farms have a negative impact on potato farming, according to experts consulted for this story…The speculation about solar’s impact on potatoes began a year ago with a statement from the agricultural trade group Potato Growers of Michigan. While the organization recognized the role of renewable energy in Michigan’s future, it didn’t want solar on farmland and cited concerns about food safety…In March 2026, Dennis Iott, the chair of the Potato Growers of Michigan, along with Kelly Turner, executive director of the Michigan Potato Growers Commission, another trade group, repeated the claims at a Michigan House Agriculture Committee hearing...At some point, the general speculation raised by industry lobbyists morphed into specific falsehoods about Frito-Lay, and caught the ear of the lawmakers from Michigan and Pennsylvania.”

title: Ohio Has Blocked a Lot of Wind and Solar. Its Residents Pay the Price

title: Emboldened clean-energy donors eye big campaign spending
snippet: “Money from renewable energy executives helped defeat conservative Rep. Chip Roy’s campaign for Texas attorney general — and now those donors are planning to deploy more cash against foes of wind and solar…The anti-Roy campaign targeted conservative audiences on platforms that appeal to them — like Truth Social and Rumble — with messaging arguing the congressman wasn’t aligned with Trump’s agenda. It did not mention clean energy at all…The group is also considering spending in primaries against Reps. Andy Biggs (R-Ariz.), Ralph Norman (R-S.C.) and Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), but has not made any decisions. Each politician is running to be their state’s governor and opposes clean energy incentives.”

title: Arizona ranks No. 2 in US for new energy storage capacity added this year
snippet: “A report released earlier this month from the Solar Energy Industries Association, or SEIA, along with Benchmark Mineral Intelligence found that 940 megawatt-hours of energy storage was installed in the Grand Canyon State during the first quarter, an amount surpassed only by the 2,696 megawatt-hours added in Texas and slightly ahead of the 936 megawatt-hours added in California. That Q1 addition brings Arizona to a milestone total of more than 20 gigawatt-hours of utility-scale energy storage installed — 20.2 to be exact.”

title: Clean energy trends and big picture news Trump is paying companies to quit offshore wind. These projects could be next.
snippet: “After two companies accepted nearly $2 billion to scrap wind farms along the East and West coasts, industry observers say other leases could soon be on the chopping block… Developers that paid top dollar for their leases — but have yet to reach construction — could have the greatest incentive to follow in the footsteps of Total and Ocean Winds. Such leases are concentrated along the New York, New Jersey and California coasts, according to industry observers interviewed for this story. Leases closest to already-canceled projects, including one early-stage project off the coast of the Carolinas, could also be at risk of cancellation.”

title: DOE opens test facility to help data centers improve grid reliability
snippet: “The U.S. Department of Energy’s National Laboratory of the Rockies (NLR) introduced the new Agora large-load test bed. This first-of-its-kind national capability is designed to help data centers become active participants in grid reliability. Agora is funded by the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Electricity and industry partners. It was designed in close collaboration with industry and utilities to address real-world challenges. It is the only dedicated large-load grid integration test bed across the U.S. national laboratory complex. As such, it replicates the technical complexity of a large-scale data center interconnection.”

title: US annual electricity consumption to grow 55% by 2050
snippet: NEMA “Annual U.S. electricity consumption will grow more than 55% by 2050, “with the steepest growth concentrated in the current decade,” the National Electrical Manufacturers Association said in a forecast published Thursday. The pace of potential growth is quickening as data center expansion plans continue to be announced. NEMA’s analysis is an update to a report it published in April 2025, which forecast 50% consumption growth in the next quarter century. The solution to meeting the new demand lies with grid enhancing technologies, demand response and behind-the-meter resources, the manufacturers group said. Electricity’s overall share of final energy delivered in the U.S. is expected to grow from 18% to 28% by 2050, it said.”

title: PJM may be ‘too big to function’
snippet: FERC Chairman Swett “The PJM Interconnection may be ‘too big to function’ and has an ‘unacceptable’ governance structure, Federal Energy Regulatory Commission Chairman Laura Swett said Tuesday at the grid operator’s annual meeting in Baltimore. PJM, the largest U.S. grid operator, faces ‘a serious legitimacy crisis’ as confidence in its decision-making has ‘completely eroded,’ Swett said. The crisis comes as PJM needs to add generation to its system as soon as possible to serve data centers, according to Swett. ‘This is not a time for weak leadership or to be crippled by fear — this is a time for difficult, history-making decisions that are not for the faint of heart, decisions that may leave many unhappy, decisions that may make or break part of a nation,’ Swett said.”

title: Q1 saw net loss of 5,900 renewable energy manufacturing jobs
snippet: EDF report “Renewable energy manufacturing employment took a hit in the first quarter of 2026, with a total loss of about 8,100 jobs and a net loss of 5,900 jobs, according to an Environmental Defense Fund report. The sector also saw $1.4 billion in cancelled investments, although there was a $1.1 billion net increase in investments overall. The cancellations followed federal actions targeting renewable energy, electric vehicles and building energy efficiency, as well as emissions standards.”

title: [OPINION] The case for clean energy abundance | It’s a good idea — and strikingly different from conventional environmentalism
snippet: “[A]ssembling large parcels of land for utility-scale solar isn’t bad for labor and it’s not bad for business and it’s not bad for household energy costs either. If environmental organizations and their donors and members supported this idea, they could make it happen. And maybe someday they will decide that clean energy abundance is the way forward and they will support ideas like this. That would be great, because a lot of them seem to think they favor clean energy abundance and I do hold out some hope that this is a ‘fake it ’til you make it’ kind of situation. For now, though, assembling large parcels of land and making them available for utility-scale solar is not what environmentalists are about. That’s because, as Soub concedes, the only way to make this work is to cut down a bunch of forest… [C]learly the powers that be in the environmental community — including organizations that say they are very focused on climate change or that (like Rewiring America) try to own the label of abundance or affordability — disagree. Which is fine. What I don’t think is fine is that lots of elected officials who like the slogan ‘clean is cheap’ don’t understand or want to acknowledge that the policy organizations they have picked this talking point up from are mostly not interested in a vision of energy abundance.”

title: America’s load growth moment is a chance to scale distributed energy
snippet: U.S. electricity demand is projected to grow by roughly 128 GW over the next five years, with data centers driving much of the increase. Jigar Shah highlighted that community-scale solar and storage (1–10 MW) can be deployed in months on the distribution system to meet load quickly and cost-effectively. He stated, “The smartest near-term path is to squeeze more value out of the system we already have,” and added, “States that get this right will scale faster, spend less and build a more durable energy market.”

title: Solar funding hits $11.1bn in Q1
snippet: Solar companies raised $11.1 billion across 53 deals in Q1 2026 — a 131% increase year-over-year — alongside more than 18 GW of project acquisitions. Debt financing reached $8.9 billion (up 154% YoY). Mercom Capital Group CEO Raj Prabhu said, “Improved policy clarity and strong demand led to an increase in Solar funding and M&A activity in Q1 2026.”

title: PV module recycling technologies ‘progressing’, says IEA-PVPS
snippet: The latest IEA-PVPS Task 12 report shows significant advances in PV module recycling, with commercial and pilot-scale technologies achieving higher recovery rates and purity for silicon, silver, copper, and aluminum. Recovery rates now reach 98% for silicon (SPR), 95% for copper/silver (9-Tech), and over 90% for semiconductor materials (First Solar). The report notes meaningful progress compared to the 2024 study.

title: Clean energy will help power the FIFA World Cup this summer
snippet: Gillette Stadium in Foxborough, Massachusetts — a 2026 FIFA World Cup venue — will be powered in part by clean energy. The stadium’s 2 MW Bloom Energy fuel cell supplies nearly 50% of its needs and avoids 1,500 metric tons of CO2 annually, while a 1 MW solar array powers much of nearby Patriot Place. The venue also participates in demand-response programs and earned LEED Gold certification in 2024.

title: Trump administration cites national security to halt US wind farm projects
snippet: The Trump administration has halted approvals for roughly 165 onshore wind projects on private lands, with the Pentagon reviewing national-security impacts and radar interference concerns. Developers report canceled meetings and stalled applications since August 2025.

title: The Trump admin is trying to stop state climate lawsuits. It isn’t working
snippet: The Department of Justice has filed multiple suits to block state climate-liability cases against oil companies (including Minnesota, Michigan, and Hawaii) and to invalidate state “climate superfund” laws. Federal judges have already dismissed DOJ actions against Michigan and Hawaii, and several state cases continue to move forward.

title: Congress once shielded gun makers. Now it’s fossil fuel companies’ turn
snippet: Republicans have introduced the “Stop Climate Shakedowns Act of 2026” to grant fossil fuel companies legal immunity from climate-liability lawsuits, modeled after the 2005 Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act. The bill would dismiss pending suits and block future state superfund laws. Sen. Ted Cruz stated it would “protect American jobs, lower energy costs, and defend American energy dominance.”

title: Clean energy trends and big picture news Ohio Grapples with Energy Double Standards Amid Fracking Waste Court Fight
snippet: “Ohio relies heavily on underground injection to dispose of this fluid, operating hundreds of Class II wells across the state. DeepRock Disposal LLC received permits from the Ohio Department of Natural Resources in 2025 for two additional wells in Washington County…Ohio has emerged as one of the nation’s toughest places for wind and solar. A 2021 law, Senate Bill 52, empowered counties and townships to restrict these projects across most of their territories. To date, 37 counties have enacted such bans…This framework does not extend to oil and gas…In November 2025, the Buckeye Environmental Network filed a mandamus action in the Franklin County Court of Appeals against the Department of Natural Resources. The group argued that regulators applied outdated rules, ignoring stricter Class II injection well standards adopted years earlier but not fully implemented…The Franklin County appeals court decision looms large. An approval could greenlight the DeepRock wells and affirm the state’s deference to industry. A reversal might mandate stricter oversight, leveling the field somewhat for cleaner alternatives.”

title: Ohio Grapples with Energy Double Standards Amid Fracking Waste Court Fight
snippet: “Ohio relies heavily on underground injection to dispose of this fluid, operating hundreds of Class II wells across the state. DeepRock Disposal LLC received permits from the Ohio Department of Natural Resources in 2025 for two additional wells in Washington County…Ohio has emerged as one of the nation’s toughest places for wind and solar. A 2021 law, Senate Bill 52, empowered counties and townships to restrict these projects across most of their territories. To date, 37 counties have enacted such bans…This framework does not extend to oil and gas…In November 2025, the Buckeye Environmental Network filed a mandamus action in the Franklin County Court of Appeals against the Department of Natural Resources. The group argued that regulators applied outdated rules, ignoring stricter Class II injection well standards adopted years earlier but not fully implemented…The Franklin County appeals court decision looms large. An approval could greenlight the DeepRock wells and affirm the state’s deference to industry. A reversal might mandate stricter oversight, leveling the field somewhat for cleaner alternatives.”

title: Senate energy chair signals departure from House on $1B cut to Mass Save
snippet: “Sen. Michael Barrett, a Democrat who leads the energy committee, [said] in an interview that the roughly $9 billion in ratepayer savings the House legislation… will not be coming from significant cuts to Mass Save, if Barrett has his way… The program, a collaboration among the utilities with state oversight, funds home weatherization projects and heat pump installations at low or no cost to reduce energy usage and utility bills over the long term… The House voted in February to cut Mass Save by $1 billion, one of the most controversial components of the sprawling energy affordability legislative package originally proposed by Gov. Maura Healey last May. The Department of Public Utilities already reduced the program’s proposed budget by $500 million last year. Barrett’s support for the program – and rebuke of the House push to cut what leaders in that chamber say has become a bloated initiative at great cost to ratepayers – signals an early flash point in what will eventually become a negotiation between the two chambers.”

title: Unfounded health claims are powering a solar backlash
snippet: “About six years ago, [Kevin Heath] agreed to lease part of his land for a solar project. It would help him pay off debt and keep the farm in the family, he said. But the opportunity was thwarted when, in 2023, following pushback from some local residents, his township passed an ordinance that banned large solar projects from land zoned for agriculture… Restrictions on solar development are proliferating nationwide, ‘often rooted in misinformation or unfounded fears,’ including ones that involve ‘potential environmental and human safety risks,’ according to an article published late last year in the Brigham Young University Law Review… It all takes a toll on the effort to build out solar infrastructure. Last year, new solar installations in the U.S. dropped by 14%.”

title: Solar moratorium debated [in Oklahoma]
snippet: “While renewable energy development in Oklahoma remains a constant issue in some communities where solar and wind farms are proposed, it remains as well in the state legislature. A similar story is being played out in Missouri where the legislative session is about to end while there is continued debate on a proposal to suspend solar energy development across the state. The bill before the legislature, SB849, would create a moratorium on all solar projects until the end of 2027.”

title: Small modular reactors and microreactors under development in the United States
snippet: “Electric utilities in the United States currently operate about 98 gigawatts (GW) of nuclear generating capacity, but very little nuclear capacity has been built in the last few decades. High capital costs and lengthy licensing and approval processes have limited the expansion of nuclear power. However, several companies are developing new small modular reactor (SMR) designs aimed at reducing capital costs and increasing siting flexibility, challenges associated with traditional nuclear power.”

title: Trump’s Shady Wind Deals Aren’t Over Yet
snippet: “The Trump administration inked two more agreements to cancel offshore wind leases and reimburse the former leaseholders nearly $1 billion on Monday, demonstrating that its previous deals with TotalEnergies was not a one-off legal settlement but rather a new, repeatable strategy to throttle the industry… This latest deal will cancel leases for two projects, known as Bluepoint Wind and Golden State Wind. Bluepoint, a project off the coast of New York and New Jersey, was a joint venture between Global Infrastructure Partners, an investment firm owned by asset manager BlackRock, and Ocean Winds, which itself is a joint venture between the French energy company Engie and the developer EDP Renewables. The companies initially paid $765 million to acquire the lease.”

snippet: “These include a material assistance limit on equipment provided by prohibited foreign entities, a ban on U.S. taxpayers with excessive Chinese equity or debt participation, and a prohibition on projects that enter into contracts giving majority Chinese-owned entities effective control over project aspects. The rules target projects placed in service in 2025 or later. Martin clarified that legacy tax credits for projects under construction by the end of 2024 are generally exempt. However, for those that fall within the crosshairs, the stakes are existential. title: Court curtails Trump administration moves to stifle wind, solar development
snippet: “In a blow to the Trump administration’s efforts to stifle renewable energy development, the Department of Interior and the Army Corps of Engineers must end a set of policies that limit solar and wind energy permitting on federal land while litigation over the issue plays out, a federal judge ruled Tuesday. RENEW Northeast, the Southern Renewable Energy Association, Interwest Energy Alliance and other clean energy groups showed that their members face imminent and irreparable harm by a set of five policy actions taken by the Trump administration, Denise Casper, a judge with the U.S. District Court for the District of Massachusetts, said in granting their request for a preliminary injunction.”

title: No evidence of PFAS leaching from solar panels, study finds
snippet: “In a perspective article, researchers from Michigan State University and Oak Ridge National Laboratory examined claims about the presence and potential leaching of Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) from solar panels…According to the researchers, PFAS or fluoropolymers may theoretically be present in a few specific parts of solar panels. They can appear in front of glass coatings, though there is no evidence of commercial use; in the backsheets, where they might be used for weather protection; and in wires and cables, as insulation materials. Encapsulants and sealants, the scientists highlighted, typically do not contain PFAS.”

title: The solar support gap
snippet: Why do pro-solar citizens reject local projects? “Land use concerns outweigh proximity worries when it comes to local public support for new solar developments, new research suggests. A research team at the University of Rhode Island used the example of a 2019 municipal referendum on a utility-scale solar proposal in the town of North Kingstown, Rhode Island, to understand why citizens with pro-solar attitudes voted against the proposal… ‘Siting became highly contentious because most proposed and developed solar arrays were on forest and farm land, which resulted in a loss of ecosystem services and rural character,’ the paper adds. ‘In many ways, North Kingstown can be seen as a microcosm of a larger debate because siting patterns and land use conflicts are similar elsewhere in the United States and around the world.’”

title: Big grid batteries are finally on a roll in New England
snippet: “Enormous new batteries keep appearing on the grid, making it devilishly tricky to keep track of which is the biggest in a given region. That’s certainly the case in New England, where acute power needs and robust state climate goals are fueling a buildout of big batteries that keep breaking capacity records. Canary Media recently covered the inauguration of the 175-megawatt Cross Town battery in Gorham, Maine, which was the largest in New England when it began operating in late November. But that trophy has already passed to a 250-megawatt facility in Medway, Massachusetts, southwest of Boston and about 10 miles from the Patriots’ Gillette Stadium.”

title: Trump budget attacks renewables, boosts ‘energy dominance’
snippet: The White House released its fiscal 2027 budget request Friday morning, unveiling plans to continue waging its longstanding war against renewable energy and climate initiatives while boosting support for artificial intelligence and fossil fuels. The White House is asking Congress to fund EPA at $4.2 billion — a 52 percent cut relative to the currently enacted level. Trump is proposing to realign DOE away from certain priorities — namely energy efficiency and renewables — toward full-throated support of artificial intelligence. “The era of wasting taxpayer dollars on unreliable, expensive energy that is dependent on foreign supply chains is over,” the budget document states. DOE would get $53.9 billion in discretionary funding, an increase of more than $4 billion relative to the fiscal 2026 level, with a $1.2 billion commitment to AI. About $3.5 billion from the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act would be repurposed from renewable energy and climate priorities to DOE programs under the banner of “Bolstering Energy Dominance,” and the administration is proposing to end “taxpayer handouts” to EV battery manufacturers while canceling $4 billion in infrastructure law funding for “wasteful and ineffective” EV charging initiatives.

title: Federal permitting obstructs clean energy deployment, survey finds
snippet: Clean energy finance platform Crux surveyed 50 developers and permitting professionals in February who are specifically developing solar or wind projects across the country and have experience in the federal permitting process, finding that the process is “slowing clean energy projects,” said Hasan Nazar, the head of policy for Crux. “There’s less energy being deployed and higher costs being incurred at a moment where we need those electrons more than ever,” Nazar said. More than 80 percent of those surveyed said they intentionally site projects to avoid triggering federal permitting requirements. Federal permitting was cited as a contributing factor to project delays and cancellations by 94 percent of respondents, and every respondent reported increased development costs, with a majority citing a 6 to 10 percent increase in total project costs and some reporting increases exceeding 25 percent. When asked what change they would most like to see in the process, 72 percent of respondents chose more predictable outcomes over faster timelines or simpler processes. “The finding here is that developers can absorb difficulty, but they cannot build under uncertainty,” Nazar said. “Almost every clean energy project in America must now be designed to purposely minimize interacting with the federal government,” Sol Systems CEO Yuri Horwitz said.

title: Solar installations fell 22% in 2025
snippet: FERC: Solar developers installed 26.5 GW last year, down 22% from the 33.8 GW they installed in 2024, according to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission. Despite this decrease, solar still led all other generation sources in 2025 installations. As of December, solar makes up 12.2% of installed generating capacity in the U.S., trailing only natural gas at 42.2% and coal at 14.3%. In 2025, the U.S. solar industry “navigated unprecedented change, ranging from numerous trade actions to the reversal of renewable energy tax credit policy,” the Solar Energy Industries Association said in a March 9 report. “Many projects stayed on track, but the market and policy uncertainty took a toll, leading to project delays and cancellations across all segments.” The decline took place despite the ongoing rush to bring projects online to qualify for Inflation Reduction Act tax credits, which had their timelines curtailed by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Natural gas added fewer units in 2025 — 84, compared with 122 the previous year — despite a 1.5 GW increase in installed capacity. Wind capacity grew as developers added 5.7 GW in 2025 compared with 4.5 GW the previous year. No new nuclear capacity came online in 2025.
