title: States are already working on solutions to large-load challenges
snippet: In an opinion piece, former FERC Chairman Mark Christie — now on the faculty at William & Mary Law School — argued that states are already working on solutions to the challenges posed by large-load customers and that FERC should not preempt them. “With all the publicity surrounding the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s proposed regulation that would preempt the states and insert FERC into retail customer interconnection for the first time, it is important to recognize that states are already working on solutions to the challenges posed by large-load customers,” Christie wrote. In October, the Department of Energy directed FERC to consider rules for interconnecting data centers and other large loads to the transmission system. Christie argued that states “are focused on solutions that protect reliability and avoid cost-shifting to residential consumers, consistent with the White House Ratepayer Protection Pledge,” with many states having already enacted large-load tariffs and “take or pay” provisions. “FERC has never even tried to regulate retail load interconnections and should leave it to the states, who have done it for decades, know what they are doing from experience and are perfectly capable of meeting these challenges,” Christie wrote, warning that “absurdly short interconnection deadlines for large-load customers whose individual loads exceed that of a medium-sized city … could be disastrous for both reliability and cost.”

title: Energy storage pricing beginning to ‘fracture’ by product type
snippet: report: Pricing for U.S. utility-scale and distribution-scale energy storage systems diverged in the first quarter of 2026, Anza Renewables said in its latest quarterly market report. Utility-scale system prices have fallen by as much as 8.6% since November and 20.9% since May, while distribution-scale system prices are largely unchanged since November but remain as much as 14% lower than in May. The divergence appears driven by suppliers focused on supporting larger projects for data center and independent power producer clients while treating the distribution-scale segment — at least for now — as an “afterthought,” Anza said. The United States installed a record amount of energy storage in 2025 — 18.9 GW/51 GWh across all customer segments — with utility-scale installations accounting for 16 GW/47.3 GWh, up 48% and 40% over 2024, according to a Wood Mackenzie report. Anza sees energy storage prices stabilizing and potentially trending upward in the coming months due to rising lithium carbonate prices and an April 1 reduction in value-added tax rebates in China. Of 79 energy storage products in Anza’s dataset, 40% are at “low” risk of violating foreign entity of concern rules, while 51% carry “high” or “highest” risk.

title: Trump’s offshore wind opposition was never really about the whales
snippet: The Trump administration has often evoked the plight of whales in its efforts to undermine U.S. offshore wind development — despite there being no evidence that wind-farm activities are harming the giant mammals — but those purported concerns didn’t stop federal officials from voting unanimously last week to override Endangered Species Act protections for imperiled whales in order to unleash oil and gas drilling in the Gulf. On March 31, a committee of Trump-appointed officials voted to exempt oil and gas drilling in the Gulf from Endangered Species Act protections. “I think it’s pretty clear they don’t care about marine species,” Michael Jasny, director of marine mammals for the Natural Resources Defense Council, said of the Trump administration. Expanding offshore oil and gas development will likely only exacerbate the threats facing endangered whales, manatees, and five species of sea turtles in the Gulf, with collisions with oil industry vessels in particular that could “jeopardize the continued existence” of the endangered Rice’s whale, of which only 51 are estimated to remain. “The windmills are driving the whales crazy,” Trump said in January 2025, weeks before signing an executive order pausing federal permitting and new leasing for offshore wind farms. The Interior Department has since tried — so far unsuccessfully — to halt construction of five in-progress offshore wind projects totaling nearly 6 gigawatts of capacity.

snippet: “Earlier this week, the Interior Department said it would reimburse the French oil giant for nearly $1 billion in lease fees the company paid in 2022 for areas off the coasts of New York and North Carolina. In exchange, TotalEnergies pledged not to develop any U.S. offshore wind projects and to invest in fossil-gas infrastructure instead…However, it is far from clear whether the department can legally carry out its agreement with TotalEnergies…To reimburse TotalEnergies, Interior will most likely tap into the Justice Department’s Judgment Fund, which uses taxpayer dollars to settle lawsuits and claims against federal agencies…The states of New York and North Carolina may be able to challenge the agreement, given that they stood to gain gigawatts’ worth of clean electricity if TotalEnergies had pursued its offshore wind project…”

title: For offshore wind, two steps forward and one step back
snippet: “Two offshore wind projects notched major victories late last week — but that doesn’t mean Trump’s crusade against the industry is over…Both Vineyard Wind and Revolution Wind were targeted by the Trump administration’s December stop-work orders, which judges later struck down. But while the federal government has declined to appeal the Revolution ruling, it is seemingly looking for new avenues to block future offshore wind projects. The New York Times reported this week that senior Trump officials are drafting settlement agreements that would pay TotalEnergies nearly $1 billion to cancel its two offshore wind leases in federal waters, and commit the company to building gas infrastructure instead.”

title: States are lifting bans on nuclear power
snippet: “Five states — Wisconsin, Kentucky, Montana, West Virginia, and, most recently, Illinois — have fully lifted their moratoria since 2016. Others are loosening the reins, with Connecticut easing restrictions on small modular reactors and Rhode Island allowing utilities to buy electricity from neighboring states’ nuclear plants. Five more — California, Massachusetts, Minnesota, New Jersey, and Vermont — are now weighing legislation to overturn their bans…The changing sentiment is a necessary but not sufficient precondition for more nuclear plants to start construction in the U.S. Big questions remain about how to finance projects, train workers, and establish supply chains for novel kinds of reactors.”

The public is no longer buying it…The scale of BESS pushback is significant: At least 96 battery projects have faced public pushback since 2021…Non-flammable, non-toxic battery cell chemistries eliminate the root cause of concern…Chemistries like advanced sodium-ion that can demonstrate higher safety performance eliminate the need for complex suppression infrastructure…For the last three decades, lithium-ion’s advantages kept it in the limelight…The industry accepted trade-offs because the market rewarded high-power, short-duration technologies, and no viable alternatives could compete on cost and energy density simultaneously…With stable chemistries available, the industry no longer needs to choose between performance and risk. The technology has arrived. Now the industry must align to deploy it.”

title: GM, LG joint venture recalls 700 workers to make non-EV batteries
snippet: “ Late last year, GM laid off thousands of workers across its Southern battery ventures as well as its all-electric Factory Zero plant in Detroit-Hamtramck…’In response to slower near-term EV adoption and an evolving regulatory environment, General Motors is realigning EV capacity,’ GM spokesman Kevin Kelly said on Oct. 29…Instead, the plant’s new battery technology is aimed toward powering data centers and grid-scale initiatives, GM confirmed…The 700 employees furloughed in January are in the process of returning to work to support the launch of the new product line.”

title: This $400B Biden climate program is surviving the Trump administration
snippet: In January, the Trump administration announced that it had completed its dismantling of yet another Biden-era climate program. This time, the target was the Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office, which Democrats had injected with almost $400 billion to support ambitious new clean energy projects…Thanks to a change in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act…the program can now directly support fossil generation such as natural gas. This federal loan support was illegal under the Biden administration, when projects had to reduce greenhouse gases. But it’s far from clear that [Trump’s secretary of energy, Chris] Wright and [The new leader of the loan office is Greg] Beard could succeed in repurposing the loan program for pure fossil fuel finance, if that’s their goal…A more likely outcome is that the revamped office will continue to support a handful of deals for “clean firm” energy projects that Trump and Wright find appealing, like nuclear, as well as critical minerals production.”

title: Two dozen states, 10 cities sue EPA over repeal of ‘endangerment’ finding central to climate fight
snippet: “Two dozen states, along with more than a dozen cities and counties, sued the Environmental Protection Agency on Thursday, challenging the Trump administration’s repeal of a scientific finding that had been the central basis for U.S. action to regulate greenhouse gas emissions and fight climate change…The repeal eliminates all greenhouse gas emissions standards for cars and trucks and could unleash a broader undoing of climate regulations on stationary sources such as power plants and oil and gas facilities.”

title: Solar and wind hit a record 17% of U.S. generation in 2025
snippet: EIA: “Electricity from utility-scale wind and solar is making up a steadily increasing share of the U.S. generation mix and rose to a cumulative 17% last year, according to a Friday report from the Energy Information Administration…EIA expects capacity additions in the U.S. to hit a record high in 2026, with solar assets making up 51% of those additions, according to a Feb. 20 report. Battery storage is expected to make up 28% of new capacity, with 24.3 GW of additions, and wind 14%, with 11.8 GW of additions.”

title: Study finds solar sheep grazing business models outperform agricultural benchmarks
snippet: “Researchers at Western University have released a comprehensive financial study indicating that the co-location of sheep grazing and solar PV arrays, a practice known as agrivoltaics, offers a robust solution to the tightening profit margins of modern agriculture…A leading driver of favorable economics is the dual-revenue nature of the business. In addition to livestock sales, farmers receive vegetation management fees from solar asset owners. The fees, which the study identifies as averaging between $212 and $374 per acre, provide a “revenue floor” that traditional farmers rarely enjoy…The study concludes that as land-use conflicts between energy developers and agricultural communities intensify, agrivoltaics provides a pragmatic path forward.”

title: Clean energy trends and big picture news Which states have the most grid batteries?
snippet: “California and Texas are far ahead of the pack when it comes to grid batteries. But another state is seeing storage expand quickly as it looks to store more of its abundant, cheap solar power for later. Arizona saw blistering growth in utility-scale battery capacity last year, more than doubling its fleet to a total of 4.7 gigawatts at the end of 2025, according to U.S. Energy Information Administration data analyzed by research firm Cleanview. The two leading states each installed far more capacity last year than Arizona did, but neither of these more mature markets grew as quickly. California expanded its fleet by 29%, to 15.2 GW, while Texas’ grew by 69%, pushing it to just over 14 GW of total installed capacity.”

raw_clip_text: Clean energy trends and big picture news Which states have the most grid batteries? “California and Texas are far ahead of the pack when it comes to grid batteries. But another state is seeing storage expand quickly as it looks to store more of its abundant, cheap solar power for later. Arizona saw blistering growth in utility-scale battery capacity last year, more than doubling its fleet to a total of 4.7 gigawatts at the end of 2025, according to U.S. Energy Information Administration data analyzed by research firm Cleanview. The two leading states each installed far more capacity last year than Arizona did, but neither of these more mature markets grew as quickly. California expanded its fleet by 29%, to 15.2 GW, while Texas’ grew by 69%, pushing it to just over 14 GW of total installed capacity.” [Canary Media, 2/12/26, link to article ] Why Texas Is Outpacing New York in Renewable Energy Development “Over the last two years, Texas added more wind and solar power than New York has in the last two decades, a gap that has increased even as the mandates required by the state's 2019 Climate Act are looming. Those mandates, which Gov. Kathy Hochul wants to delay, include having an energy system relying entirely on clean energy by 2040. But nearly seven years after they were established, the state isn't on track to hit that mandate or its near-term target of converting the grid to 70% renewable energy by 2030. Texas never set ambitious clean energy goals, but is building faster than New York, in part because of deregulation, limited local opposition and investments into the infrastructure that carries power from rural parts of the state to urban cores. Other states leading in renewable energy development, including Kansas, Oklahoma and Florida, have no state clean energy mandates.” [Governing, 3/11/26, link to article ] Utilities are spending billions on the data center boom. What are the risks? “The data centers being planned and built across the U.S. need a massive amount of electricity, and utilities are racing to build new generation and grid infrastructure to meet unprecedented demand growth — driven in significant part by these large load customers — while coming up with contract structures that protect their other customers. But several factors are working against utilities as they seek to manage risk, experts and industry sources say, potentially leaving them and their ratepayers or members vulnerable to stranded investments.” [Utility Dive, 3/6/26, link to article ] Interior bends on solar, but wind energy on public lands remains stalled “The Interior Department’s pivot to again consider some solar projects on federal lands hasn’t revived wind energy. A small wind farm partially on federal land in Wyoming that won approval from Interior in the final days of the Biden administration has sat in limbo for more than a year. The Two Rivers Wind Project needs a final sign-off from the Bureau of Land Management before it can start construction, said a local official following the development of wind energy in the region. It’s the only wind farm that had been in the BLM pipeline during the Biden administration that appears to still be viable at the agency under President Donald Trump, who regularly denounces wind energy as a scam.” [E&E News, 3/17/26, link to article ] Good news corner SEIA: Solar energy added most capacity to the grid in 2025 “The U.S. solar industry installed 43 gigawatts (GW) of new capacity in 2025, making it the dominant source of new capacity added to the grid for the fifth straight year. Overall, solar and energy storage represent 79 percent of new capacity installed last year, according to the U.S. Solar Market Insight 2025 Year in Review report from the Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA) and Wood Mackenzie. The report found 11 states set new annual installation records in 2025, and 12 states added over 1 GW of new solar capacity. Texas led all states with 11 GW of new installations. Deployment in Indiana and Utah has skyrocketed, with Indiana deploying nearly 3 GW, up from 1.6 GW in 2024. More than two-thirds of all solar capacity installed in 2025 was built in Texas, Indiana, Florida, Arizona, Ohio, Utah, and Arkansas, all of which are in the top 10 in solar capacity.” [Daily Energy Insider, 3/12/26, link to article ] How a tiny Texas town is using wind energy to help out senior citizens “A wonky tax deal with wind companies means more food and medical resources for older residents of ​’The Biggest Little Town in the World.’” [Canary Media, 3/17/26, link to article ]

title: Why Texas Is Outpacing New York in Renewable Energy Development
snippet: “Over the last two years, Texas added more wind and solar power than New York has in the last two decades, a gap that has increased even as the mandates required by the state's 2019 Climate Act are looming. Those mandates, which Gov. Kathy Hochul wants to delay, include having an energy system relying entirely on clean energy by 2040. But nearly seven years after they were established, the state isn't on track to hit that mandate or its near-term target of converting the grid to 70% renewable energy by 2030. Texas never set ambitious clean energy goals, but is building faster than New York, in part because of deregulation, limited local opposition and investments into the infrastructure that carries power from rural parts of the state to urban cores. Other states leading in renewable energy development, including Kansas, Oklahoma and Florida, have no state clean energy mandates.”

title: Interior bends on solar, but wind energy on public lands remains stalled
snippet: “The Interior Department’s pivot to again consider some solar projects on federal lands hasn’t revived wind energy. A small wind farm partially on federal land in Wyoming that won approval from Interior in the final days of the Biden administration has sat in limbo for more than a year. The Two Rivers Wind Project needs a final sign-off from the Bureau of Land Management before it can start construction, said a local official following the development of wind energy in the region. It’s the only wind farm that had been in the BLM pipeline during the Biden administration that appears to still be viable at the agency under President Donald Trump, who regularly denounces wind energy as a scam.”

snippet: A pv magazine feature by Phoebe Skok examines how sweeping import tariffs and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act reshaped the U.S. energy storage industry in 2025 and what developers are navigating heading into 2026. Trump's policy changes have made domestic manufacturing a necessity rather than a preference, forcing developers to absorb new political and financial risks. "We are no longer in an era of cheap imported batteries," said Zora Chung, CFO at battery optimization platform ReJoule, noting that heavy tariff implementation and tightening tax credit eligibility have reversed the long trend of falling foreign cell prices. "The cost of having a compliant battery system is significantly higher than before." Jon M. Williams, CEO of lithium-ion battery manufacturer Viridi Parente, said higher costs and tighter margins are pushing developers to weigh total lifecycle value over upfront capital cost, with permitting delays and insurance premiums hitting harder as a result. Eligibility for the 48E Investment Tax Credit — worth a 30% ITC — now hinges on strict limits for foreign-supplied components, generating significant demand for supply chain traceability.

[PV Magazine, Mar 11 2026, link to article ] 150+ Local Governments Have Moved to Block Battery Storage Development, New Database Reveals: At least 150 local governments across 17 states have enacted moratoriums, bans, or restrictive ordinances targeting battery energy storage systems, according to the first publicly available nationwide database of such restrictions, compiled by Delaware-based developer Carina Energy. New York leads the country with 98 moratoriums — 65% of the national total — concentrated in the Hudson Valley, Capital Region, and Long Island. States including Indiana, Michigan, Iowa, and Maine are seeing a rising wave of new restrictions as utility-scale battery projects expand into new markets. The firm’s BESS Moratorium Monitor tracks 17 states through weekly review of municipal agendas and ordinances and also tracks seven states that have enacted legal pathways allowing developers to bypass local moratoriums under certain conditions. “The moratorium problem is growing faster than the industry realizes,” said Malaquias Encarnacion, Managing Director of Carina Energy. “Finding 150 tells you that local opposition to battery storage is becoming systemic. This isn’t a handful of outliers anymore — it’s a new regulatory reality.” [GlobeNewswire / Carina Energy, Mar 10 2026, link to article ]

title: How Will the War in Iran Affect Your Utility Bills?
snippet: Energy experts fear the Trump administration’s decision to attack Iran could set off a utility bill spiral similar to the one triggered by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Qatar has halted production at the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility after Iranian drone strikes, and shipments through the Strait of Hormuz — through which 15% of the global oil supply and 20% of global LNG normally flow — have been cut off. The disruption is “wreaking havoc with global gas and LNG markets, even more so than oil,” according to Wood Mackenzie analysts. The situation compounds pre-existing rate pressure: U.S. electricity rates rose 5% nationwide in 2025, and in some states like Pennsylvania, nearly 9%. The article traces how growing LNG exports have tethered domestic gas prices to global market swings: “It’s U.S. consumers who are bidding against global consumers for the same gas,” said Clark Williams-Derry of the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis. LNG export facilities, by contrast, stand to profit. “It’s a gold rush,” Williams-Derry said.

title: Why Electricity Bills Are So High—and How the Blowback Could Hit Trump
snippet: A detailed Inside Climate News investigation finds that U.S. residential electricity rates rose an average of 5% in 2025, with consumers increasingly frustrated and politicians across the spectrum taking notice. The single most significant driver, found in every state, is utilities’ surge in infrastructure spending — long-lived equipment investment roughly doubled from $108.6 billion in 2015 to a forecasted $207.9 billion in 2025, with the largest one-year jump between 2024 and 2025. State-level renewable energy mandates play a modest role in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic but are barely detectable elsewhere. Data centers absorb a disproportionate share of public blame, but researchers say the evidence is more complicated and varies substantially by state and utility. The deeper structural problem is utility companies’ financial incentive to maximize capital spending, since regulated returns are tied to infrastructure investment.

title: Solar Power Surging in Michigan, Midwest as Renewables Rapidly Gain Ground
snippet: A new report from the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis finds that despite Trump administration efforts to boost coal and natural gas, renewables are still gaining market share at a “blistering” pace across the Midwest Independent System Operator region — and Michigan in particular is poised for major growth. Solar provided barely 1% of MISO’s power as recently as 2023 but had grown to 4.5% by 2025, driven largely by improvements in battery storage technology that allow utilities to dispatch renewable power on demand. Report coauthor Dennis Wamsted said solar’s trajectory in MISO mirrors that of Texas a decade ago. Michigan has more than 3,600 MW of new solar in the pipeline scheduled to come online by 2028 — the largest of any state in MISO. “It’s like a secret sauce,” Wamsted said of solar paired with storage. “You put them together and you have a competitor for any kind of coal or natural gas plant, because you now have dispatchable renewable power.”

title: Clean Energy Trends and Big Picture News Trump EPA repeals Endangerment Finding, stripping legal bedrock for solar and clean energy
snippet: “In a move described as the “single largest deregulatory action in U.S. history,” the EPA has rescinded the 2009 finding that greenhouse gases threaten public health, a decision that could dismantle the legal justification for federal solar incentives and emissions standards.”
raw_clip_text: Clean Energy Trends and Big Picture News Trump EPA repeals Endangerment Finding, stripping legal bedrock for solar and clean energy: “In a move described as the “single largest deregulatory action in U.S. history,” the EPA has rescinded the 2009 finding that greenhouse gases threaten public health, a decision that could dismantle the legal justification for federal solar incentives and emissions standards.” [PV Magazine, 2/12/26, link to article ] Navigating the OBBBA cliff for solar tax credits: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (passed July 2025) moved up deadlines for utility-scale solar to keep full IRA tax credits — construction must begin by July 4, 2026 and be placed in service by Dec. 31, 2027 — while replacing the 5% Cost Safe Harbor with a stricter Physical Work Test for projects over 1.5 MW. “At a time when we need energy abundance, these rules create new federal red tape,” said Heather O’Neill, president and CEO of Advanced Energy United. Developers now face heavier documentation requirements including geotagged photos and unredacted contracts. [pv magazine USA, Feb 18 2026, link to article ] Fear of farmland loss slows solar and wind development: Rural opposition over loss of agricultural land is slowing both solar and wind projects across the country, even though agrivoltaics can give farmers extra income (one Texas rancher now runs 3,000 sheep under panels on 8,000 acres). Experts point out that urban sprawl actually threatens far more farmland than renewables ever will, but Trump administration cuts to USDA funding and REAP grants have stalled progress. [Canary Media, Feb 18 2026, link to article ] States push climate superfund bills despite Trump’s opposition: Maine, New Jersey, Illinois, Oregon, Rhode Island and Connecticut are advancing “climate superfund” legislation that would force oil and gas companies to pay for climate damages. “Climate superfund laws are based on the principle that if you make a mess, you clean it up,” said Jamie Flynn of the Conservation Law Foundation. Trump has called the bills “extortion” and issued an executive order opposing them. [Canary Media, Feb 17 2026, link to article ] The EPA’s biggest emissions-fighting tool is dead — at least for now: The Trump administration formally revoked the 2009 EPA endangerment finding that greenhouse gases endanger public health and welfare — the legal foundation for nearly all federal climate rules since 2007. “Most people have never heard of this safeguard — the ‘endangerment’ finding — but repealing it sends a clear message: this government doesn’t care,” said David Widawsky of the World Resources Institute. Court battles are expected. [Canary Media, Feb 13 2026, link to article ] Southern California air board rejected pollution rules after AI-generated flood of comments: An AI-powered campaign using the platform CiviClick generated more than 20,000 form emails that helped kill a South Coast AQMD proposal to phase out gas furnaces and water heaters. The rules would have cut nitrogen oxide emissions and prevented nearly 2,500 premature deaths and over 10,000 asthma cases. Experts called it “the next step in digital astroturfing.” [Los Angeles Times, Feb 17 2026, link to article ] Good News Corner How battery deployment in the Eastern U.S. could scale as in Texas and California: Texas has already deployed nearly ten times more grid-scale battery storage than all four Eastern grid regions combined, proving batteries can handle rising peak demand. A new GridLab study outlines 15 concrete policy fixes to unlock the 140 GW of battery projects already sitting in Eastern interconnection queues. “Texas and California… have proved that grid-scale battery storage can effectively tackle rising peak demand,” said GridLab Executive Director Ric O’Connell. [pv magazine USA, Feb 18 2026, link to article ] With AI demands, can renewable energy be the economic future of rural Kansas?: Surging electricity needs from AI data centers (projected 25% jump in U.S. demand by 2030) are opening the door for wind and solar to bring tax revenue, construction jobs, permanent maintenance roles and landowner lease payments to declining rural counties. Kansas already gets nearly half its electricity from renewables. “We have excellent wind and we have excellent solar. More days of sunshine than San Diego, California,” said Lona DuVall, Finney County Economic Development director. [KCUR, Feb 17 2026, link to article ]

title: How battery deployment in the Eastern U.S. could scale as in Texas and California
snippet: Texas has already deployed nearly ten times more grid-scale battery storage than all four Eastern grid regions combined, proving batteries can handle rising peak demand. A new GridLab study outlines 15 concrete policy fixes to unlock the 140 GW of battery projects already sitting in Eastern interconnection queues. “Texas and California… have proved that grid-scale battery storage can effectively tackle rising peak demand,” said GridLab Executive Director Ric O’Connell.

title: With AI demands, can renewable energy be the economic future of rural Kansas?
snippet: Surging electricity needs from AI data centers (projected 25% jump in U.S. demand by 2030) are opening the door for wind and solar to bring tax revenue, construction jobs, permanent maintenance roles and landowner lease payments to declining rural counties. Kansas already gets nearly half its electricity from renewables. “We have excellent wind and we have excellent solar. More days of sunshine than San Diego, California,” said Lona DuVall, Finney County Economic Development director.
